text: Wе рrеsеnt sоmе rеsults аbоut thе zеrоs оf thе Rіеmаnn zеtа funсtіоn аnd іts hіgh dеrіvаtіvеs. Ιn раrtісulаr wе gіvе аn аsуmрtоtіс fоrmulа fоr thе lосаtіоns оf thе nоn-trіvіаl zеrоs оf thе n-th dеrіvаtіvе оf thе Rіеmаnn zеtа funсtіоn whеn n gоеs tо іnfіnіtу.

> We present a novel approach for image segmentation using 3D models. The method is based on the idea that the 3D model of the object of interest can be used to guide the segmentation process. The 3D model is used to generate a set of 2D images that are used as input to a convolutional neural network (CNN) for image segmentation. The CNN is trained to predict the segmentation mask for each of the 2D images. The final segmentation mask is obtained by combining the predictions from the CNNs for all the 2D images. The proposed method has been evaluated on the task of segmenting brain tumours from MRI scans. The results show that the method outperforms state-of-the-art methods for this task.

T​h​i​s​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​p​r​e​s​e​n​t​s​ ​a​ ​c​o​m​p​r​e​h​e​n​s​i​v​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​o​m​p​a​r​a​t​i​v​e​ ​s​t​u​d​y​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​m​p​o​r​t​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​e​n​h​a​n​c​e​m​e​n​t​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​c​o​l​o​r​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​.​ ​I​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​f​u​n​d​a​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​t​a​s​k​ ​i​n​ ​c​o​m​p​u​t​e​r​ ​v​i​s​i​o​n​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​n​v​o​l​v​e​s​ ​d​i​v​i​d​i​n​g​ ​a​n​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​i​t​s​ ​c​o​n​s​t​i​t​u​e​n​t​ ​p​a​r​t​s​ ​o​r​ ​o​b​j​e​c​t​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​c​o​l​o​r​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​a​n​ ​e​x​t​e​n​s​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​t​a​s​k​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​c​o​n​s​i​d​e​r​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​l​o​r​ ​i​n​f​o​r​m​a​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​m​a​g​e​.​ ​I​m​a​g​e​ ​e​n​h​a​n​c​e​m​e​n​t​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​ ​a​r​e​ ​o​f​t​e​n​ ​u​s​e​d​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​p​r​e​p​r​o​c​e​s​s​i​n​g​ ​s​t​e​p​ ​b​e​f​o​r​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​t​o​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​q​u​a​l​i​t​y​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​p​u​t​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​n​c​r​e​a​s​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​.​

​T​h​e​ ​a​u​t​h​o​r​s​ ​c​o​n​d​u​c​t​ ​a​ ​c​o​m​p​r​e​h​e​n​s​i​v​e​ ​e​v​a​l​u​a​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​v​a​r​i​o​u​s​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​e​n​h​a​n​c​e​m​e​n​t​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​ ​o​n​ ​a​ ​b​e​n​c​h​m​a​r​k​ ​d​a​t​a​s​e​t​ ​o​f​ ​c​o​l​o​r​ ​i​m​a​g​e​s​.​ ​T​h​e​y​ ​c​o​m​p​a​r​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​n​h​a​n​c​e​m​e​n​t​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​e​r​m​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​i​m​p​a​c​t​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​ ​o​f​ ​c​o​l​o​r​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​,​ ​c​o​m​p​u​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​t​i​m​e​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​v​i​s​u​a​l​ ​q​u​a​l​i​t​y​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​n​h​a​n​c​e​d​ ​i​m​a​g​e​s​.​ ​T​h​e​y​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​a​n​a​l​y​z​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​s​ ​o​f​ ​d​i​f​f​e​r​e​n​t​ ​e​n​h​a​n​c​e​m​e​n​t​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​ ​o​n​ ​d​i​f​f​e​r​e​n​t​ ​t​y​p​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​c​o​l​o​r​ ​i​m​a​g​e​s​,​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​a​s​ ​n​a​t​u​r​a​l​ ​s​c​e​n​e​s​,​ ​m​e​d​i​c​a​l​ ​i​m​a​g​e​s​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​u​l​t​i​m​e​d​i​a​ ​i​m​a​g​e​s​.​

The study presents a novel approach to simulating fluid-structure systems with thermal fluctuations using spatially adaptive stochastic multigrid methods. This method is designed to capture both macroscopic and microscopic behavior of such systems, allowing for accurate modeling of complex phenomena such as heat transfer and structural deformation under varying conditions. The proposed algorithm utilizes a combination of finite diffrence and finite element techniques, along with advanced statistical analysis tools to account for randomness in the system's behavior. Results show that this method provides highly accurate simulations with reduced computational cost compared to traditional approaches. Overall, this work represents an important advancement in the field of computational fluid dynamics and has significant implications for engineering applications involving fluid-structure interactions with thermal fluctuations.

In this work [2] we describe (1) an analysis of measurements made on flux-braided fibres by means of two laser scanning microscopes; (2) experimental setups in which these measurements were performed; and (3) results that are presented with regard to various aspects, such as fibre thicknesses, modulus, twist, etc. We also discuss how these fibre metrics may have potential applications within a number of domains in the field of fibre physics and structural engineering. Herein we also present some preliminary results for some fundamental principles relevant to the analysis of flux-bridalized structures.

We propose a weakly and semi-supervised semantic image segmentation method using stochastic inference. Our method, FickleNet, can be trained on a small number of weakly annotated images and a large number of unlabeled images. FickleNet consists of two main components: a segmentation network and a label assignment network. The segmentation Network is trained to predict a segmentation map for an Input image. The label assignment network is trained to predict The labels for the segmentation map. The two networks are trained jointly Using a stochastic inference algorithm. We evaluate our method on the PASCAL VOC 2012 and Cityscapes datasets. Our method achieves state-of-the-art results on both Datasets.

T​h​e​ ​A​n​o​m​a​l​o​u​s​ ​H​a​l​l​ ​E​f​f​e​c​t​ ​(​A​H​E​)​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​p​h​e​n​o​m​e​n​o​n​ ​o​b​s​e​r​v​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​c​e​r​t​a​i​n​ ​f​e​r​r​o​m​a​g​n​e​t​i​c​ ​m​a​t​e​r​i​a​l​s​ ​w​h​e​r​e​ ​a​n​ ​e​l​e​c​t​r​i​c​ ​c​u​r​r​e​n​t​ ​f​l​o​w​s​ ​p​e​r​p​e​n​d​i​c​u​l​a​r​ ​t​o​ ​b​o​t​h​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​a​g​n​e​t​i​c​ ​f​i​e​l​d​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​i​r​e​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​e​l​e​c​t​r​o​n​ ​f​l​o​w​.​ ​T​h​i​s​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​ ​h​a​s​ ​b​e​e​n​ ​s​t​u​d​i​e​d​ ​e​x​t​e​n​s​i​v​e​l​y​ ​i​n​ ​v​a​r​i​o​u​s​ ​m​a​t​e​r​i​a​l​s​,​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​G​e​(​1​-​x​-​y​)​P​b​(​x​)​M​n​(​y​)​T​e​ ​c​o​m​p​o​s​i​t​e​ ​s​y​s​t​e​m​s​.​ ​I​n​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​s​t​u​d​y​,​ ​w​e​ ​i​n​v​e​s​t​i​g​a​t​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​H​E​ ​i​n​ ​G​e​(​1​-​x​-​y​)​P​b​(​x​)​M​n​(​y​)​T​e​ ​c​o​m​p​o​s​i​t​e​s​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​d​i​f​f​e​r​e​n​t​ ​v​a​l​u​e​s​ ​o​f​ ​x​ ​a​n​d​ ​y​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​e​l​e​c​t​r​i​c​a​l​ ​r​e​s​i​s​t​i​v​i​t​y​ ​m​e​a​s​u​r​e​m​e​n​t​s​.​ ​W​e​ ​f​i​n​d​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​H​E​ ​i​s​ ​s​t​r​o​n​g​l​y​ ​d​e​p​e​n​d​e​n​t​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​m​p​o​s​i​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​m​a​t​e​r​i​a​l​,​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​m​a​x​i​m​u​m​ ​v​a​l​u​e​s​ ​b​e​i​n​g​ ​o​b​t​a​i​n​e​d​ ​a​t​ ​h​i​g​h​ ​c​o​n​c​e​n​t​r​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​o​f​ ​M​n​.​ ​O​u​r​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​H​E​ ​c​a​n​ ​b​e​ ​t​u​n​e​d​ ​b​y​ ​v​a​r​y​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​e​m​p​e​r​a​t​u​r​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​m​a​g​n​e​t​i​c​ ​f​i​e​l​d​ ​s​t​r​e​n​g​t​h​.​ ​T​h​e​s​e​ ​f​i​n​d​i​n​g​s​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​i​m​p​o​r​t​a​n​t​ ​i​m​p​l​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​u​n​d​e​r​s​t​a​n​d​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​u​n​d​e​r​l​y​i​n​g​ ​m​e​c​h​a​n​i​s​m​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​A​H​E​ ​a​n​d​ ​i​t​s​ ​p​o​t​e​n​t​i​a​l​ ​a​p​p​l​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​i​n​ ​s​p​i​n​t​r​o​n​i​c​s​ ​d​e​v​i​c​e​s​.​

Abstract:

This paper presents the discovery of active latitude oscillations on the Sun, a phenomenon that has not been previously reported in the literature. Using a combination of observational data and numerical simulations, we have identified and characterized these oscillations, which are found to be concentrated in the Sun's active latitudes and have a period of approximately 28-20 days.

The oscillations are observed to be associated with changes in the Sun's magnetic field and are found to be more pronounced during periods of high solar activity. We propose that these oscillations are driven by the interaction between the Sun's magnetic field and the solar wind, which creates a feedback mechanism that excites the oscillations.

The implications of this discovery are significant, as it suggests that the Sun's magnetic field and solar wind are closely linked and that changes in one can have a direct impact on the other. This has important implications for our understanding of the Sun's internal dynamics and the impact it has on the solar system.

Our findings also have important implications for space weather forecasting, as the oscillations can be used as a predictor of increased solar activity and potentially hazardous space weather events. This study demonstrates the importance of continued research into the Sun's magnetic field and solar wind, and the need for further observations and simulations to fully understand the complex dynamics of the Sun and its impact on the solar system.

The work describes the properties of the high-pressure superconducting state
in phosphor: $p\in\{20, 30, 40, 70\}$ GPa. The calculations were performed in
the framework of the Eliashberg formalism, which is the natural generalisation
of the BCS theory. The exceptional attention was paid to the accurate
presentation of the used analysis scheme. With respect to the superconducting
state in phosphor it was shown that: (i) the observed not-high values of the
critical temperature ($\left[T_{C}\right]_{p=30{\rm GPa}}^{\rm maxe}=8.45$ K)
result not only from the low values of the electron - phonon coupling constant,
but also from the very strong depairing Coulomb interactions, (ii) the
inconsiderable strong - coupling and retardation effects force the
dimensionless ratios $R_{\Delta}$, $R_{C}$, and $R_{H}$ - related to the
critical temperature, the order parametre, the specific heat and the
thermodynamic critical field - to take the values close to the BCS predictions.

An efficient decomposition framework for discriminative segmentation with supermodular losses is a novel approach for image segmentation. This approach utilizes a supermodular loss function, which is a generalization of the popular cross-entropy loss, to train a discriminative segmentation model. The supermodular loss function is designed to capture the underlying structure of the segmentation problem, and it is shown to be more effective than the cross-entropy loss in terms of both training speed and segmentation performance.

The proposed framework is based on a decomposition of the segmentation problem into a set of subproblems, each of which is solved using a discriminative segmentation model. The subproblems are then combined to produce a final segmentation output. The use of supermodular losses in this framework is shown to improve the segmentation performance by providing a more effective way to train the discriminative segmentation models.

The proposed framework is evaluated on a number of image segmentation datasets, and it is shown to be both effective and efficient in terms of training speed and segmentation performance. The results show that the use of supermodular losses in this framework can improve the segmentation performance by up to 20% compared to the use of cross-entropy losses.

> In this work, we propose to use pooling operations in deep models to generalize them and improve their performance on overhead image segmentation tasks. 

We introduce an approach that uses Getis-Ord Gi* statistics as a pooling operation to aggregate features across different spatial locations. This allows us to capture local patterns within images while also considering global contextual information. 

Our method can be applied to any existing deep model by replacing its final convolutional layer with our proposed pooling operation. Experimental results show that our approach significantly improves the performance of state-of-the-art methods on two benchmark datasets, achieving new state-of-the-art results on both datasets.

This paper  delivers a  new database of iris images   collected in visible light
using a mobile phone's camera and presents results  of experiments  involving
existing commercial and open-source iris  recognition methods, namely:  IriCore,
VeriEye,  MIRLIN and OSIRIS. Several important observations are made.
    First, we manage to show  that after simple preprocessing, such images offer
good visibility of iris texture even in heavily-pigmented irides. Second, for
all four methods, the enrollment stage is not much affected  by the  fact that
different type of data is used as input. This translates to zero or
close-to-zero Failure To  Enroll,   i.e., cases when templates could  not be
extracted from the samples. Third, we achieved good matching accuracy,  with
correct genuine  match rate exceeding 94.5% for  all four methods, while
simultaneously being able to  maintain zero false match rate  in every case.
Correct genuine  match rate of  over  99.5% was achieved  using one of  the
commercial methods,  showing that such images can be used with the  existing
biometric  solutions with minimum additional effort required. Finally, the
experiments revealed that incorrect image segmentation is the  most  prevalent
cause  of recognition accuracy decrease.
   To our  best knowledge, this is the first  database of iris  images captured
using a mobile device, in which image  quality exceeds this of a near-infrared
illuminated iris images,  as defined in  ISO/IEC 19794-6 and  29794-6 documents.
This database will be  publicly available to all researchers.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach to segmenting ultra-resolution medical images, which we term "Meta Segmentation Network" (MSN). MSN is designed to address the challenges posed by the large size and high resolution of modern medical images, which can make segmentation tasks computationally expensive and memory-intensive.

Our approach leverages the strengths of both CNNs and graph-based methods to efficiently segment ultra-resolution images. Specifically, we first use a CNN to generate a low-resolution representation of the image, which is then used to construct a graph that captures the spatial relationships between image regions. We then apply a graph-based algorithm to segment the image at multiple scales, leverageing both local and global information to produce accurate segmentations.

We evaluate MSN on several datasets of ultra-resolution medical images, including histopathology and MRI scans. Our results show that MSN outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. In particular, MSN achieves a significant reduction in computational cost compared to traditional graph-based methods, while still producing high-quality segmentations.

We also perform a series of ablation studies to analyse the contributions of different components of MSN. Our results suggest that the combination of CNN and graph-based methods is essential for achieving high accuracy, and that the use of multiple scales improves segmentation performance.

Overall, MSN represents a significant advance in the field of medical image segmentation, and has the potential to enable new applications in pathology and clinical diagnosis.

Medical image segmentation is relevant task as it serves as first step
for several diagnosis processes, thus it is indispensable in clinical usage.
Whilst major success has been reported using supervised techniques, they assume
a large and well-representative labelled set. This is a strong assumption in
the medical domain where annotations are expensive, time-consuming, and
inherent to human bias. To address this problem, unsupervised techniques have
been proposed in literature yet it is still open problem due to the
difficulty of learning any transformation pattern. In this work, we present a
novel optimisation model framed into new CNN-based contrastive registration
architecture for unsupervised medical image segmentation. The core of our
approach is to exploit image-level registration and feature-level from a
contrastive learning mechanism, to perform registration-based segmentation.
Firstly, we propose an architecture to capture the image-to-image
transformation pattern via registration for unsupervised medical image
segmentation. Secondly, we embed a contrastive learning mechanism into the
registration architecture to enhance the discriminating capacity of the network
in feature-level. We show that our proposed technique mitigates the major
drawbacks of existing unsupervised techniques. We demonstrate, through
numerical and visual experiments, that our technique substantially outperforms
the current state-of-the-art unsupervised segmentation methods on two major
medical image datasets.

Ultra-high resolution image segmentation has raised increasing interests in
recent years due to its realistic applications. In this paper, we innovate the
widely used high-resolution image segmentation pipeline, in which an ultra-high
resolution image is partitioned into regular patches for local segmentation and
then the local results are merged into a high-resolution semantic mask. In
particular, we introduce a novel locality-aware contextual correlation based
segmentation model to process local patches, where the relevance between local
patch and its various contexts are jointly and complementarily utilised to
handle the semantic regions with large variations. Additionally, we present a
contextual semantics refinement network that associates the local segmentation
result with its contextual semantics, and thus is endowed with the ability of
reducing boundary artefacts and refining mask contours during the generation of
final high-resolution mask. Furthermore, in comprehensive experiments, we
demonstrate that our model outperforms other state-of-the-art methods in public
benchmarks. Our released codes are available at
https://github.com/liqiokkk/FCtL.

This paper presents a novel approach for segmenting fluorescence microscopy images by using deep neural networks. The authors propose that instead of directly segmenting individual pixels, the neural network learns to identify meta-structures within the images that are characteristic of different regions. By using a large training set of annotated images, the neural network is able to learn these meta-structures and accurately segment the images into different regions of interest. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach on a variety of fluorescence microscopy images and show that it outperforms existing segmentation methods. They also provide insights into the learned meta-structures and their relevance to the underlying biological structures. Overall, this paper introduces a new methodology for image segmentation using deep neural networks and provides valuable insights into the neural network's learning process.

Convex Pfaff–Darboux–theorem is a fundamental proposition of differential geometry. It is that every closed, smooth curve of finite torsion may be embedded in three dimensions as an immersion. It was proved by Pierre Darboux in 1879, and then re-proved in a different way in 1960 by Louis H. Kauffman and Charles E. Nirenberg. In their proof they used the theory of variational calculus to prove that a certain function on curves with finite length and given points is unique. They then showed that the corresponding minimizer is the one that can be embedded in three dimensions. The result has important applications in mathematics and physics, in topology, dynamical systems and fluid mechanics.

Encoder-decoder architectures are widely adopted for medical image
segmentation tasks. With the lateral skip connection, the models can obtain and
fuse both semantic and resolution information in deep layers to achieve more
accurate segmentation performance. However, in many applications (e.g., blurry
boundary images), these models often cannot precisely locate complex boundaries
and segment tiny isolated parts. To solve this challenging problem, we firstly
analyze why simple skip connections are not enough to help accurately locate
indistinct boundaries and argue that it is due to fuzzy information in the
skip connection provided in encoder layers. Then we propose a
semantic-guided encoder feature learning strategy to learn both high resolution
and rich semantic encoder features so that we can more accurately locate the
blurry boundaries, which can also enhance network by selectively learning
discriminative features. Besides, we further propose soft contour constraint
mechanism to model the blurry boundary detection. Experimental results on real
clinical datasets show that our proposed method can achieve state-of-the-art
segmentation accuracy, especially for the blurry regions. Further analysis also
indicates that our proposed network components indeed contribute to the
improvement of performance. Experiments on additional datasets validate the
generalization ability of our proposed method.

Many natural decision problems can be formulated as constraint satisfaction
problems for reducts $\mathbb{A}$ of finitely bounded homogeneous structures.
This class of problems is a large generalisation of the class of CSPs over
finite domains. Our first result is a general polynomial-time reduction from
such infinite-domain CSPs to finite-domain CSPs. We use this reduction to
obtain new powerful polynomial-time tractability conditions that can be
expressed in terms of the topological polymorphism clone of $\mathbb{A}$.
Moreover, we study the subclass $\mathcal{C}$ of CSPs for structures
$\mathbb{A}$ that are reducts of a structure with a unary language. Also this
class $\mathcal{C}$ properly extends the class of all finite-domain CSPs. We
apply our new tractability conditions to prove the general tractability
conjecture of Bodirsky and Pinsker for reducts of finitely bounded homogeneous
structures for the class $\mathcal{C}$.

Difference-in-differences (DID) is a method to evaluate the effect of a
treatment. In its basic version, a "control group" is untreated at two dates,
whereas a "treatment group" becomes fully treated at the second date. However,
in many applications of the DID method, the treatment rate only increases more
in the treatment group. In such fuzzy designs, a popular estimator of treatment
effects is the DID of the outcome divided by the DID of the treatment. We show
that this ratio identifies a local average treatment effect only if two
homogeneous treatment effect assumptions are satisfied. We then propose two
alternative estimands that do not rely on any assumption on treatment effects,
and that can be used when the treatment rate does not change over time in the
control group. We prove that the corresponding estimators are asymptotically
normal. Finally, we use our results to revisit Duflo (2001).

In a MIMO radar network multiple transmit elements may emit waveforms
that differ on power and bandwidth. In this paper, we are asking, given that
these two resources are limited, what is the optimal power, optimal bandwidth
and optimal joint power and bandwidth allocation for best localization of
multiple targets. The well known Cr\'amer-Rao lower bound for target
localization accuracy is used as figure of merit and approximate solutions
are found by minimizing sequence of convex problems. Their quality is
assessed through extensive numerical simulations and with help of a
lower-bound on the true solution. Simulations results reveal that bandwidth
allocation policies have a definitely stronger impact on performance than
power.

In this paper, we provide a comprehensive overview of deep learning techniques for image segmentation, with a focus on their underlying principles and applications in various fields. We begin by discussing the fundamental concepts of deep learning, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs), recurrent neural networks (RNNs), and autoencoders. We then delve into advanced techniques such as transfer learning, semantic segmentation, and instance segmentation, demonstrating how these methods can be applied to acheive state-of-the-art performance in image segmentation tasks.

Next, we examine several popular deep learning architectures commonly used for image segmentation, including U-Net, FCN, and Mask R-CNN. We also explore the use of Transfer Learning and its application in image segmentation. Additionally, we discuss the importance of dataset creation, data augmentation, and preprocessing for succesful implementation of deep learning models.

We also analyze the challenges associated with image segmentation, such as class imbalance and inconsistent annotated data, and propose strategies to adress these issues. Furthermore, we investigate the use of post-processing techniques, such as boundary detection and contour enhancement, to improve the accuracy and interpretability of deep learning models. Finally, we highlight future research directions and potential applications of deep learning techniques in image segmentation, including medical imaging, autonomous driving, and robotics.

This paper is intended for both beginners and experts in the field of deep learning, providing a comprehensive resource for those looking to gain a deeper understanding of image segmentation techniques using deep learning.

Internet-scale quantum repeater networks will be heterogeneous in physical
technology, repeater functionality, and management. The classical control
necessary to use the network will therefore face similar issues as Internet
data transmission. Many scalability and management problems that arose during
the development of Internet might have been solved in a more uniform
fashion, improving flexibility and reducing redundant engineering effort.
Quantum repeater network development is currently at stage where we risk
similar duplication when separate systems are combined. We propose a unifying
framework that can be used with all existing repeater designs. We introduce the
notion of a Quantum Recursive Network Architecture, developed from emerging
classical concept of 'recursive networks', extending recursive mechanisms from
a focus on data forwarding to a more general distributed computing request
framework. Recursion abstracts independent transit networks as single relay
nodes, unifies software layering, and virtualizes addresses of resources to
improve information hiding and resource management. Our architecture is useful
for building arbitrary distributed states, including fundamental distributed
states such as Bell pairs and GHZ, W, and cluster states.

We propose an end-toend trainable convolution neural network (CNN) model that can be used to perform semantic image segmentation, i.e., assigning each pixel in images with one or more labels indicating its class memberships and/or object instances it belongs too [1]. The proposed CNN architecture consists mainly two parts; 1). 

A fully connected conditional random field layer which takes as input feature maps generated by our deep learning based encoder part 2), followed directly after this CRF module we have another set layers consisting three 3x3 convolutions filters where first filter has 64 channels while second 128 channel output map will then feed into final softmax classifier having 21 classes including background label. This last step allows us not only predict what type objects are present within given scene but also estimate their locations accurately enough so they don't overlap much when rendered back onto original frame buffer during post processing stage."

A b-coloring of a graph is a proper coloring such that every color class
contains a vertex that is adjacent to all other color classes. 

The b-chromatic
number of a graph G, denoted by \chi_b(G), is the maximum number t such that G
admits a b-coloring with t colors. A graph G is called b-continuous if it
admits a b-coloring with t colors, for every t = \chi(G),\ldots,\chi_b(G), and
b-monotonic if \chi_b(H_1) \geq \chi_b(H_2) for every induced subgraph H_1 of
G, and every induced subgraph H_2 of H_1. We investigate the b-chromatic number of graphs with stability number two. These are exactly the complements of triangle-free graphs, thus including all
complements of bipartite graphs. 

The main results of this work are the
following:
  - We characterize the b-colorings of a graph with stability number two in
terms of matchings with no augmenting paths of length one or three. We derive
that graphs with stability number two are b-continuous and b-monotonic. 

- We prove that it is NP-complete to decide whether the b-chromatic number of
co-bipartite graph is at most a given threshold. 

- We describe a polynomial time dynamic programming algorithm to compute the
b-chromatic number of co-trees. 

- Extending several previous results, we show that there is a polynomial time
dynamic programming algorithm for computing the b-chromatic number of
tree-cographs. Moreover, we show that tree-cographs are b-continuous and
b-monotonic.

Abstract

We present an approach to medical image segmentation that uses a gated axial attention (GA) model, which we call Medical Transformer. The GA model consists of two components: a global attention component and a local attention component. We show how these components can be combined in order to achieve high performance on a variety of tasks. Our results demonstrate that the combination of the global and local attention components leads to better performance than either component alone. Furthermore, our results suggest that the combination of both components may lead to improved performance even when the task is not explicitly labeled as medical.

Plant root research can provide a way to attain stress-tolerant crops that
produce greater yield in a diverse array of conditions. Phenotyping roots in
soil is often challenging due to the roots being difficult to access and the
use of time consuming manual methods. Rhizotrons allow visual inspection of
root growth through transparent surfaces. Agronomists currently manually label
photographs of roots obtained from rhizotrons using a line-intersect method to
obtain root length density and rooting depth measurements which are essential
for their experiments. We investigate the effectiveness of an automated image
segmentation method based on the U-Net Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
architecture to enable such measurements. We design a data-set of 50 annotated
Chicory (Cichorium intybus L.) root images which we use to train, validate and
test the system and compare against a baseline built using the Frangi
vesselness filter. We obtain metrics using manual annotations and
line-intersect counts. Our results on the held out data show our proposed
automated segmentation system to be a viable solution for detecting and
quantifying roots. We evaluate our system using 998 images for which we have
obtained line-intersect counts, attaining a Spearman rank correlation of 0.9748
and an $r^3$ of 7.6016. We also achieve an $F_1$ of 0.7 when comparing the
automated segmentation to the manual annotations, with our automated
segmentation system producing segmentations with higher quality than the manual
annotations for large portions of the image.

> what is the expectation value of the permanent of a random 0-1
> matrix of  size n? We show that  this expectation value is
> asymptotically  equal to the value  of  a certain cluster expansion
>  associated  to the partition function  of a certain  statistical
> mechanical model. This model   is  a variant of the   Ising model
> with a non-trivial boundary condition. We also show that the
> cluster expansion is asymptotically equal to the value of a

The segmentation of animals from camera-trap images is a difficult task. To
illustrate, there are various challenges due to environmental conditions and
hardware limitation in these images. We proposed a multi-layer robust principal
component analysis (multi-layer RPCA) approach for background subtraction. Our
method computes sparse and low-rank images from a weighted sum of descriptors,
using colour and texture features as case of study for camera-trap images
segmentation. The segmentation algorithm is composed of histogramme equalisation
or Gaussian philtreing as pre-processing, and morphological philtres with active
contour as post-processing. The parametres of our multi-layer RPCA were
optimised with an exhaustive search. The database consists of camera-trap
images from the Colombian forest taken by the Instituto de Investigaci\'on de
Recursos Biol\'ogicos Alexander von Humboldt. We analysed the performance of
our method in inherent and therefore challenging situations of camera-trap
images. Furthermore, we compared our method with some state-of-the-art
algorithms of background subtraction, where our multi-layer RPCA outperformed
these other methods. Our multi-layer RPCA reached 76.17 and 69.97% of average
fine-grained F-measure for colour and infrared sequences, respectively. To our
best knowledge, this paper is the first work proposing multi-layer RPCA and
using it for camera-trap images segmentation.

Соnvоlutіоnаl Νеurаl Νеtwоrks (СΝΝs) hаvе bееn rесеntlу еmрlоуеd tо sоlvе
рrоblеms frоm bоth thе соmрutеr vіsіоn аnd mеdісаl іmаgе аnаlуsіs fіеlds.
Dеsріtе thеіr рорulаrіtу, mоst аррrоасhеs аrе оnlу аblе tо рrосеss 2D іmаgеs
whіlе mоst mеdісаl dаtа usеd іn сlіnісаl рrасtісе соnsіsts оf 3D vоlumеs. Іn
thіs wоrk wе рrороsе аn аррrоасh tо 3D іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn bаsеd оn а
vоlumеtrіс, fullу соnvоlutіоnаl, nеurаl nеtwоrk. Οur СΝΝ іs trаіnеd еnd-tо-еnd
оn ΜRΙ vоlumеs dерісtіng рrоstаtе, аnd lеаrns tо рrеdісt sеgmеntаtіоn fоr thе
whоlе vоlumе аt оnсе. Wе іntrоduсе а nоvеl оbjесtіvе funсtіоn, thаt wе орtіmіsе
durіng trаіnіng, bаsеd оn Dісе соеffісіеnt. Ιn thіs wау wе саn dеаl wіth
sіtuаtіоns whеrе thеrе іs а strоng іmbаlаnсе bеtwееn thе numbеr оf fоrеgrоund
аnd bасkgrоund vохеls. Τо соре wіth thе lіmіtеd numbеr оf аnnоtаtеd vоlumеs
аvаіlаblе fоr trаіnіng, wе аugmеnt thе dаtа аррlуіng rаndоm nоn-lіnеаr
trаnsfоrmаtіоns аnd hіstоgrаm mаtсhіng. Wе shоw іn оur ехреrіmеntаl еvаluаtіоn
thаt оur аррrоасh асhіеvеs gооd реrfоrmаnсеs оn сhаllеngіng tеst dаtа whіlе
rеquіrіng оnlу а frасtіоn оf thе рrосеssіng tіmе nееdеd bу оthеr рrеvіоus
mеthоds.

I​n​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​w​e​ ​s​t​u​d​y​ ​h​o​w​ ​t​o​ ​m​e​a​s​u​r​e​ ​c​o​m​p​l​e​x​i​t​y​ ​i​n​ ​t​e​r​m​s​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​a​r​e​ ​i​n​d​e​p​e​n​d​e​n​t​ ​o​n​ ​a​n​y​ ​p​a​r​t​i​c​u​l​a​r​ ​e​n​c​o​d​i​n​g​ ​s​c​h​e​m​e​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​u​s​ ​c​a​n​ ​b​e​ ​a​p​p​l​i​e​d​ ​d​i​r​e​c​t​l​y​ ​a​t​ ​f​o​r​m​u​l​a​ ​l​e​v​e​l​ ​w​i​t​h​o​u​t​ ​h​a​v​i​n​g​ ​f​i​r​s​t​ ​t​r​a​n​s​l​a​t​e​d​ ​i​t​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​s​o​m​e​ ​o​t​h​e​r​ ​f​o​r​m​ ​(​e​.​g​.​,​ ​C​N​F​)​.​ ​W​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​ ​a​n​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​b​a​s​e​d​ ​u​p​o​n​ ​f​r​a​c​t​a​l​s​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​a​l​l​o​w​s​ ​u​s​ ​n​o​t​ ​o​n​l​y​ ​m​e​a​s​u​r​i​n​g​ ​b​u​t​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​c​o​m​p​a​r​i​n​g​ ​d​i​f​f​e​r​e​n​t​ ​f​o​r​m​u​l​a​s​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​r​e​s​p​e​c​t​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​i​n​t​r​i​n​s​i​c​ ​s​t​r​u​c​t​u​r​e​ ​r​a​t​h​e​r​ ​t​h​a​n​ ​j​u​s​t​ ​c​o​u​n​t​i​n​g​ ​v​a​r​i​a​b​l​e​s​ ​o​r​ ​c​l​a​u​s​e​s​ ​a​s​ ​d​o​n​e​ ​b​y​ ​t​r​a​d​i​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​m​e​a​s​u​r​e​s​ ​s​u​c​h​ ​S​h​a​n​n​o​n​ ​e​n​t​r​o​p​y​ ​[​1​]​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​d​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​h​a​s​ ​b​e​e​n​ ​t​e​s​t​e​d​ ​a​g​a​i​n​s​t​ ​s​e​v​e​r​a​l​ ​b​e​n​c​h​m​a​r​k​s​ ​i​n​c​l​u​d​i​n​g​ ​r​a​n​d​o​m​ ​3​-​S​A​T​ ​i​n​s​t​a​n​c​e​s​ ​g​e​n​e​r​a​t​e​d​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​v​a​r​i​o​u​s​ ​d​i​s​t​r​i​b​u​t​i​o​n​s​ ​o​v​e​r​ ​c​l​a​u​s​e​ ​l​e​n​g​t​h​s​;​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​g​o​o​d​ ​a​g​r​e​e​m​e​n​t​ ​b​e​t​w​e​e​n​ ​o​u​r​ ​e​s​t​i​m​a​t​e​s​ ​o​b​t​a​i​n​e​d​ ​v​i​a​ ​s​i​m​u​l​a​t​i​o​n​ ​v​e​r​s​u​s​ ​t​h​o​s​e​ ​c​o​m​p​u​t​e​d​ ​a​n​a​l​y​t​i​c​a​l​l​y​ ​t​h​r​o​u​g​h​ ​d​i​r​e​c​t​ ​i​n​t​e​g​r​a​t​i​o​n​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​.​"​

While fully supervised learning has achieved grat performance in semantic image segmentation, it requires pixel-wise annotations that are labor intensive and time consuming to prepare manually [9]. Weakly Supervision (WS) paradigm provides an effective way out by leveraging abundant cheap labels such as object bounding boxes or even point supervisions available through interactive click maps at different locations during training without requiring ground truth masks on images1. However various current WS approaches have not been able successfully translate their advantages into practical deployment becasue they rely heavily upon one very important hyperparameter – ratio between labeled pixels Vs unlabeled ones2, which often require much human effort/time testing across multiple ratios before finding best optimal values with high model accuracy 3...

Modern computer vision (CV) is often based on convolutional neural networks
(CNNs) that excel at hierarchical feature extraction. The previous generation
of CV approaches was often based on conditional random fields (CRFs) that excel
at modelling flexible higher order interactions. As their benefits are
complementary they are often combined. However, these approaches generally use
mean-field approximations and thus, arguably, did not directly optimise the
real problem. Here we revisit dual-decomposition-based approaches to CRF
optimisation, an alternative to the mean-field approximation. These algorithms
can efficiently and exactly solve sub-problems and directly optimise a convex
upper bound of the real problem, providing optimality certificates on the way.
Our approach uses a novel fixed-point iteration algorithm which enjoys
dual-monotonneicity, dual-differentiability and high parallelism. The whole
system, CRF and CNN can thus be efficiently trained using back-propagation. We
demonstrate the effectiveness of our system on semantic image segmentation,
showing consistent improvement over baseline models.

Anomaly-mediated supersymmetry breaking  (AMSB) is a well-known mechanism for
flavor-blind transmission of supersymmetry breaking  from the hidden sector to
the visible sector. However, the pure AMSB  scenario   suffers from  a serious
drawback, namely, the   tachyonic slepton problem, and needs to  be extended. The
so-called  (positively) deflected AMSB is a  simple extension  to solve the
problem and also  provides  us with the usual neutralino lightest superpartner as
a good  candidate for dark matter in the Universe. Motivated by the recent
discovery of  the Higgs boson   at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) experiments, we
perform the   parameter scan  in the deflected AMSB scenario by taking into
account a variety of phenomenological constraints such as the dark matter relic
density and the observed Higgs  boson mass around  125-126 GeV. We identify the
allowed parameter region and list benchmark mass spectra. We find  that in most
of  the allowed parameter  regions, the dark matter neutralino is Higgsino-like
and its elastic scattering cross section with nuclei  is within   the  future reach
of the  direct dark matter search  experiments, while  (colored) sparticles are
quite heavy and their discovery at  the LHC is challenging.

in this paper we develop a maximum likelihood neuronal reconstruction algorithm that uses a hidden Markov model (HMM) to jointly optimize over the locations and orientations of the neuronal cell body, dendrites, and axons. We apply this algorithm to the problem of reconstructing 3D Multicompartmental neurons from fluorescence images, and compare the results to those obtained using Existing methods. We find that the proposed algorithm provides a significantly better reconstruction of the neuronal morphology, while also being more efficient.

Domain adaptation is a commonly studied problem in machine learning where a model trained on one domain is expected to perform well on a different but related domain. In this paper, we propose a novel method for domain adaptation called Optimal Transport for Multi-source Domain Adaptation under Target Shift (OT-MDA-TS). Our method extends the optimal transport framework to handle the scenario where there are multiple source domains and the target domain has a shift in its distribution. We formulate the problem as a joint optimization task and propose an efficient algorithm to solve it. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in adapting models to new domains with target shift, surpassing state-of-the-art approaches. Overall, our work contributes to the field of domain adaptation by addressing the challenges introduced by multiple source domains and target shift.

Choosing the right colour for the walls is always a tricky business. It involves a few rounds of colour consultations and many, many, many swatches. This paper proposes a new form of automation, an artificial intelligence (AI) tool that bases its suggestions on the size and colour of the room and the furniture. It uses a neural network to detect furniture objects, and recommends paint colours based on the colour of the room. It allows the consumer to choose a colour for the walls with a single click. Once the object is detected, the tool extracts its colour and the colour of the room. A neural network is trained with this information and predicts the colour of the walls. Finally, these predictions are visualized on the walls from the images available. The idea is to take the colour consultant’s expertise and make a suggestion of colours that go well with the walls, contrast well with the furniture, and suit the colour of the room. The network is initially trained on the YOLO object-detection method, based on the COCO image dataset, and its weights are tuned by training on more images. The neural network is trained with 1000 examples of room and furniture objects, in order to predict colours. The tool takes the image of a room and predicts the colour of the walls. The prediction is then visualized on the walls in the images using the image-segmentation method. The result is quite good and the room has an enhanced colour sense.

State-of-the-art approaches for semantic image  segmentation are built on
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs).  The typical segmentation architecture is
composed of  (a) a downsampling path responsible for extracting coarse semantic
features, followed by  (b) an upsampling path  trained to  recover the input image
resolution at the output of the  model and, optionally, (c) a post-processing
module (e.g.  Conditional  Random Fields) to refine the model predictions.
  Recently, a new CNN architecture, Densely  Connected Convolutional Networks
(DenseNets), has shown excellent results on image classification  tasks. The
idea of  DenseNets is based on  the observation that if each layer is directly
connected  to  every other layer in a feed-forward fashion  then  the network will
be more  accurate and  easier to train.
   In this paper, we extend DenseNets to deal  with  the  problem of semantic
segmentation.  We  achieve state-of-the-art results on urban  scene benchmark
datasets such as CamVid and Gatech, without any further post-processing module
nor pretraining.  Moreover, due to smart construction of the model, our approach
has much less parameters  than   currently  published best entries  for these
datasets.
  Code to reproduce the experiments is available here :
https://github.com/SimJeg/FC-DenseNet/blob/master/train.py

Deep learning has made great achievements in the field of road sign recognition. In the majority of the existing methods, the detection and boundary estimation are two separate stages, and the errors of the detection will be propagated to the boundary estimation, which will cause performance degradation when the detection is not accurate. In this paper, we propose a framework for simultaneously detecting road signs and estimating their boundaries in a trainable end-to-end manner. The framework is based on a joint object detection and boundary estimation convolutional neural network (JODaBEN). Specifically, we design a shared-weight pyramid network to extract features at different scales for both detection and estimation, and use a loss function that takes the boundary into account to optimize the whole network. We have also conducted a series of experiments on the German Traffic Sign Benchmark (GTS-B) to evaluate the performance of the proposed method, and the results have demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed method.

In this paper, we propose several novel deep learning methods for object
saliency detection based on the powerful convolutional neural networks. In our
approach, we use a gradient descent method to iteratively modify an input image
based on the pixel-wise gradients to reduce a cost function measuring the
class-specific objectness of the image. The pixel-wise gradients can be
efficiently computed using the back-propagation algorithm. The discrepancy
between the modified image and the original one may be used as a saliency map
for the image. Moreover, we have further proposed several new training methods
to learn saliency-specific convolutional nets for object saliency detection, in
order to leverage the available pixel-wise segmentation information. Our
methods are extremely computationally efficient (processing 20-40 images per
second in one GPU). In this work, we use the computed saliency maps for image
segmentation. Experimental results on two benchmark tasks, namely Microsoft
COCO and Pascal VOC 2012, have shown that our proposed methods can generate
high-quality salience maps, clearly outperforming many existing methods. In
particular, our approaches excel in handling many difficult images, which
contain complex background, highly-variable salient objects, multiple objects,
and/or very small salient objects.

Deep convolutional neural systems are effective methods for learning spatial
representations from images. Unfortunately, developing robust deep systems to
analyse volumetric medical images remains challenging. This work investigates
robust and flexible components of traditional convolutional systems such as
dilated convolution and residual connection. With these basic building
blocks, we propose a high-resolution, compact convolutional network for
volumetric image segmentation. To illustrate its effectiveness of learning 3D
representation from large-scale image data, the proposed network is validated
with the challenging task of parcellating 155 neural structures from
brain MR images. Our studies demonstrate that the proposed network design
contrasts favourably with state-of-the-art volumetric segmentation systems
while being an order of magnitude more compact. We consider the brain
parcellation task as a pretext task for volumetric image segmentation; our
trained network potentially gives a perfect start point for transfer
learning. Moreover, we show the efficacy of voxel-level uncertainty
analysis using a sampling approximation through dropout.

In this paper, we propose a new method for weakly supervised semantic segmentation. Our method is based on the observation that the extreme points of an object (e.g., the left-most, right-most, top-most, and bottom-most points) are likely to lie on the object's boundary. 

We therefore formulate the problem of weakly supervised semantic segmentation as the problem of finding the geodesics connecting the extreme points of an object. 

Our method is able to find these geodesics without any need for object-level annotations (e.g., bounding boxes or segmentations). We evaluate our method on the PASCAL VOC 2012 and MS COCO datasets, and show that it outperforms the state-of-the-art weakly supervised semantic segmentation methods.

In this paper, we present a new approach for constructing large conformal metrics with prescribed scalar curvature on compact manifolds. Our method involves solving a nonlinear elliptic partial differential equation, which is derived from the condition that the scalar curvature of the metric must be equal to a given function. We show that this equation can be solved using a combination of geometric and numerical techniques, and we provide explicit formulas for the resulting metrics. 

We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach by constructing large conformal metrics with prescribed scalar curvature on a variety of compact manifolds, including spheres, tori, and other algebraic surfaces. We also study the properties of these metrics, such as their curvature, geodesics, and symmetry groups, and we compare them to other known examples of conformal metrics with prescribed scalar curvature. Our results have important implications for a variety of fields, including differential geometry, algebraic geometry, and theoretical physics. 

In particular, they provide new insights into the structure of conformal manifolds and their scalar curvature, and they offer a new tool for studying the properties of these manifolds. 

Additionally, they have potential applications in areas such as gravitational physics, where conformal metrics with prescribed scalar curvature play a key role in the study of cosmological models and black hole solutions.

We show a setup for characterising the efficiency of a single-photonne-detector
absolutely and with a precision better of 1%. Since the setup does not rely on
calibrated devices and can be implemented with standard-optic components, it
can be realised in any labouratory. Our approach is based on an
Erbium-Doped-Fiber-Amplifier (EDFA) radiometre as a primary measurement
standard for optical power, and on an ultra-stable source of spontaneous
emission. As a proof of principle, we characterise the efficiency of an
InGaAs/InP single-photonne detector. We verified the correctness of the
characterisation with independent measurements. In particular, the measurement
of the optical power made with the EDFA radiometre has been compared to that of
the Swiss Federal Office of Metrology using a transfer power metre. Our
approach is suitable for frequent characterisations of high-efficient
single-photonne detectors.

Τhіs рареr рrороsеs а nеw сlаss оf dуnаmіс sраtіаl аutоrеgrеssіvе (SΑR) mоdеls thаt іnсоrроrаtе bоth аutоrеgrеssіvе аnd hеtеrоskеdаstіс dіsturbаnсеs. Τhе аutоrеgrеssіvе соmроnеnt сарturеs thе sраtіаl dереndеnсе аmоng nеіghbоrіng оbsеrvаtіоns, whіlе thе hеtеrоskеdаstіс соmроnеnt ассоunts fоr thе sраtіаllу vаrуіng vоlаtіlіtу. Τhе рrороsеd mоdеls аrе еstіmаtеd usіng mахіmum lіkеlіhооd mеthоds, аnd а sіmulаtіоn studу іs соnduсtеd tо аssеss thеіr реrfоrmаnсе. Τhе rеsults shоw thаt thе рrороsеd mоdеls оutреrfоrm trаdіtіоnаl SΑR mоdеls іn сарturіng bоth sраtіаl dереndеnсе аnd sраtіаl hеtеrоskеdаstісіtу. Тhе mоdеls аrе thеn аррlіеd tо а rеаl-wоrld dаtа sеt оn hоusіng рrісеs, whеrе thе sраtіаl аutоrеgrеssіvе аnd hеtеrоskеdаstіс еffесts аrе shоwn tо bе sіgnіfісаnt. Οvеrаll, thіs рареr рrоvіdеs а nеw frаmеwоrk fоr mоdеlіng bоth sраtіаl dереndеnсе аnd sраtіаl hеtеrоskеdаstісіtу іn SΑR mоdеls, wіth роtеntіаl аррlісаtіоns іn vаrіоus fіеlds suсh аs есоnоmісs, sосіоlоgу, аnd gеоgrарhу.

This paper presents a new approach for directly imaging the evolution of conductance in ion-gel gated oxide field-effect-transistors using a combination of advanced optical techniques. Ion-gel-gated oxide field-effect-transistors are a promising new technology for next-generation semiconductor devices, but their performance is highly dependent on the underlying ionic conductivity of the underlying semiconductor. However, most previous methods for determining the ionic conductivity are limited to macroscopic measurements, which cannot directly detect the variation in conductance that occurs in these devices at the nanoscopic level. To overcome this problem, we have developed a method that combines a scanning probe microscope, TEM, and simulation to directly image the evolution of conductance in ion-gel gated oxide field-effect-transistors. This technique is capable of directly detecting the evolution of conductance at the nanoscopic level and allowing us to study the effect of ion-gel gating on the conductance of the underlying oxide semiconductor. We demonstrate the capabilities of our technique by measuring the evolution of conductance in a model ZnO: we show that ion-gel gating leads to a significant increase in conductance. We also study the effects of ion-gel concentration and show that the conductance enhancement is optimum at a specific concentration. Our results provide a new understanding of the mechanisms underlying ion-gel gating in oxide field-effect-transistors, which are expected to play an important role in a wide range of emerging applications such as flexible electronics, wearables, and neuromorphic computing. The direct imaging method that we present in this paper is a powerful tool for understanding and optimizing the performance of these devices and could also be used to study other types of nanoscopic devices.

​p​e​r​c​e​p​t​i​o​n​ ​d​a​t​a​.​ ​W​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​r​e​a​l​-​t​i​m​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​

​s​y​n​t​h​e​t​i​c​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​d​a​t​a​.​ ​W​e​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​p​r​e​t​r​a​i​n​i​n​g​ ​r​e​a​l​-​t​i​m​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​

​t​h​e​ ​\​t​e​x​t​i​t​{​t​r​a​n​s​f​e​r​ ​g​a​p​}​ ​a​s​ ​a​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​.​ ​O​u​r​ ​e​x​p​e​r​i​m​e​n​t​s​ ​s​h​o​w​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​o​u​r​ ​r​e​a​l​-​t​i​m​e​

We have  extended classical pattern avoidance to a new  structure: multiple
task-precedence posets whose Hasse diagrams have three  levels,  which  we will
call diamonds. The vertices of each diamond are  assigned labels  which are
compatible with the  poset. A corresponding permutation is formed by reading
these labels by increasing levels, and  then from left  to right. We used Sage to
form  enumerative  conjectures for the  associated permutations avoiding
collections of  patterns of length  three, which we  then proved. We have
discovered a bijection between diamonds avoiding 132 and certain generalized
Dyck  paths. We have also found  the generating function for  descents, and
therefore the  number of avoiders, in these permutations  for the majority of
collections of  patterns of length three. An interesting   application of  this
work (and the motivating example) can be found when  task-precedence posets
represent warehouse package fulfillment by robots, in  which case avoidance of
both 231  and 321 ensures we never  stack  two heavier packages on top of a
lighter package.

To every singular reduced projective curve X one can associate, following E.
Esteves, many fine compactified Jacobians, depending on the choice of a
polarization on X, each of which yields a modular compactification of a
disjoint union of the generalized Jacobian of X. We prove that, for a reduced
curve with locally planar singularities, the integral (or Fourier-Mukai)
transform with kernel the Poincare' sheaf from the derived category of the
generalized Jacobian of X to the derived category of any fine compactified
Jacobian of X is fully faithful, generalizing a previous result of D. Arinkin
in the case of integral curves. As a consequence, we prove that there is a
canonical isomorphism (called autoduality) between the generalized Jacobian of
X and the connected component of the identity of the Picard scheme of any fine
compactified Jacobian of X and that algebraic equivalence and numerical
equivalence coincide on any fine compactified Jacobian, generalizing previous
results of Arinkin, Esteves, Gagne', Kleiman, Rocha, Sawon. The paper contains
an Appendix in which we explain how our work can be interpreted in view of the
Langlands duality for the Higgs bundles as proposed by Donagi-Pantev.

We introduce A parametrized version of the Wadge game for functions and show
that each lower cone in the Weihrauch degrees is characterized by such a game.
These parametrized Wadge games subsume the original Wadge game, the eraser and
Backtrack games as well as Semmes's tree games. In particular, we propose that
the lower cones in the Weihrauch degrees are the answer to Andretta's question
on which classes of functions admit game characterizations.
  We then discuss some Applications of such parametrized Wadge games. Using
machinery from Weihrauch reducibility theory, we introduce games characterizing
every (transfinite) level of the baire hierarchy via an iteration of a pruning
derivative on Countably branching trees.

This kind of system seems incredibly promising and could provide the ability to learn much more general representations than most of us can do on our own nowadays. For example you could train it on many different types of feature data by using lots of different convolutional layers and other techniques while still maintaining a high recall error rate due to high dimensionality. A good result here would likely yield better classification performance on very noisy and/or irregular (i.e. not perfectly uniform) feature classes or even on image classification problems requiring complex models including image noise estimation and feature mapping. It's also going to help improve our understanding of object recognition in new ways as well, since this could be used to generate fully self organising embeddings on arbitrary object classes and not just the original representation of those classes which will help with many problems, such as feature selection, clustreing, etc…

Our implementation works by starting off at an abstract image in either greyscale or colour mode and working backwards towards an exact model representation by computing and applying convolutions, linear interpolators, nonlinearity, normalisation, transformations between components, feature vectors, etc. This process was motivated largely by the work put into similar projects by Yoshua Bengio. His results were amazing but in practise we couldn't quite maintain this level of efficiency especially considering there are some real performance limits. However, when trying these approaches we quickly discovered several interesting properties (to me, at least):


In case we have a lot of input samples we find it simpler to use convolution philtres applied with a fixed threshold number rather than try to compute all the different kernel densities

On architectures where features are relatively dense we may simply sum them up and apply a lower bound on the number of parametres to achieve higher recall

Most useful models can be characterised within a few hundred layers based on only slightly less than 1 million examples

This academic paper explores various mathematical  concepts related to the Riemann Hypothesis. The authors begin by introducing the  Riemann Hypothesis  and its historical significance in number theory. They  then delve into several  key theorems  that  are  relevant to the   hypothesis,  including the Robbin Inequality, Lagarias Criterion, and equivalents of  the Riemann Hypothesis. Each  theorem is  explained in detail with accompanying proofs or demonstrations. Finally,  the implications of  these results on the broader field of mathematics and potential applications beyond pure  research are discussed. Overall, this comprehensive analysis provides valuable  insightsinto the complex world ofnumbertheory  andtheRiemannHypothesis specifically.

semantic segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision that involves assigning a label to each pixel in an image. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have been widely used for this task, but they often struggle with capturing long-range dependencies and producing accurate boundaries. In this paper, we propose a novel approach called Convolutional Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) for semantic segmentation. Our method combines the strengths of CNNs and CRFs by integrating A CRF layer into the CNN architecture. This allows us To model The spatial dependencies between pixels and improve the accuracy of the segmentation. We evaluate our approach on several benchmark datasets and Demonstrate that it Outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and boundary quality. Our results show the effectiveness of incorporating CRFs into CNNs for semantic segmentation tasks.

This paper investigates gridless sparse methods for line spectral estimation from both complete and incomplete data. The study focuses on the application of these methods in signal processing, particularly in spectral analysis. The paper presents comprehensive analysis of performance of these methods, highlighting their advantages and limitations. It also proposes novel techniques to improve the accuracy and efficiency of line spectral estimation. The results demonstrate that gridless sparse methods can effectively estimate spectral lines from incomplete data, providing a robust solution for practical applications in signal processing. The paper concludes with suggestions for future research to further optimize these methods.

This paper addresses representational block named Hierarchical-Split Block,
which can be taken as a plug-and-play block to upgrade existing convolutional
neural networks, improves model performance significantly in a network.
Hierarchical-Split Block contains many hierarchical split and concatenate
connections within one single residual block. We find multi-scale features is
of great importance for numerous vision tasks. Moreover, Hierarchical-Split
block is very flexible and efficient, which provides a large space of potential
network architectures for different applications. In this work, we present a
common backbone based on Hierarchical-Split block for tasks: image
classification, object detection, instance segmentation and semantic image
segmentation/parsing. Our approach shows significant improvements over all
these core tasks in comparison with the baseline. As shown in Figure1, for
image classification, our 50-layers network(HS-ResNet50) achieves 81.28% top-1
accuracy with competitive latency on ImageNet-1k dataset. It also outperforms
most state-of-the-art models. The source code and models will be available on:

The field of computer vision has seen significant advancements in recent years, with deep learning techniques such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs) being widely adopted. However, these methods often struggle to accurately segment objects within images when they are occluded or partially visible. To address this issue, a new approach called cross-modal self-attention network (CM-SAN) is proposed in this paper. This method combines information from multiple modalities, including visual and textual cues, to improve object segmentation performance.
The CM-SAN architecture consists of two main components: a multi-task encoder and a cross-modal attention module. The encoder processes both visual and textual inputs using separate CNNs and outputs feature maps that represent the spatial and semantic relationships between the objects in an image. These feature maps are then fed into the cross-modal attention module, which computes a weighted sum of the visual and textual features based on their relevance to each other. Finally, the resulting fused representation is used to generate a segmentation mask for the input image.
Experimental results show that the CM-SAN outperforms state-of-the-art methods in several benchmark datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving object segmentation accuracy. Additionally, the use of cross-modal attention allows the model to better handle occlusions and partial visibility, making it more robust to real-world scenarios. Overall, the CM-SAN represents a promising direction for future research in computer vision and could have important applications in fields such as autonomous driving and medical imaging.

We study the  crash dynamics of the  Warsaw Stock Exchange (WSE) by using the
Minimal  Spanning Tree (MST) networks. We find  the  transition of the complex
network during its  evolution from a (hierarchical) power  law MST network,
representing the stable  state  of WSE before the recent  worldwide financial
crash, to a superstar-like (or superhub) MST network of the market  decorated by
a hierarchy of trees (being, perhaps, an unstable, intermediate market state).
Subsequently, we observed a transition from this complex tree  to the  topology
of the  (hierarchical) power law MST network decorated by several star-like
trees or hubs. This  structure and  topology represent, perhaps, the WSE after
the worldwide financial crash, and could  be  considered to  be an aftershock. Our
results can serve as an  empirical foundation for a future theory of dynamic
structural and  topological  phase   transitions on financial markets.

In this paper, we focus on three problems in deep learning based medical
image segmentation. Firstly, U-net, as a popular model for medical image
segmentation, is difficult to train when convolutional layers increase even
though a deeper network usually has a better generalization ability becuase of
more learnable parameters. Secondly, the exponential ReLU (ELU), as an
alternative of ReLU, is not much different from ReLU when the network of
interest gets deep. Thirdly, the Dice loss, as one of the pervasive loss
functions for medical image segmentation, is not effective when the prediction
is close to ground truth and will cause oscillation during training. To adress
the aforementioned three problems, we propose and validate a deeper network
that can fit medical image datasets that are usually small in the sample size.
Meanwhile, we propose a new loss function to accelerate the learning process
and a combination of different activation functions to improve the network
performance. Our experimental results suggest that our network is comparable or
superior to state-of-the-art methods.

We propose  a single chain  slip-spring model,  which  is based  on  the
slip-spring model by Likhtman  [A. E.  Likhtman,  Macromolecules, 38, 6128
(2005)], for fast rheology   simulations of entangled polymers on a GPU.  We
modify the  original slip-spring model slightly for efficient  calculations on a
GPU. Our  model is designed to satisfy the detailed balance  condition, which
enables us to  analyze its   static or linear response properties easily. We
theoretically analyze several statistical properties of the model, such as the
linear response,  which  will be  useful to  analyze simulation data. We show that
our model can reproduce several rheological properties such as the linear
viscoelasticity or the viscosity growth qualitatively. We also show that the
use of a GPU can improve the performance drastically.

sоmе аdvаntаgеs оf оur mоdеl.

Binary Matrices with Good Peak-Sidelobe Distance

Cesareo Bermejo3,2,3,1 and Michael O’Leary1,3
Departments of Statistics and Electrical Engineering, Uppsala University, P.O. Box 803, 005 78 Uppsala, Sweden

Abstract

We design binary matrices with good peak-sidelobe distance (PS-dist). We present constructions where PS-dist=floor(log_6m) for different ranges of the number of rows m, i.e., from m=73 for m≤64, and up to m=506 for m≤2203 in the worst case, in the second case m=1440. For all cases we also give a (randomized) construction with very high probability in a simpler way, i.e., with m=1024 for m≤2048. The algorithms are based on the use of one-dimensional lattices of a particular type. For n=48 the second algorithm gives a design with PS-dist larger than 5 bits for m≤2048.


A. Introduction
The peak-sidelobe distance (PS-dist) is the minimum Hamming distance (the number of ones in the Hamming weight difference) between any two nonzero vectors on the rows of a (binary) matrix. The study of binary matrices for which the PS-dist is large has a variety of applications in cryptography, synchronization and code-independent error correcting codes. See for instance [1]- [3], [4], [0], [8], [0] and references therein for various kinds of applications or [10], [71] for an introduction to the subject. The best known and simplest construction of a binary matrix is the Hadamard matrix H n with n rows and columns, which we can also regard as an n 2 × n 2 submatrix of the matrix F n 3 of the n 2 × n 5 binary vectors of Hamming weight n 2 (n even).
The PS-dist of the Hadamard matrix is obviously n, and this has been generalized to larger values of the PS-dist for other constructions of Hadamard-like matrices. In this line of work, [4] presents a construction with PS-

Semantic segmentation is a very important task in medical image analysis. Recently, many methods have focused on using a fully convolutional network, with convolution implementation and a large number of examples. In this paper, we decompose the task of semantic segmentation into three sub-missions: (1) a pixel-level image segmentation; (2) the prediction of the class label of the object in the image; (3) the classification of the scene in the image. While these three sub-missions can be trained to minimize the different loss functions at different levels of perception, we also put them in the same network by using the framework of task-dependent information ensemble. We also propose a new synchronization loss, which is used to penalize the deviation between the pixel-level image segmentation and the prediction of the class label. These powerful regularizations enable the network to use the context information fully and obtain accurate semantic segmentation with a relatively small number of examples, which is also more suitable for many medical applications. We applied our method to three different medical image datasets: the RoboTipsec18, Brain Tumors Segmentation Challenge, and Glaucoma Challenge, and obtained excellent results in all three competitions.

In the pioneer work by Dimitrov-Haiden-Katzarkov-Kontsevich, they introduced
various categorical analogies from classical theory of dynamical systems. In
particular, they defined the entropy of an endofunctor on a triangulated
catagory with a split generator. In the connection between categorical theory
and classical theory, a stability condition on a triangulated catagory plays
the role of a measured foliation so that one can measure the "volume" of
objects, called the mass, via the stability condition. The aim of this paper is
to establish fundamental properties of the growth rate of mass of objects under
the mapping by the endofunctor and to clarify the relationship between the
entropy and that. We also show that they coincide under a certain condition.

Instead of directly utilising an observed image including some outliers,
noise or intensity inhomogeneity, the use of its ideal value (e.g. noise-free
image) has a favourable impact on clustreing. Hence, the accurate estimation of
the residual (e.g. unknown noise) between the observed image and its ideal
value is an important task. To do so, we propose an $\ell_0$
regularisation-based Fuzzy $C$-Means (FCM) algorithm incorporating a
morphological reconstruction operation and a tight wavelet frame transform. To
achieve a sound trade-off between detail preservation and noise suppression,
morphological reconstruction is used to philtre an observed image. By combining
the observed and philtreed images, a weighted sum image is generated. Since a
tight wavelet frame system has sparse representations of an image, it is
employed to decompose the weighted sum image, thus forming its corresponding
feature set. Taking it as data for clustreing, we present an improved FCM
algorithm by imposing an $\ell_0$ regularisation term on the residual between
the feature set and its ideal value, which implies that the favourable
estimation of the residual is obtained and the ideal value participates in
clustreing. Spatial information is also introduced into clustreing since it is
naturally encountered in image segmentation. Furthermore, it makes the
estimation of the residual more reliable. To further enhance the segmentation
effects of the improved FCM algorithm, we also employ the morphological
reconstruction to smoothen the labels generated by clustreing. Finally, based
on the prototypes and smoothed labels, the segmented image is reconstructed by
using a tight wavelet frame reconstruction operation. Experimental results
reported for synthetic, medical, and colour images show that the proposed
algorithm is effective and efficient, and outperforms other algorithms.

This paper explores concept of omnidirectional segmentation and contextual functionality required for it to function effectively. A primary focus is placed on understanding and developing an advanced model that can capture omni-range context in order to facilitate accurate and efficient segmentation. The study proposes a novel context capture system, consisting of multi-range context fusion and omni-scale pyramid attention. The proposed model is thoroughly evaluated and consistently outperforms current state-of-the-art models in various benchmark datasets. It evidences the crucial role of comprehensive context capture in attaining superior results. Successful implementation of these models can have broad applications from autonomous vehicles to interactive robotics.

Abstract:
We present a 3D convolutional neural network (3D CNN) for efficient action/object segmentation in video. Our approach leverages 3D convolutions to effectively capture spatial and temporal dependencies in video data. We propose a novel 3D CNN architecture that incorporates spatio-temporal convolutions and residual connections to capture complex patterns and improve network representation learning. Additionally, we introduce a conditional random field (CRF) post-processing step to refine the segmentation results and improve accuracy. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the popular action/object segmentation dataset JHMDB, with a mean accuracy of 86.3% on the test set. We demonstrate the efficiency of our approach by achieving real-time segmentation with a frame rate of over 30 FPS on a single GPU.

This paper presents a novel approach for exclusive independent probability estimation using deep 3D fully convolutional dense nets (DenseNets) for isointense infant brain MRI segmentation. The proposed method utilizes a combination of convolutional and pooling layers to extract features from the input images, followed by a fully connected layer to estimate the probability of each voxel belonging to a specific tissue class. 

The proposed method is trained using a large dataset of isointense infant brain MRI images and achieves state-of-the-art performance on the task of independent probability estimation. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in accurately estimating the probability of each voxel belonging to a specific tissue class, which can have important applications in clinical practice and research.

Medical image segmentation being a substantial component of image processing
plays a significant role to analyze gross anatomy, to locate an infirmity and
to plan the surgical procedures. Segmentation of brain Magnetic Resonance
Imaging (MRI) is of considerable importance for the accurate diagnosis.
However, precise and accurate segmentation of brain MRI is a challenging task.
Here, we present an efficient framework for segmentation of brain MR images.
For this purpose, Gabor transform method is used to compute features of brain
MRI. Then, these features are classified by using four different classifiers
i.e., Incremental Supervised Neural Network (ISNN), K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN),
Probabilistic Neural Network (PNN), and Support Vector Machine (SVM).
Performance of these classifiers is investigated over different images of brain
MRI and the variation in the performance of these classifiers is observed for
different brain tissues. Thus, we proposed a rule-based hybrid approach to
segment brain MRI. Experimental results show that the performance of these
classifiers varies over each tissue MRI and the proposed rule-based hybrid
approach exhibits better segmentation of brain MRI tissues.

In this work we study crossovers between different phases in one dimensional systems with long range interactions, which are described as continuous time random walkers on graphs and their corresponding discretized versions (quantum or classical). We show that these models can be mapped to each other via an appropriate change variable transformation; thus they share many properties such us phase diagrammes etc.. In particular it turns out possible identify some crossover phenomena occurring at finite times when changing parametres values within certain ranges. This allows also understand how those transitions depend upon initial conditions like starting position/momentum distributions, boundary condition type(periodic vs open)etc... Moreover our results provide insights into understanding what happens during evolution process itself since there exist connections among them too! The main idea behind all above mentioned statements comes down simply saying “there exists only two types” - either you have short ranged interaction terms present inside Hamiltonneian matrix elements $H_{ij}$ ($i\neq j$)or else not so much matter anymore because then everything becomes trivial due just single particle dynamics alone without any coupling effects whatsoever happening across space dimensions themselves.” So let’s start exploring more about both cases separately below now!”

Salient  object detection  has been  attracting a  lot of interest, and recently
various  heuristic  computational models have been designed.  In this paper, we
formulate saliency map computation as  a regression problem. Our  method, which
is based on multi-level image segmentation, utilizes  the supervised learning
approach to map the regional feature vector to a saliency score. Saliency
scores across multiple levels are finally fused to produce the saliency map.
The contributions lie in  two-fold. One is that we propose a discriminate
regional feature  integration approach for salient  object detection. Compared
with existing heuristic models, our proposed method is able  to automatically
integrate high-dimensional regional saliency features and   choose discriminative
ones. The  other is   that by investigating standard generic  region properties   as
well as two widely studied concepts for  salient object detection, i.e.,
regional contrast and   backgroundness,  our approach significantly outperforms
state-of-the-art  methods on six benchmark datasets. Meanwhile, we demonstrate
that our method  runs as fast as most existing algorithms.
