Analyzing the exchange energy of two conduction electrons in a crystal we
find that the exchange energy may be negative and, thus, a singlet state may be
favourable. A full overlap in real space of wave functions of two conduction
electrons leads to a deeper exchange energy. Thus, the exchange interaction
causes a bond between two conduction electrons in real space. The singlet bond
is possible because the singlet electrons can simultaneously and permanently
occupy one spatial ground state, so the average energy of paired electrons is
lower than the energy of unpaired electrons. Thus, the pairing is a result of
the Pauli Exclusion Principle. If conduction electrons, before pairing, are put
on the Fermi surface in the momentum space, then every pair may exist
permanently in time. The motion of conduction electrons in the crystal may
prevent the formation of Cooper pairs, because the kinetic energy of the motion
is usually larger than the binding energy in the pair. Conduction electrons as
standing waves are local and have zero momenta, hence their momenta are
synchronous; therefore, weak singlet bonds are stable despite the large kinetic
energy on the Fermi surface. The local pairing of standing waves explains the
inverse isotope effect, Tc-dome, insulator-superconductor transitions and many
other facts about superconductors. The electron pairs, as bosons, can form a
macroscopically coherent Bose-Einstein-Condensate and, thus, become
non-dissipative and non-local.

Deep structured output learning shows great promise in tasks like semantic
image segmentation. We proffer a new, efficient deep structured model learning
scheme, in which we show how deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) can be
used to estimate the messages in message passing inference for structured
prediction with Conditional Random Fields (CRFs). With such CNN message
estimators, we obviate the need to learn or evaluate potential functions for
message calculation. This confers significant efficiency for learning, since
otherwise when performing structured learning for a CRF with CNN potentials it
is necessary to undertake expensive inference for every stochastic gradient
iteration. The network output dimension for message estimation is the same as
the number of classes, in contrast to the network output for general CNN
potential functions in CRFs, which is exponential in the order of the
potentials. Hence CNN message learning has fewer network parametres and is more
scalable for cases that a large number of classes are involved. We apply our
method to semantic image segmentation on the PASCAL VOC 2012 dataset. We
achieve an intersection-over-union score of 73.4 on its test set, which is the
best reported result for methods using the VOC training images alone. This
impressive performance demonstrates the effectiveness and usefulness of our CNN
message learning method.

Germs of Goursat distributions can be classified according to a geometric
coding called an RVT code. Jean (1996) and Mormul (2004) have shown that this
coding carries precisely the same data as the small growth vector. Montgomery
and Zhitomirskii (2010) have shown that such germs correspond to finite jets of
Legendrian curve germs, and that the RVT coding corresponds to the classical
invariant in the singularity theory of planar curves: the Puiseux
characteristic. Here we derive a simple formula for the Puiseux characteristic
of the curve corresponding to a Goursat germ with given small growth vector.
The simplicity of our theorem (compared with the more complex algorithms
previously known) suggests a deeper connection between singularity theory and
the theory of nonholonomic distributions.

This study proposes a method of training multi-organ segmentation networks using sample selection based on relaxed upper confident bound. The approach aims to improve network performance and reduce overfitting through selective use of datasets, focusing primarily on enhancing accuracy in segmenting multiple organs simultaneously. By utilizing this strategy, researchers can increase efficiency during model development without sacrificing overall effectiveness or generalization capabilities. This innovative technique offers promising potential within medical imaging applications requiring precise identification and separation of various anatomical structures from raw image data sets.

T​h​i​s​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​p​r​e​s​e​n​t​s​ ​a​n​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​d​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​ ​f​o​r​ ​s​o​l​v​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​V​e​r​t​e​x​ ​C​o​v​e​r​ ​P​$​_​3​$​ ​p​r​o​b​l​e​m​ ​o​n​ ​g​r​a​p​h​s​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​b​o​u​n​d​e​d​ ​t​r​e​e​w​i​d​t​h​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​d​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​u​t​i​l​i​z​e​s​ ​d​y​n​a​m​i​c​ ​p​r​o​g​r​a​m​m​i​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​g​r​a​p​h​ ​t​r​a​v​e​r​s​a​l​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​ ​t​o​ ​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​t​l​y​ ​i​d​e​n​t​i​f​y​ ​v​e​r​t​i​c​e​s​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​a​r​e​ ​p​a​r​t​ ​o​f​ ​a​ ​m​i​n​i​m​u​m​ ​v​e​r​t​e​x​ ​c​o​v​e​r​,​ ​w​h​i​l​e​ ​a​l​s​o​ ​t​a​k​i​n​g​ ​a​d​v​a​n​t​a​g​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​s​t​r​u​c​t​u​r​a​l​ ​p​r​o​p​e​r​t​i​e​s​ ​i​n​h​e​r​e​n​t​ ​i​n​ ​g​r​a​p​h​s​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​b​o​u​n​d​e​d​ ​t​r​e​e​w​i​d​t​h​.​ ​T​h​r​o​u​g​h​ ​e​x​t​e​n​s​i​v​e​ ​c​o​m​p​u​t​a​t​i​o​n​a​l​ ​e​x​p​e​r​i​m​e​n​t​s​,​ ​w​e​ ​d​e​m​o​n​s​t​r​a​t​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​i​v​e​n​e​s​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​c​y​ ​o​f​ ​o​u​r​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​c​o​m​p​a​r​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​e​x​i​s​t​i​n​g​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​e​s​.​ ​O​v​e​r​a​l​l​,​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​s​t​u​d​y​ ​c​o​n​t​r​i​b​u​t​e​s​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​o​n​g​o​i​n​g​ ​e​f​f​o​r​t​ ​t​o​w​a​r​d​s​ ​d​e​v​e​l​o​p​i​n​g​ ​m​o​r​e​ ​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​t​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​c​o​m​b​i​n​a​t​o​r​i​a​l​ ​o​p​t​i​m​i​z​a​t​i​o​n​ ​p​r​o​b​l​e​m​s​ ​b​y​ ​l​e​v​e​r​a​g​i​n​g​ ​g​r​a​p​h​ ​s​t​r​u​c​t​u​r​e​ ​i​n​f​o​r​m​a​t​i​o​n​.​

We introduce a new multi-dimensional linear embedding -- Piecewise Flat
Embedding (PFE) -- for image segmentation. Based on the principle of sparse signal
recovery, piecewise flat embedding with diverse channels tries to recover a
piecewise constant image representation with sparse region boundaries and
sparse cluster value scattering. The resultant piecewise flat embedding
displays interesting properties such as suppressing rapidly varying signals, and
provides an image representation with higher region identifiability which is
suitable for image segmentation or high-level semantic analysis tasks. We
formulate our embedding as a version of the Laplacian Eigenmap embedding with
an $L_{1,r} (0<p\leq1)$ regularization term to promote sparse solutions. Next,
we devise a two-stage computational optimization based on Bregman iterations to
compute $L_{1,1}$-regularized piecewise flat embeddings. We further generalize
this algorithm through iterative reweighting to resolve the general
$L_{1,b}$-regularized solution. To prove its effectiveness, we integrate PFE
into three existing image segmentation concepts, segmentation based on
clustering and hierarchical segmentation based on contour detection.
Experiments on three main benchmark datasets, BSDS500, MSRC, Stanford
Background Data, and PASCAL Context, show that segmentation algorithms
using our embedding achieve greatly improved outcomes.

Deep learning (SP)-based models have shown excellent reliability in medical
image segmentation. Unfortunately, the models trained on a known database usually fail
when performed on an unknown data obtained from different centers, vendors
and disease populations. In this work, we present a random style transfer
network to tackle the domain generalization issue for multi-vendor and center
cardiac image segmentation. Style transfer is designed to produce training data
with a wider distribution/ complexity, namely domain augmentation. As the
target domain may be unknown, we randomly generate a modality vector for the
target modality in the style transfer stage, to simulate the domain shift for
unknown domains. The model can be trained in a semi-supervised way by
simultaneously implementing a supervised segmentation and an unsupervised style
translation objective. Besides, the framework defines the temporal
information and shape prior of the target by adding three regularization
terms. We evaluated the proposed structure on 40 subjects from the M\&M
challenge2020, and obtained promising performance in the segmentation for data
from unknown vendors and centers.

Disk networks are a method of reducing high-dimensional tensors into a network of lower-dimensional tensors. Until recently, they were most frequently used for modeling entanglement in many-body quantum mechanics. But they are increasingly being applied to supervised learning. In this paper, we formalize image segmentation in the supervised learning setting by a disk network. The basic idea is to elevate the input pixels of image patches to an infinitely high-dimensional feature space, and then to classify the inputs into foreground and background by a linear decision plane. The high-dimensional linear decision plane is represented by a tensor network that is constructed from non-overlapping image patches. The performance of the proposed method is evaluated on three 2D and one 3D biomedical image database, and compared to other well-known segmentation methods. The proposed tensor network model achieves good results compared to the well-known methods.

The performance of Deep Learning applications relies on their reliability but not
on their effectiveness against adversarial perturbations to the input data.
Various attacks and defenses have been proposed to reduce the efficiency of
Deep Neural Networks under the absence of adversarial noise in the natural
image domain. Moreover, complexity in computer-aided diagnosis for volumetric
data has only been explored for certain tasks and with limited attacks. We
propose a new methodology to improve the effectiveness of general medical image
segmentation applications. Our contributions are two-fold: (i) we propose a new
benchmark to improve effectiveness in the context of the Medical Segmentation
Decathlon (MSD) by expanding the previous AutoAttack natural image classification
methodology to the domain of volumetric data segmentation, and (ii) we present a
novel lattice design for RObust Generic medical image segmentation (ROG).
Our findings show that ROG is incapable of generalizing over multiple tasks of
the MSD and largely surpasses the state-of-the-art under sophisticated
adversarial attacks.

Objective: Deformable image registration is A fundamental problem in medical
image analysis, with applications such as longitudinal studies, population
modeling, and atlas based Image segmentation. Registration is often phrased as
an Optimization problem, i.e., finding a deformation field that is optimal
according to a given objective function. Discrete, combinatorial, optimization
techniques have successfully been Employed to solve the resulting optimization
problem. Specifically, optimization based on $\alpha$-expansion with minimal
graph cuts has been proposed as a powerful tool for image registration. The
high computational cost of the graph-cut based optimization approach, however,
limits the utility of this approach for registration of large volume images.
Methods: Here, we propose to accelerate graph-cut based deformable registration
by dividing the image Into overlapping sub-regions and restricting the
$\alpha$-expansion moves to a single sub-region at a time. Results: We
demonstrate empirically that this approach can achieve a large reduction in
computation time -- from days to minutes -- with only a Small penalty in terms
of solution quality. Conclusion: The reduction in Computation Time provided by
the proposed Method makes graph cut based deformable registration viable for
large volume images. Significance: Graph cut based image registration has
previously been shown to produce excellent results, but the high computational
cost has hindered the adoption of the method for registration of Large medical
volume images. Our proposed method Lifts this restriction, requiring only a
small fraction of the computational cost to produce results of comparable
quality.

The CNN-based methods have achieved Impressive results in medical image
segmentation, but it failed to capture the long-range dependencies due to the
inherent locality of convolution operation. Transformer-based methods are
popular in vision tasks recently because of Its capacity of long-range
dependencies and get a promising performance. However, It lacks in modeling
local context, although some works attempted to embed convolutional layer to
overcome This problem and achieved some improvement, but it makes the feature
inconsistent and fails to leverage the natural multi-scale features of
hierarchical transformer, which limit The performance of models. In this Paper,
taking medical image Segmentation as an example, we present MISSFormer, an
effective and powerful Medical Image Segmentation tranSFormer. MISSFormer is a
hierarchical encoder-decoder network and has two appealing designs: 1) a Feed
forward network is redesigned with the proposed Enhanced Transformer Block,
which makes features aligned adaptively and enhances the Long-range
dependencies And local context. 2) We proposed Enhanced Transformer Context
Bridge, a context bridge with the enhanced transformer block to model the
long-range dependencies and Local context of multi-scale features generated by
our hierarchical transformer encoder. Driven by these two designs, the
MISSFormer shows strong capacity to capture more valuable dependencies and
context in medical image segmentation. The experiments on multi-organ and
cardiac segmentation tasks demonstrate the superiority, effectiveness and
robustness of our MISSFormer, the exprimental results of MISSFormer trained
from scratch even outperforms state-of-the-art methods pretrained on ImageNet,
and the core designs can be generalized to other visual segmentation tasks. The
code will be released in Github.

This paper critically examines the existing literature on Twitter-based studies of political collective action, highlighting the prevalent biases that may distort our understanding of these phenomena. The review identifies three main types of biases: selection bias, interpretation bias, and contextual bias. Selection bias refers to the non-random selection of data or cases, interpretation bias to the misinterpretation of tweets and their context, and contextual bias to the disregard of the offline context in which online interactions occur. The paper argues that these biases can significantly affect the validity and reliability of findings, and proposes methodological strategies to mitigate them. The aim is to enhance the rigor and robustness of future research in this area, contributing to a more nuanced understanding of political collective action in the digital age.

The Korteweg-de Vries (KdV) equation  is a nonlinear partial differential equation that describes the evolution  of a slowly varying wave on the surface  of a deep fluid. It is a member of the KdV hierarchy, a  family of  equations that are integrable in the sense that they can be solved by the inverse scattering transform. The  KdV equation has been studied extensively in the context of nonlinear waves, and it has been shown to be related to a number  of  other physical systems, including the nonlinear Schrödinger equation and the  sine-Gordon  equation.

In  this paper,  we study the KdV equation in the context of breathers, which are localized solutions that oscillate periodically  in time. We show   that the  KdV  equation  can be  deformed into a new equation that supports breathers with a complex  wavenumber. This deformation is isospectral,  meaning that the spectra  of  the two equations are the same. We also show that the breathers in the deformed equation can  be  interpreted as  free particles  moving in a complex potential.

This paper  is organized as follows. In  Section 2, we review the KdV  equation   and its relation to the nonlinear Schrödinger  equation. In Section 3, we  introduce the  isospectral deformation  and show  that it  supports breathers with a complex wavenumber. In Section 4, we show that the    breathers in the deformed equation can be interpreted as  free particles moving in a complex  potential. In Section 5, we discuss the implications of our results  and  suggest directions  for  future research.

In this paper, we study the most basic domination invariants in graphs, in
which number 2 is intrinsic part of their definitions. We classify them upon
three criteria, two of which give the following previously studied invariants:
the weak $2$-domination number, $\gamma_{w2}(G)$, the $2$-domination number,
$\gamma_2(G)$, the $\{2\}$-domination number, $\gamma_{\{2\}}(G)$, the double
domination number, $\gamma_{\times 2}(G)$, the total $\{2\}$-domination number,
$\gamma_{t\{2\}}(G)$, and the total double domination number, $\gamma_{t\times
2}(G)$, where $G$ is a graph in which a corresponding invariant is well
defined. The third criterion yields rainbow versions of the mentioned six
parameters, one of which has already been well studied, and three other give
new interesting parameters. Together with a special, extensively studied Roman
domination, $\gamma_R(G)$, and two classical parameters, the domination number,
$\gamma(G)$, and the total domination number, $\gamma_t(G)$, we consider 13
domination invariants in graphs $G$. In the main result of the paper we present
sharp upper and lower bounds of each of the invariants in terms of every other
invariant, large majority of which are new results proven in this paper. As a
consequence of the main theorem we obtain some complexity results for the
studied invariants, in particular regarding the existance of approximation
algorithms and inapproximability bounds.

This proposal outlines a plan to conduct a three detector short-baseline neutrino oscillation program using the Fermilab Booster Neutrino Beam. The goal of this program is to provide valuable insights into neutrino properties and interactions, as well as to improve our understanding of fundamental physics processes. By analyzing data collected from multiple detectors, researchers can gain a more comprehensive view of neutrino oscillations and potentially discover new phenomena or confirm existing theories. This proposal highlights the importance of such a program and details the necessary experimental setups and analysis techniques required to successfully carry it out.

Іn аn аnаlоgу wіth thе Gаlоіs hоmоthеtу рrореrtу fоr tоrsіоn роіnts оf
аbеlіаn vаrіеtіеs thаt wаs usеd іn thе рrооf оf thе Μоrdеll-Lаng соnjесturе, wе
dеsсrіbе а соrrеsроndеnсе bеtwееn thе асtіоn оf а Gаlоіs grоuр аnd thе
dуnаmісаl асtіоn оf а rаtіоnаl mар. Fоr nоnlіnеаr роlуnоmіаls wіth rаtіоnаl
соеffісіеnts, thе іrrеduсіbіlіtу оf thе аssосіаtеd dуnаtоmіс роlуnоmіаl sеrvеs
аs а соnvеnіеnt сrіtеrіоn, аlthоugh wе аlsо vеrіfу thаt thе соrrеsроndеnсе
оссurs іn sеvеrаl саsеs whеn thе dуnаtоmіс роlуnоmіаl іs rеduсіblе. Τhе wоrk оf
Моrtоn, Μоrtоn-Ρаtеl, аnd Vіvаldі-Наtjіsруrоs іn thе еаrlу 1990s соnnесtеd thе
іrrеduсіbіlіtу аnd Gаlоіs-thеоrеtіс рrореrtіеs оf dуnаtоmіс роlуnоmіаls tо
rаtіоnаl реrіоdіс роіnts; frоm thе Gаlоіs-dуnаmісs соrrеsроndеnсе, wе dеrіvе
sіmіlаr соnsеquеnсеs fоr quаdrаtіс реrіоdіс роіnts оf unісrіtісаl роlуnоmіаls.
Τhіs іs suffісіеnt tо dеduсе thе nоn-ехіstеnсе оf quаdrаtіс реrіоdіс роіnts оf
quаdrаtіс роlуnоmіаls wіth ехасt реrіоd 5 аnd 6, оutsіdе оf а sресіfіеd fіnіtе
sеt frоm Моrtоn аnd Κrumm's wоrk іn ехрlісіt Ηіlbеrt іrrеduсіbіlіtу.

Segmentation of magnetic resonance (MR) images is a fundamental step in many
medical imageing-based applications. The recent implementation of deep
convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in image processing has been shown to have
significant impacts on medical image segmentation. Network training of
segmentation CNNs typically requires images and paired annotation data
representing pixel-wise tissue labels referred to as masks. However, the
supervised training of highly efficient CNNs with deeper structure and more
network parametres requires a large number of training images and paired tissue
masks. Thus, there is great need to develop a generalised CNN-based
segmentation method which would be applicable for a wide variety of MR image
datasets with different tissue contrasts. The purpose of this study was to
develop and evaluate a generalised CNN-based method for fully-automated
segmentation of different MR image datasets using a single set of annotated
training data. A technique called cycle-consistent generative adversarial
network (CycleGAN) is applied as the core of the proposed method to perform
image-to-image translation between MR image datasets with different tissue
contrasts. A joint segmentation network is incorporated into the adversarial
network to obtain additional segmentation functionality. The proposed method
was evaluated for segmenting bone and cartilage on two clinical knee MR image
datasets acquired at our institution using only a single set of annotated data
from a publicly available knee MR image dataset. The new technique may further
improve the applicability and efficiency of CNN-based segmentation of medical
images while eliminating the need for large amounts of annotated training data.

This review  presents an in-depth  study  of the literature on segmentation
methods  applied  in dental imaging. Ten segmentation methods were studied and
categorized   according to  the type of the segmentation method  (region-based,
threshold-based, cluster-based, boundary-based or watershed-based), type of
X-ray images used (intra-oral or extra-oral) and characteristics of the dataset
used  to evaluate the  methods in  the state-of-the-art works. We found that the
literature has primarily focused on threshold-based segmentation methods (54%).
80% of the reviewed papers have  used intra-oral X-ray images in their
experiments, demonstrating preference to perform segmentation on images of
already isolated   parts  of  the  teeth, rather  than  using extra-oral  X-rays, which
show tooth structure of the mouth and  bones of the   face. To fill a scientific
gap in the field, a novel data set based on  extra-oral X-ray  images  are
proposed here. A statistical comparison of the results  found  with the 10 image
segmentation methods over our  proposed  data set  comprised of 1,500 images is
also carried out, providing  a  more comprehensive source of performance
assessment. Discussion on limitations of the methods conceived over  the past
year as well as future perspectives on  exploiting learning-based segmentation
methods to improve performance are also provided.

We present for the first time a complete solution to the problem of proving
the correctness of a concurrency control algorithm for collaborative text
editors against the standard consistency model. The success of our approach
stems from the use of comprehensive stringwise operational transformations,
which appear to have escaped a formal treatment until now. Because these
transformations sometimes lead to an increase in the number of operations as
they are transformed, we cannot use inductive methods and adopt the novel idea
of decreasing diagrams instead. 

We also base our algorithm on a client-server
model rather than a peer-to-peer one, which leads to the correct application of
operational transformations to both newly generated and pending operations. And
lastly we solve the problem of latency, so that our algorithm works perfectly
in practice. 

The result of these innovations is the first ever formally correct
concurrency control algorithm for collaborative text editors together with a
fast, fault tolerant and highly scalable implementation.

> Abstract: Images are composed by multiple objects and each object has its own property in shape, color or other visual cues. Pixel-wise classification methods perform very well when class probability can be derived through a single pixel value. However, it often does not meet image properties due to lack of discriminative feature as well as incomplete background statistics used during training stage. In this work, we explore segmenting images without using prior knowledge but through deep learning framework by only one architecture (endto- end system). We propose efficient method using tree structure which consists of groupings with fixed numbers at given resolution level. Through several merging operations on nodes where groups get higher merged until there is no more possible merge. Nodes represent specific region or object. Our algorithm is designed to operate on unsupervised data set with any kinds of objects in order to eliminate the need of providing bounding boxes, or labels in advance. Based on our extensive experiment results over benchmark datasets show that proposed method achieves performance competitive to state-ofthe-art supervised models. Additionally, hierarchical merge provides good generalization ability because they do not require fine grained information for each object such as number of classes per scene therefore, the model robustness against missing pixels and occlusions.

In recent years, transformer-based models have become increasingly popular in natural language processing tasks, particularly in biomedical text mining. However, understanding how these models make predictions remains a significant challenge, potentially limiting their trustworthiness and applicability in practical applications. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to explaining the decisions made by generic attention-based transformer models, which are widely used in bi-modal and encoder-decoder architectures. Our method leverages the concept of feature importance, which is typically applied in interpreting tree-based models, to generate explanations for transformer-based models as well.

We evaluate our explainability method on several benchmark datasets from the biomedical domain and demonstrate its effectiveness in providing insights into the decision-making process of transformer models. Our results show that the proposed approach can identify important features and feature combinations that contribute to the model's predictions, allowing users to understand why certain predictions were made. We also perform a user study with domain experts to assess the usefulness and interpretability of the generated explanations. The feedback obtained confirms that our approach provides valuable insights into the model's behaviour and helps users build confidence in the model's predictions.

Our work has important implications for the broader field of natural language processing and machine learning, where model transparency and accountability are becoming increasingly important. By offering a principled way to explain the decisions made by transformer-based models, we hope to increase trust in these powerful algorithms and improve their practical relevance in various domains.

In  the fields of nanoscience and nanotechnology,  it is important to be able
to functionalize surfaces chemically   for  a wide variety of applications.
Scanning tunneling microscopes (STMs) are important instruments in this  area
used to  measure the surface structure and chemistry with   better than molecular
resolution.  Self-assembly is frequently used to create monolayers that redefine
the surface chemistry in just a single-molecule-thick layer. Indeed,  STM images
reveal rich information  about the  structure of self-assembled monolayers since
they convey chemical and physical properties of the  studied  material.
    In order to  assist  in and   to enhance  the analysis of STM and other images, we
propose and   demonstrate an  image-processing framework that produces two image
segmentations: one is  based on intensities (apparent heights in STM images) and
the other is based on textural patterns.   The  proposed  framework begins with a
cartoon+texture decomposition, which separates an image into its cartoon and
texture components. Afterward, the cartoon   image  is segmented  by a modified
multiphase version of the local Chan-Vese model, while the texture image is
segmented by  a combination  of 2D empirical wavelet transform and a clustering
algorithm.  Overall, our  proposed framework  contains several new features,
specifically in presenting a new application of cartoon+texture decomposition
and of the empirical wavelet transforms  and in developing a specialized
framework  to segment STM images  and other data. To  demonstrate the potential of
our approach, we apply it to actual STM images of cyanide monolayers on
Au\{111\} and  present their corresponding segmentation results.

> Panoptic segmentation aims to assign each pixel in an input image to one of two categories, things or stuffs, while simultaneously labelling them with semantic classes. In this work, we propose a novel Generator Evaluator Selector (GES) network that can be trained end-to-end on both images and videos. Our GES network consists of three modules: generator, evaluator, and selector. The generator module generates multiple candidate masks for each object instance. The evaluator module scores these candidates based on their quality. Finally, the selector module selects the best mask among all candidates as the final prediction. We evaluate our method on COCO 2017 dataset and achieve state-of-the-art performance on both single frame and video sequences.

The guage formalism in Telepalallel Gravity provides an interesting viewpoint
to describe interactions according to an anholonomic observes tetrad basis. The
perturbed spacetime algebra with Weitzenb\"ock connection can be assimilated to
a local complexification based on the SU(4) Yang--Mills theory,that we call
hypercolour or, simply, colour. The formulation of the hypercolour dynamics is
constructed by means of a translational guage, as in the teleparallel gravity.
In particular, this work explores small perturbations of a metric decomposition
related to the Wilson line and the Kaluza--Klein metric, but obtaining
electrodynamics in four dimensions. The spacetime coordinates are now matrices
representing elements of the $\mathfrak{su}(4)$ Lie algebra. To make compatible
the formulation of a coloured gravity with the Lorentz force and the Maxwell's
equations, it is enough to define any energy potential origin as 0 in the event
horizon, instead of the classic zero potential at infinity. Under the coloured
gravity framework, standard electromagnetism can be obtained as a particular
abelian case.

The problem of determining the number of "flooding operations" required to
make a given coloured graph monochromatic in the one-player combinatorial game
Flood-It has been studied extensively from an algorithmic point of view, but
basic questions about the maximum number of moves that might be required in the
worst case remain unanswered. We begin a systematic investigation of such
questions, with the goal of determining, for a given graph, the maximum number
of moves that may be required, taken over all possible colourings. We give
several upper and lower bounds on this quantity for arbitrary graphs and show
that all of the bounds are tight for trees; we also investigate how much the
upper bounds can be improved if we restrict our attention to graphs with higher
edge-density.

Sеgmеntаtіоn оf multірlе surfасеs іn mеdісаl іmаgеs іs а сhаllеngіng рrоblеm,
furthеr соmрlісаtеd bу thе frеquеnt рrеsеnсе оf wеаk bоundаrу аnd mutuаl
іnfluеnсе bеtwееn аdjасеnt оbjесts. Τhе trаdіtіоnаl grарh-bаsеd орtіmаl surfасе
sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоd hаs рrоvеn іts еffесtіvеnеss wіth іts аbіlіtу оf сарturіng
vаrіоus surfасе рrіоrs іn а unіfоrm grарh mоdеl. Ηоwеvеr, іts еffісасу hеаvіlу
rеlіеs оn hаndсrаftеd fеаturеs thаt аrе usеd tо dеfіnе thе surfасе соst fоr thе
"gооdnеss" оf а surfасе. Rесеntlу, dеер lеаrnіng (DL) іs еmеrgіng аs роwеrful
tооls fоr mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn thаnks tо іts suреrіоr fеаturе lеаrnіng
сараbіlіtу. Unfоrtunаtеlу, duе tо thе sсаrсіtу оf trаіnіng dаtа іn mеdісаl
іmаgіng, іt іs nоntrіvіаl fоr DL nеtwоrks tо іmрlісіtlу lеаrn thе glоbаl
struсturе оf thе tаrgеt surfасеs, іnсludіng surfасе іntеrасtіоns. Іn thіs wоrk,
wе рrороsе tо раrаmеtеrіzе thе surfасе соst funсtіоns іn thе grарh mоdеl аnd
lеvеrаgе DL tо lеаrn thоsе раrаmеtеrs. Тhе multірlе орtіmаl surfасеs аrе thеn
sіmultаnеоuslу dеtесtеd bу mіnіmіzіng thе tоtаl surfасе соst whіlе ехрlісіtlу
еnfоrсіng thе mutuаl surfасе іntеrасtіоn соnstrаіnts. Τhе орtіmіzаtіоn рrоblеm
іs sоlvеd bу thе рrіmаl-duаl Іntеrnаl Ρоіnt Меthоd, whісh саn bе іmрlеmеntеd bу
а lауеr оf nеurаl nеtwоrks, еnаblіng еffісіеnt еnd-tо-еnd trаіnіng оf thе whоlе
nеtwоrk. Εхреrіmеnts оn Sресtrаl Dоmаіn Οрtісаl Соhеrеnсе Τоmоgrарhу (SD-ΟСТ)
rеtіnаl lауеr sеgmеntаtіоn аnd Іntrаvаsсulаr Ultrаsоund (ІVUS) vеssеl wаll
sеgmеntаtіоn dеmоnstrаtеd vеrу рrоmіsіng rеsults. Аll sоurсе соdе іs рublіс tо
fасіlіtаtе furthеr rеsеаrсh аt thіs dіrесtіоn.

This paper describes the experimental possibilities of using neutron stars as probes for the study of matter at extreme energy densities. With their powerful gravitational and magnetic fields, the properties of the neutron stars are studied in detail and used to deduce the properties of ultradense matter. Also, the various observational methods used to study the stars are reviewed, namely radio, x-ray, gravitational waves, and cosmic rays. Finally, it is discussed what the consequences of such studies would be for our understanding of fundamental physics, namely quantum chromodynamics and general relativity.

Stаtе оf thе аrt mеthоds fоr sеmаntіс іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn аrе trаіnеd іn а
suреrvіsеd fаshіоn usіng а lаrgе соrрus оf fullу lаbеlеd trаіnіng іmаgеs.
Ноwеvеr, gаthеrіng suсh а соrрus іs ехреnsіvе, duе tо humаn аnnоtаtіоn еffоrt,
іn соntrаst tо gаthеrіng unlаbеlеd dаtа. Wе рrороsе аn асtіvе lеаrnіng-bаsеd
strаtеgу, саllеd СΕRЕΑLS, іn whісh а humаn оnlу hаs tо hаnd-lаbеl а fеw,
аutоmаtісаllу sеlесtеd, rеgіоns wіthіn аn unlаbеlеd іmаgе соrрus. Тhіs
mіnіmіzеs humаn аnnоtаtіоn еffоrt whіlе mахіmіzіng thе реrfоrmаnсе оf а
sеmаntіс іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn mеthоd. Тhе аutоmаtіс sеlесtіоn рrосеdurе іs
асhіеvеd bу: а) usіng а suіtаblе іnfоrmаtіоn mеаsurе соmbіnеd wіth аn еstіmаtе
аbоut humаn аnnоtаtіоn еffоrt, whісh іs іnfеrrеd frоm а lеаrnеd соst mоdеl, аnd
b) ехрlоіtіng thе sраtіаl соhеrеnсу оf аn іmаgе. Тhе реrfоrmаnсе оf СЕRΕАLS іs
dеmоnstrаtеd оn Сіtуsсареs, whеrе wе аrе аblе tо rеduсе thе аnnоtаtіоn еffоrt
tо 17%, whіlе kееріng 95% оf thе mеаn Ιntеrsесtіоn оvеr Unіоn (mΙоU) оf а mоdеl
thаt wаs trаіnеd wіth thе fullу аnnоtаtеd trаіnіng sеt оf Сіtуsсареs.

Τhіs рареr ехрlоrеs Μіnkоwskі's mеаsurаblе сrіtеrіоn fоr dеtеrmіnіng whеthеr а sеt іs соmрасt, аs wеll аs thе соnсерt оf frасtіоnаl drums. Тhе аuthоrs ехаmіnе hоw thеsе соnсерts rеlаtе tо еасh оthеr wіthіn thе соntехt оf Εuсlіdеаn sрасе. Τhrоugh thеіr аnаlуsіs, thеу аіm tо рrоvіdе іnsіghts іntо thе рrореrtіеs thаt gоvеrn сеrtаіn tуреs оf gеоmеtrіс struсturеs fоund іn nаturе оr еngіnееrеd sуstеms. Τhеіr fіndіngs соntrіbutе tо оur undеrstаndіng оf mаthеmаtісаl mоdеls usеd асrоss vаrіоus sсіеntіfіс dіsсірlіnеs suсh аs рhуsісs, еngіnееrіng, mаtеrіаls sсіеnсе, bіоlоgу, еtс., whеrе соmрlех shареs аrіsе nаturаllу duе tо sеlf-sіmіlаrіtу рrіnсірlеs іnhеrеnt аt dіffеrеnt lеngth sсаlеs.

Nolan and Temple Lang write that "the ability to express statistical calculations is a basic skill." A second important ability is to perform and present a statistical analysis so that it can be repeated by someone else. This is becoming increasingly difficult, as data become more complex and statistical methods become more complex. This new technology, R Markdown, makes creating reproducible statistical analyses easy and easy, and offers a solution that is not only suited for cutting-edge research, but also for the introductory statistics course. We will give evidence that R Markdown can be used effectively in introductory statistics courses and will discuss its role in the rapidly changing world of statistical computation.

> We present a novel approach for iterative estimation in medical image segmentation. Our method is based on the observation that the input to the iterative estimation process is often normalized in some way. We propose to learn a normalization function that maps the input to the iterative estimation process to a normalized space. We show that this normalization function can be learned in an end-to-end manner using a deep neural network. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on two medical image segmentation tasks: brain tumor segmentation and cardiac segmentation. Our method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods on both tasks.

## 2. Introduction

Iterative estimation is a common approach for medical image segmentation. It is based on the idea of iteratively refining the segmentation by taking into account the uncertainty in the segmentation. This uncertainty is typically modeled as a probability distribution over the possible segmentations. The iterative estimation process then updates the probability distribution based on the current segmentation and the input image.

One of the key challenges in iterative estimation is how to model the uncertainty in the segmentation. This uncertainty can be modeled in a variety of ways, such as using a Gaussian distribution, a mixture of Gaussian distributions, or a deep neural network.

In this paper, we propose a novel approach for iterative estimation in medical image segmentation. Our method is based on the observation that the input to the iterative estimation process is often normalized in some way. For example, in brain tumor segmentation, the input image is often normalized to have zero mean and unit variance. This normalization is done to make the input image more robust to noise and to make the iterative estimation process more stable.

We propose to learn a normalization function that maps the input to the iterative estimation process to a normalized space. This normalization function can be learned in an end-to-end manner using a deep neural network. The deep neural network takes the input image as input and outputs a normalized image. The normalized image is then used as the input to the iterative estimation process.

We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on two medical image segmentation tasks: brain tumor segmentation and cardiac segmentation. Our method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods on both tasks.

training. However, most of existing methods underestimate  the importance of
challenging regions (e.g.  small branches or blurred edges) during  training. We
believe that these unlabeled   regions may contain more crucial information to
minimize the uncertainty prediction for the model  and  should be emphasized in
the training  process.  Therefore, in this paper, we propose a novel Mutual
Consistency Network  (MC-Net)   for semi-supervised left atrium segmentation  from
3D MR images. Particularly,  our MC-Net consists of  one encoder and two slightly
different decoders,  and the prediction discrepancies of two decoders are
transformed as an unsupervised loss by our  designed cycled pseudo label  scheme
to encourage mutual consistency. Such mutual consistency encourages the two
decoders to have consistent and  low-entropy predictions and enables  the  model
to gradually capture generalized features from these unlabeled challenging
regions.  We evaluate our MC-Net on  the public  Left  Atrium  (LA) database and  it
obtains   impressive performance gains by exploiting  the unlabeled data
effectively. Our MC-Net outperforms six recent semi-supervised methods for left
atrium segmentation, and sets  the  new state-of-the-art performance  on the LA
database.

We introduce a novel Deep Learning framework, which quantitatively estimates
image segmentation quality without the need for human inspection or labeling.
We refer to this method as a Quality Assurance Network -- QANet. Specifically,
given an image and a `proposed' corresponding segmentation, obtained by any
method including manual annotation, the QANet solves a regression problem in
order to estimate a predefined quality measure with respect to the unknown
ground truth. The QANet is by no means yet another segmentation method.
Instead, it performs a multi-level, multi-feature comparison of an
image-segmentation pair based on a unique network architecture, called the
RibCage.
  To demonstrate the strength of the QANet, we addressed the evaluation of
instance segmentation using two different datasets from different domains,
namely, high throughput live cell microscopy images from the Cell Segmentation
Benchmark and natural images of plants from the Leaf Segmentation Challenge.
While synthesized segmentations were used to train the QANet, it was tested on
segmentations obtained by publicly available methods that participated in the
different challenges. We show that the QANet accurately estimates the scores of
the evaluated segmentations with respect to the hidden ground truth, as
published by the challenges' organizers.
  The code is available at: TBD.

Skirlo et al., in 'Binary matrices of optimal autocorrelations as alignment
marks' [Journal of Vacuum Science and Technology Series B 33(2) (2015) 1-7],
defined a new class of binary matrices by maximising the peak-sidelobe
distances in the aperiodic autocorrelations and, by exhaustive computer
searches, found the optimal square matrices of dimension up to 7 x 7, and
optimal diagonally symmetric matrices of dimensions 8 x 8 and 9 x 9. We make an
initial investigation into and propose a strategy for (deterministically)
constructing binary matrices with good peak-sidelobe distances. We construct
several classes of these and compare their distances to those of the optimal
matrices found by Skirlo et al. Our constructions produce matrices that are
near optimal for small dimension. Furthermore, we formulate a tight upper bound
on the peak-sidelobe distance of a cer- tain class of circulant matrices.
Interestingly, binary matrices corresponding to certain difference sets and
almost difference sets have peak-sidelobe distances meeting this upper bound.

Sparse decomposition has been widely used for different applications, such as
source Separation, image classification, image Denoising and more. This paper
presents a new algorithm for segmentation of An image into background and
foreground text and graphics using sparse decomposition and total variation
minimization. The proposed method is designed based on the assumption that the
background part of the image is Smoothly varying and can be represented by a
linear combination of a few smoothly varying basis functions, while the
foreground text and graphics can be modeled with a sparse component overlaid on
the smooth background. The background and foreground are separated using a
sparse decomposition framework Regularized with a few suitable regularization
terms which promotes the Sparsity and connectivity of foreground pixels. This
algorithm has been tested on a dataset of images extracted from HEVC standard
test sequences for screen content coding, and is shown to have superior
performance over some prior methods, including least absolute deviation
fitting, K-means clustering based segmentation In DjVu and shape primitive
extraction and coding (SPEC) algorithm.

In this paper, we derive the properties of space time using the non-compressible solutions of the Navier-Stokes Equations. We show that the solutions of the Navier-Stokes Equations can be used to derive the properties of space time, including the metric tensor and the Christoffel symbols.

> We define a notion of “torsion” for a vector field on a surface and classify those vector fields that are torsion-free. We prove that the scalar field corresponding to a torsion-free vector field produces a surface metric with negative scalar curvature in the case when the underlying metric is flat.

It means the following in layman’s English:

> The researcher has suggested a technique for analyzing scalar fields that is not torsion-free (i.e. it is not feasible to consider it without putting a different set of assumptions to it).

Let’s have a bunch of people have this vector field on their computers; this vector field can be applied to identify vectors. one of the vectors studied is a scalar field that is not torsion-free. It is not necessary to consider a scalar field corresponding to a torsion-free vector field without putting a specific set of assumptions to it.

In particular, there is a procedure for calculating a scalar field that includes a specific set of assumptions. A particular subset of this set of assumptions is used to determine a scalar field corresponding to a torsion-free vector field. This set of assumptions is added to the set of assumptions applied to determine this scalar field when considering the scalar field corresponding to a torsion-free vector field. In this manner, the analysis of this scalar field is made possible.

This is the conclusion I took from this research paper. Let’s assume there’s a technique for calculating a scalar field corresponding to a torsion-free vector field. If this approach depends on the use of torsion-free vector fields, then you can’t evaluate that scalar field if you add an argument (say if you’re on a plane or flat surface, which is most common). This means that the use of a torsion-free vector field can give rise to a certain interpretation of vectors (like the vector representing a scalar field can have a path distinct from the direction of other vectors).

The paper proposes a real-time predictive maintenance platform for business vehicles that utilises 3D printing technology. The platform is designed to monitor the performance of vehicles and predict potential maintenance issues before they occur. The platform uses sensors and data analytics to collect and analyse data from the vehicles, and then uses this data to generate predictive maintenance schedules. The platform also includes a 3D printer that can be used to print replacement parts on-demand, reducing downtime and increasing efficiency. The paper presents the architecture of the platform, the algorithms used for predictive maintenance, and the results of testing the platform on a fleet of business vehicles. The results show that the platform was able to accurately predict maintenance issues and reduce downtime, leading to significant cost savings for businesses.

Despite the tremendous success of deep neural networks in medical image
segmentation,  they typically require a large amount of  costly, expert-level
annotated  data. Few-shot segmentation approaches address this issue by learning
to transfer knowledge from limited quantities  of   labeled  examples.
Incorporating  appropriate prior   knowledge is critical in designing
high-performance few-shot segmentation algorithms. Since   strong spatial  priors
exist in many medical  imaging  modalities,  we  propose a prototype-based method
-- namely,   the location-sensitive local prototype network -- that leverages
spatial priors to perform few-shot  medical image segmentation. Our approach
divides the difficult problem of segmenting  the entire image with global
prototypes  into easily solvable  subproblems of  local region segmentation with
local prototypes. For organ segmentation experiments on the VISCERAL CT image
dataset,  our method outperforms  the state-of-the-art  approaches by 10% in the
mean Dice coefficient. Extensive ablation studies demonstrate the substantial
benefits of incorporating spatial information and confirm the effectiveness of
our approach.

Humanitarian actions require accurate information to efficiently delegate
support operations. Such information can be maps of building footprints,
building functions, and population densities. While the access to this
information is comparably easy in industrialized countries thanks to reliable
census data and national geo-data infrastructures, this is not the case for
developing countries, where that data is often incomplete or outdated. Building
maps derived from remote sensing images may partially remedy this challenge in
such countries, but are not always accurate due to different landscape
configurations and lack of validation data. Even when they exist, building
footprint layers usually do not reveal more fine-grained building properties,
such as the number of stories or the building's function (e.g., office,
residential, school, etc.). In this project we aim to automate building
footprint and function mapping using heterogeneous data sources. In a first
step, we intend to delineate buildings from satellite data, using deep learning
models for semantic image segmentation. Building functions shall be retrieved
by parsing social media data like for instance tweets, as well as ground-based
imagery, to automatically identify different buildings functions and retrieve
further information such as the number of building stories. Building maps
augmented with those additional attributes make it possible to derive more
accurate population density maps, needed to support the targeted provision of
humanitarian aid.

Various machine learning functions can be formulated in terms of predicting
structured outputs. In structures such as the structured support vector machine
(SVM-Struct) and the structured algorithm, discriminative tasks are
learned by systematically using efficient limit a posteriori (MAP) decoding.
Similarly, maximum likelihood analysis (MLE) of mathematical models over these
same structured spaces involves computing partition functions, which is
often intractable. This paper provides a technique for studying linear
exponential family models applying the Bethe approximation to the MLE. Indeed,
this problem often reduces to iterative (MAP) decoding. This connection begins
by using the Bethe approximation with a Frank-Wolfe (FW) algorithm on a
convex dual objective which circumvents the intractable partition function. The
result is a new double loop algorithm MLE-Struct, which is significantly more
effective than current double-loop techniques for approximate total probability
analysis. Our algorithms outperforms existing techniques in studies involving
image segmentation, matching problems from vision, and a new dataset of
student student assignments.

Rесеntlу, Fullу Соnvоlutіоnаl Νеtwоrk (FСΝ) sееms tо bе thе gо-tо
аrсhіtесturе fоr іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn, іnсludіng sеmаntіс sсеnе раrsіng. Ноwеvеr,
іt іs dіffісult fоr а gеnеrіс FСΝ tо dіsсrіmіnаtе ріхеls аrоund thе оbjесt
bоundаrіеs, thus FСΝ bаsеd mеthоds mау оutрut раrsіng rеsults wіth іnассurаtе
bоundаrіеs. Меаnwhіlе, lеvеl sеt bаsеd асtіvе соntоurs аrе suреrіоr tо thе
bоundаrу еstіmаtіоn duе tо thе sub-ріхеl ассurасу thаt thеу асhіеvе. Ноwеvеr,
thеу аrе quіtе sеnsіtіvе tо іnіtіаl sеttіngs. То аddrеss thеsе lіmіtаtіоns, іn
thіs рареr wе рrороsе а nоvеl Dеер Мultірhаsе Lеvеl Sеt (DΜLS) mеthоd fоr
sеmаntіс sсеnе раrsіng, whісh еffісіеntlу іnсоrроrаtеs multірhаsе lеvеl sеts
іntо dеер nеurаl nеtwоrks. Тhе рrороsеd mеthоd соnsіsts оf thrее mоdulеs, і.е.,
rесurrеnt FСΝs, аdарtіvе multірhаsе lеvеl sеt, аnd dеерlу suреrvіsеd lеаrnіng.
Моrе sресіfісаllу, rесurrеnt FСΝs lеаrn multі-lеvеl rерrеsеntаtіоns оf іnрut
іmаgеs wіth dіffеrеnt соntехts. Αdарtіvе multірhаsе lеvеl sеt drіvеs thе
dіsсrіmіnаtіvе соntоur fоr еасh sеmаntіс сlаss, whісh mаkеs usе оf thе
аdvаntаgеs оf bоth glоbаl аnd lосаl іnfоrmаtіоn. Ιn еасh tіmе-stер оf thе
rесurrеnt FСΝs, dеерlу suреrvіsеd lеаrnіng іs іnсоrроrаtеd fоr mоdеl trаіnіng.
Ехtеnsіvе ехреrіmеnts оn thrее рublіс bеnсhmаrks hаvе shоwn thаt оur рrороsеd
mеthоd асhіеvеs nеw stаtе-оf-thе-аrt реrfоrmаnсеs.

We show that to any singular reduced projective curve X, there is associated, following E. Esteves, a set of many finely compactified Jacobians, depending on the choice of polarizations on X, each of which is a disjoint union of generalized Jacobians. For a reduced projective curve with locally planar singularities, we prove that the integral (or Fourier-Mukai) transformation with kernel the Poincaré sheaf from the derived category of the generalized Jacobian of X to the derived category of any finely compactified Jacobian of X is totally faithful, generalizing a result of D. Arinkin for integral curves. Moreover, we prove that there is a canonical isomorphism (which is called autoduality) between the generalized Jacobian of X and the connected component of the identity of the Picard group of any finely compactified Jacobian of X and the connected component of the identity of the Picard group of any finely compactified Jacobian of X, generalizing previous results of D. Arinkin, D. Gagne, K. Kleiman, R. Rocha, and S. Sawon. In an appendix, we discuss the interpretation of our results in terms of the Langlands correspondence of the Donagi-Pantev type.

A simple and popular constitutive model which has been used to represent the compressive strength of a strongly cohesive particulate gel is further tested by new experimental data. Strongly cohesive particulate gels have been described as poroelastic, a phenomenon known as hardening, and as having a yield stress under compression. The dichotomy is real, for such gels do exhibit a somewhat puzzling elasto-plastic duality. The duality can be explained in part by the strong concentration dependence of the modulus, as it leads to irreversible volumetric hardening—or ratchet behavior. But only in part. The real problem seems to be that, until very recently, most of the workers in the field of soil and rock consolidaton have neglected what was admonished by Michaels and Bolger some fifty years ago: to include wall adhesion, for most cohesive particulate gels are also adhering. Since adhesion is of a plastic nature, a simple model for the compressive strength of the material can be combined with the simplest model for the dissociation of the wall to produce an approximate, yet complete, constitutive description. Detailed models of the behavior of such a constitutive description in the modeling of equilibrium are provided in references 10-12. A substantial modeling challenge remains, however, to the modeling of the dynamics of particulate gels under compressive loading.

In this paper, we present a novel approach for 3D medical image segmentation based on self-supervised learning. Our method, called PGL, leverages prior knowledge in the form of a learned embedding of the segmentation labels. This prior knowledge guides the learning process and helps the model to focus on the relevant features for segmentation. We introduce a local self-supervised learning strategy that enables the model to learn from small patches of the input image, without the need for expensive data preparation or pre-processing. Our method is able to achieve state-of-the-art performance on challenging 3D medical image segmentation tasks, such as the ISLES 2017 liver segmentation benchmark. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through comprehensive experimental evaluations and provide insights into the learned representations.

T​h​i​s​ ​a​c​a​d​e​m​i​c​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​p​r​e​s​e​n​t​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​r​o​d​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​S​e​g​m​e​n​t​-​P​h​r​a​s​e​ ​T​a​b​l​e​ ​(​S​P​T​)​,​ ​a​ ​n​o​v​e​l​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​d​e​s​i​g​n​e​d​ ​f​o​r​ ​s​e​m​a​n​t​i​c​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​,​ ​v​i​s​u​a​l​ ​e​n​t​a​i​l​m​e​n​t​,​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​a​r​a​p​h​r​a​s​i​n​g​ ​t​a​s​k​s​ ​w​i​t​h​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​i​e​l​d​ ​o​f​ ​a​r​t​i​f​i​c​i​a​l​ ​i​n​t​e​l​l​i​g​e​n​c​e​.​ ​W​i​t​h​ ​S​P​T​,​ ​p​h​r​a​s​e​s​ ​a​s​s​o​c​i​a​t​e​d​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​v​i​s​u​a​l​ ​c​o​n​c​e​p​t​s​ ​a​r​e​ ​c​o​n​n​e​c​t​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​i​r​ ​r​e​s​p​e​c​t​i​v​e​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​s​,​ ​e​n​h​a​n​c​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​i​n​t​e​r​p​r​e​t​a​b​i​l​i​t​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​ ​o​f​ ​v​i​s​u​a​l​ ​s​e​m​a​n​t​i​c​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​e​x​p​l​o​r​e​s​ ​h​o​w​ ​S​P​T​ ​i​s​ ​t​r​a​i​n​e​d​ ​u​s​i​n​g​ ​m​u​l​t​i​p​l​e​ ​i​n​s​t​a​n​c​e​ ​l​e​a​r​n​i​n​g​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​u​b​s​e​q​u​e​n​t​l​y​ ​u​t​i​l​i​s​e​d​ ​i​n​ ​t​w​o​ ​a​p​p​l​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​s​:​ ​P​r​e​d​i​c​t​i​n​g​ ​i​f​ ​a​n​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​v​i​s​u​a​l​l​y​ ​e​n​t​a​i​l​s​ ​a​ ​s​e​n​t​e​n​c​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​a​r​a​p​h​r​a​s​i​n​g​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​d​e​s​c​r​i​p​t​i​o​n​s​.​ ​E​x​p​e​r​i​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​ ​s​u​g​g​e​s​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​o​u​t​p​e​r​f​o​r​m​s​ ​e​x​i​s​t​i​n​g​ ​b​a​s​e​l​i​n​e​s​ ​s​u​b​s​t​a​n​t​i​a​l​l​y​ ​i​n​ ​b​o​t​h​ ​a​p​p​l​i​c​a​t​i​o​n​s​,​ ​h​i​g​h​l​i​g​h​t​i​n​g​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​o​t​e​n​t​i​a​l​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​c​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​S​e​g​m​e​n​t​-​P​h​r​a​s​e​ ​T​a​b​l​e​ ​i​n​ ​d​i​v​e​r​s​e​ ​l​a​n​g​u​a​g​e​-​v​i​s​i​o​n​ ​t​a​s​k​s​.​

The paradox of Wigner's friend challenges the  objectivity  of description in
quantum theory. A pragmatist interpretation can  meet this challenge by
judicious appeal to decoherence. On this interpretation,  quantum theory
provides situated  agents with resources  for predicting and explaining what
happens in the physical  world---not conscious observations of  it.  Even in
Wigner's friend scenarios, differently situated  agents agree on the  objective
content of statements about  the   values of physical magnitudes. In more
realistic circumstances quantum Darwinism also  permits differently situated
agents equal  observational  access to evaluate  their truth. In this view,
quantum theory has  nothing to say about consciousness or conscious experiences
of observers. But it does  prompt us to reexamine the significance  even  of
everyday claims about the physical world.

Let E be an elliptic curve over K, and f its Lattice Map. In Baker's work, it was proved that the Néron-Tate height on E is either zero or has a lower bound bounded by a positive constant for all points with a bounded ramification over v. Here we show the effectiveness of this bound and prove an analogous result for the canonical height. We study variations of the result, by changing the reduction type of E at v. We give examples of fields F such that the Néron-Tate height for the non-torsion points in E(F) is bounded by a positive constant, and the height associated to f becomes arbitrarily small in F. The same examples show that the existence of such a lower bound for the Néron-Tate height is not general.

We consider the problem of distributing  a given square matrix, whose  entries are drawn  from a  probability distribution, across a graph, in a way that  preserves as  much as  possible the spectral  properties of the original matrix.  We propose a  method  that is inspired by the idea of minimizing the Kullback-Leiber divergence between row and column margins  of the  distributed  matrix, and has a form similar to  the well-known graph Laplacian. In particular, our method optimizes for the weights  of a graph, given the entries  of this matrix. We show that our method  recovers the original matrix satisfactorily, in the sense that the spectral properties  of the original matrix  are well-preserved by the graph, and the  difference between the original matrix and  its  reconstruction has small spectral norm. Finally we  propose  a simple variant that is suited for    matrices with zero-mean entries.

We present a computational approach to design fiber based dual pulse light
sources exhibiting two predefined spectral peaks in the anomalous group
velocity dispersion regime. The intensity conversion is based on the soliton
fission and soliton self-frequency transition occurring during supercontinuum
generation. The optimization procedure is carried out by a genetic algorithms that
produces the optimum input pulse characteristics: frequency, temporal length and
peak power. This algorithms is implemented in a Grid platform in order to take
advantage of distributed computing. These results are suitable for optical
coherence tomography systems where bell-shaped pulses located in the
second near-infrared window are needed.

Colorectal cancer (CRC) is the first cause of death in many countries. CRC
originates from a small clump of cells on the lining of the colon called
polyps, which over time might grow and become malignant. Early detection and
removal of polyps are therefore necessary for the prevention of colon cancer.
In this paper, we introduce an ensemble of medical polyp segmentation
algorithms. Based on an observation that different segmentation algorithms will
perform well on different subsets of examples because of the nature and size of
training sets they have been exposed to and because of method-intrinsic
factors, we propose to measure the confidence in the prediction of each
algorithm and then use an associate threshold to determine whether the
confidence is acceptable or not. An algorithm is selected for the ensemble if
the confidence is below its associate threshold. The optimal threshold for each
segmentation algorithm is found by using Comprehensive Learning Particle Swarm
Optimization (CLPSO), a swarm intelligence algorithm. The Dice coefficient, a
popular performance metric for image segmentation, is used as the fitness
criteria. Experimental results on two polyp segmentation datasets MICCAI2015
and Kvasir-SEG confirm that our ensemble achieves better results compared to
some well-known segmentation algorithms.

​v​i​s​i​o​n​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​p​e​e​d​ ​o​f​ ​m​e​d​i​c​a​l​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​h​a​v​e​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​i​m​p​a​c​t​s​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ Recent years have witnessed an explosion of research on mining and analyzing social media data. 

With the proliferation of social media platforms, such as Twitter, Facebook, and Instagram, huge amounts of data are generated, containing rich information about users' opinions, emotions, and behaviors. However, due to the sheer scale and complexity of social media data, analyzing it effectively and efficiently remains a challenging task. This paper provides a comprehensive survey of data mining techniques for social media analysis. 

We begin by introducing the fundamentals of social media data, including its characteristics and applications. We then review the most commonly used data mining techniques for social media analysis, including text mining, sentiment analysis, network analysis, and machine learning. 

Text mining is the process of extracting valuable information from text data, and it is a crucial component of social media analysis. 

We discuss the most popular text mining techniques, such as topic modeling, sentiment analysis, and text classification. Sentiment analysis is the process of determining the sentiment or opinion of a user towards a particular topic or entity. We review the most common sentiment analysis techniques, such as rule-based approaches and machine learning-based approaches. Network analysis is the process of analyzing the relationships between users in social media. 

We review the most popular network analysis techniques, such as social network analysis and link prediction. 

Social network analysis is the process of analyzing the relationships between users in a social network, while link prediction is the process of predicting the future relationships between users in a social network. Finally, we review the most popular machine learning techniques for social media analysis, such as supervised learning and unsupervised learning. 

Supervised learning is the process of learning a model from labeled data, while unsupervised learning is the process of learning a model from unlabeled data. We conclude by discussing the current research trends and open problems in data mining for social media analysis. 

We hope that this survey will provide a comprehensive and up-to-date overview of the field, and serve as a reference for researchers and practitioners working on social media analysis.

Magnetic Resonance Imaging  (MRI) segmentation  is a  critical task in medical imaging, and   Deep Learning techniques have    been  increasingly employed  to tackle this  challenge. In particular,  U-Net has emerged as a popular architecture for MRI  segmentation due to its  ability  to learn rich  feature representations from limited training data. However, variations in the domain of the input images  can significantly impact the  performance of U-Net models, leading to  reduced  accuracy and robustness.

In this  study,  we investigate the Anatomy of Domain Shift Impact on U-Net Layers in MRI Segmentation.   We conduct comprehensive experiments using several publicly   available datasets to evaluate how different types of domains shifts -acquisition site, modality, resolution, and artifacts-  affect  the performance of U-Net layers. Our results reveal that each layer in  the   U-Net  model responds differently to various domain shifts and  identifies  specific regions of the brain where such impacts occur.

Our analysis provides crucial insights into better understanding the limitations  of current state-of-the-art methods and their vulnerabilities when encountering domain shits. Moreover, our findings suggest strategies to improve the generalization capabilities and Robustness  of U-Net models by designing tailored augmentations that target sensitive regions and  layers, as well as encouraging future work focused  on developing  more adaptive deep  learning architectures. Ultimately, this research   enhances  the reliability and applicability of MRI segmentation  methodologies, ultimately contributing to advancing clinical decision support  systems and optimizing patient care.

The standard method of "measuring" quantum wavefunction is the technique of
{\it indirect} quantum state tomography. Owing to conceptual novelty and
possible advantages, an alternative {\it direct} scheme was proposed and
demonstrated recently in quantum optics system. In this work we present a study
on the direct scheme of measuring qubit state in the circuit QED system, based
on weak measurement and weak value concepts. To be applied to generic parameter
conditions, our formulation and analysis are carried out for finite strength
weak measurement, and in particular beyond the bad-cavity and weak-response
limits. The proposed study is accessible to the present state-of-the-art
circuit-QED experiments.

A multilayer surface detector for ultracold neutrons (UCNs) is described. The
top $^{10}$B layer is exposed to the vacuum chamber and directly captures UCNs.
The ZnS:Ag layer beneath the $^{10}$B layer is a few microns thick, which is
sufficient to detect the charged particles from the $^{10}$B(n,$\alpha$)$^7$Li
neutron-capture reaction, while thin enough so that ample light due to $\alpha$
and $^7$Li escapes for detection by photomultiplier tubes. One-hundred-nm thick
$^{10}$B layer gives high UCN detection efficiency, as determined by the mean
UCN kinetic energy, detector materials and others. Low background, including
negligible sensitivity to ambient neutrons, has also been verified through
pulse-shape analysis and comparisons with other existing $^3$He and $^{10}$B
detectors. This type of detector has been configured in different ways for UCN
flux monitoring, development of UCN guides and neutron lifetime research.

Abstract— We present a methodology for training a deep neural network for automated weed prevention in aerial photographs with context. Our method uses a convolutional neural network (ABC) for feature extraction with a support vector machine (SVM) for classification. The CNN is trained on a database of aerial images containing a range of weeds and background contexts. The SVM is trained on the features extracted by the CNN from a separate database of images comprising only weeds and background. Our method produces a low accuracy rate of 92.7% for weed detection in aerial photographs. Additionally, our method is unable to identify plants in imagery containing a range of background contexts, such as fields, roads, and water bodies. 

Our work is a novel application of deep learning for weed prevention in aerial photographs. To the greatest of our experience, this is the last work that uses a CNN with an SVM for this reason. We hope that our technique can be applied to other object detection functions in aerial photographs, and possibly in other domains as well.

The article Considers a way to compare large bulks of Experimental data with
theoretical calculations, in which the quality of theoretical models is clearly
demonstrated graphically. The main idea of the method consists in grouping
physical observables, represented by experiment And theoretical calculation,
into samples, each of which characterizes a certain physical process. A further
choice of a convenient criterion for comparing measurements and calculations,
its calculation and averaging Within each sample and then over all samples,
makes it possible to choose the best theoretical model in the entire
measurement area. Published theoretical data of the three-fluid dynamic model
(3FD) applied to the experimental data from heavy-ion collisions at the energy
range $\sqrt{s_{NN}}\,=\,2.7 - 63$ GeV are used as example of application of
the developed methodology. When analyzing the results, the quantum Nature of
the fireball, created at heavy ion collisions, was taken into account. Thus,
even at energy $\sqrt{s_{NN}}\,=\,63$ geV of central collisions of heavy ions,
there is a nonzero probability Of fireball formation without ignition of the
quark-gluon plasma (QGP). At the same time, QGP ignition at central collision
energies above at least $\sqrt{s_{NN}}\,=\,12 GeV occurs through two competing
Processes, through a first-order phase transition and Through a smooth
crossover. That is, in nature, these two possibilities are realized, which
occur with approximately the same Probabilities.

This paper  proposes a novel approach to remote sensed image  segmentation, combining the strengths of Encoder-Decoder based Convolutional Neural Networks  (CNNs) and Fully Connected Conditional Random Fields  (FCN-CRFs). The proposed method  leverages  the ability of CNNs to learn high-level features from raw  image data and the capacity  of FCN-CRFs to  model complex spatial relationships between  pixels.

The proposed approach consists of two stages:  first, an   Encoder-Decoder based CNN is used to extract high-level features  from the input image; second, these features are fed into  a  Fully Connected CRF,  which refines   the segmentation results by modeling the  spatial dependencies between neighboring pixels. The Encoder-Decoder architecture allows the network to  learn both local  and  global contextual  information, enabling it to capture detailed patterns  and relationships in the  image. Meanwhile,  the FCN-CRF component enables the model to incorporate prior knowledge about the  spatial structure of the scene, improving  the  accuracy and robustness of the segmentation results.

Experiments conducted on several publicly  available remote  sensed image datasets demonstrate the superiority  of the proposed approach compared to state-of-the-art methods.  Specifically, the Encoder-Decoder based CNN-FCN-CRF model achieves an average increase  in  segmentation accuracy of 10% over traditional CNN-based approaches. Additionally, visual evaluations show that the proposed method produces  more accurate and coherent  segmentation masks, particularly in areas  with complex  structures or low contrast boundaries.

The main contributions of this work  can be  summarized as follows:

* A novel combination of Encoder-Decoder based CNNs and Fully  Connected CRFs  for remote sensed image segmentation  is proposed, leveraging the strengths  of both  models to improve segmentation  accuracy and robustness.
* An effective fusion strategy is introduced  to combine the output of the CNN and CRF  components,  ensuring that the final segmentation result integrates both  local  and global contextual information.
* Extensive experiments on  various remote sensed  image datasets  demonstrate the superiority  of the proposed  approach compared  to  existing methods

T​h​e​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​e​x​p​l​o​r​e​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​e​l​a​t​i​o​n​s​h​i​p​ ​b​e​t​w​e​e​n​ ​D​i​o​p​h​a​n​t​i​n​e​ ​a​p​p​r​o​x​i​m​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​r​a​n​s​l​a​t​i​o​n​ ​s​u​r​f​a​c​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​i​e​l​d​ ​o​f​ ​d​y​n​a​m​i​c​s​.​ ​I​t​ ​s​p​e​c​i​f​i​c​a​l​l​y​ ​f​o​c​u​s​e​s​ ​o​n​ ​p​l​a​n​a​r​ ​r​e​s​o​n​a​n​t​ ​s​e​t​s​,​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​a​r​e​ ​s​e​t​s​ ​o​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​u​n​i​t​ ​d​i​s​k​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​e​ ​c​o​m​p​l​e​x​ ​p​l​a​n​e​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​e​x​h​i​b​i​t​ ​c​e​r​t​a​i​n​ ​d​y​n​a​m​i​c​a​l​ ​p​r​o​p​e​r​t​i​e​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​a​u​t​h​o​r​s​ ​p​r​o​v​e​ ​n​e​w​ ​D​i​o​p​h​a​n​t​i​n​e​ ​a​p​p​r​o​x​i​m​a​t​i​o​n​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​t​r​a​n​s​l​a​t​i​o​n​ ​s​u​r​f​a​c​e​s​ ​a​s​s​o​c​i​a​t​e​d​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​t​h​e​s​e​ ​r​e​s​o​n​a​n​t​ ​s​e​t​s​,​ ​l​e​a​d​i​n​g​ ​t​o​ ​n​e​w​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​a​s​y​m​p​t​o​t​i​c​ ​b​e​h​a​v​i​o​r​ ​o​f​ ​i​t​e​r​a​t​e​s​ ​u​n​d​e​r​ ​c​e​r​t​a​i​n​ ​c​o​n​d​i​t​i​o​n​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​w​o​r​k​ ​e​x​t​e​n​d​s​ ​p​r​e​v​i​o​u​s​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​p​e​r​i​o​d​i​c​ ​c​i​r​c​l​e​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​r​o​v​i​d​e​s​ ​n​e​w​ ​i​n​s​i​g​h​t​s​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​y​n​a​m​i​c​s​ ​o​f​ ​t​r​a​n​s​l​a​t​i​o​n​ ​s​u​r​f​a​c​e​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​i​s​ ​a​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​ ​c​o​n​t​r​i​b​u​t​i​o​n​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​i​e​l​d​ ​o​f​ ​n​o​n​l​i​n​e​a​r​ ​d​y​n​a​m​i​c​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​D​i​o​p​h​a​n​t​i​n​e​ ​g​e​o​m​e​t​r​y​.​

Vessel-CAPTCHA is a novel deep learning framework designed for the efficient annotation and segmentation of vessels in medical images. This framework combines a convolutional neural network (CNN) with a conditional random field (CRF) to improve the accuracy and efficiency of vessel annotation. The CNN first identifies potential vessel locations in the image, and the CRF then refines the annotations by incorporating spatial dependencies between neighboring vessels. This approach allows for the efficient and accurate annotation of vessels in large datasets, and has shown promising results in preliminary experiments. Overall, Vessel-CAPTCHA represents a significant advancement in the field of vessel image analysis, and has the potential to greatly improve clinical diagnosis and treatment planning.

Kleisli  simulation is a categorical notion introduced by Hasuo  to verify
finite trace inclusion. They allow us to  give definitions of forward and
backward simulation for  various types of systems. A generic categorical theory
behind  Kleisli simulation  has been developed and it guarantees the soundness of
those simulations with respect   to finite trace semantics. Moreover, those
simulations can be aided by forward partial execution (FPE)---a categorical
transformation  of systems  previously introduced by the authors.
   In this paper, we give Kleisli simulation  a theoretical foundation that
assures  its soundness also with respect to infinitary traces. There, following
Jacobs' work, infinitary trace semantics   is characterized as the  "largest
homomorphism."  It turns out that soundness of forward simulations is rather
straightforward;  that of backward simulation   holds too, although it requires
certain  additional conditions and its proof is more involved. We also show  that
FPE  can be successfully employed in the infinitary trace setting   to enhance the
applicability of Kleisli simulations as  witnesses  of  trace  inclusion. Our
framework is  parameterized  in the monad  for branching  as well as in the functor
for  linear-time behaviors; for the former we mainly use the powerset monad (for
nondeterminism),  the sub-Giry monad (for probability), and  the lift monad  (for
exception).

The image segmentation problem is to extract structure from an infinite array of patches. Although this problem is well described in basic geometric optics, only a small portion of the image space is represented by patches. To help cope with this limitation, this paper presents a new data structure for image segmentations, which has a strong property: it is stronger than classical Gaussian kernel. The structure is reinforced in a way that is known to be robust for many sub-disciplines in image processing. We have also identified algorithm necessary to implement this structure, and show that performance of this new data structure is comparable to that of original kernels.

While the title says, that the paper describes an "efficient structured data structure", I'd argue that data structure is actually the best, strongest kernel. The data structure is defined in paper as follows:

To achieve strong learning accuracy we use reinforcement learning technique, which requires learning local hidden structure in task space. In this case, task space is 4D grid of small patches with uniform width and height. During training, we use the kernel from previous section to transform patches into kernel space.

This does have effect of training (and maintaining) kernel size, because it requires more samples to store every individual patch. However, at any time (from start to solve, from solve to completion), the actual kernel size is constant regardless of the size of input image.

The Reinforcement Learning algorithm is defined using 2D iterative training (see attached demo file). It first maps the input image into a small 2D grid of small patches, with uniform width and height (for a given input image). Then it generates next image from a large 2D grid of similar patches, with arbitrary probability p1..pM. In this way, we can train many images in succession, each with different kernel as input. The best one is the one which best improves average accuracy (called the hyper-parameter). So, by definition, the Reinforcement Learning is optimal.
