This paper introduces a novel approach to learning affinity, a critical component in various computer vision tasks, through Spatial Propagation Networks (SPN). The proposed method leverages the spatially varying guidance to propagate information throughout an image, thereby learning the affinity effectively. 

The SPN is designed to be flexible and can be easily integrated into various tasks such as semantic segmentation, edge detection, and depth estimation. 

Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing techniques in terms of accuracy and efficiency. The paper also provides insights into the propagation process, contributing to a better understanding of how SPN learns and refines affinity.

Multi-modal learning is typically performed with network architectures
containing modality-specific layers and shared layers, utilizing co-registered
images of different modalities. We propose a novel learning scheme for unpaired
cross-modality image segmentation, with a highly compact architecture achieving
superior segmentation accuracy. In our method, we heavily reuse network
parameters, by sharing all convolutional kernels across CT and MRI, and only
employ modality-specific internal normalization layers which compute respective
statistics. To effectively train such a highly compact model, we introduce a
novel loss term inspired by knowledge distillation, by explicitly constraining
the KL-divergence of our derived prediction distributions between modalities.
We have extensively validated our approach on two multi-class segmentation
problems: i) cardiac structure segmentation, and ii) abdominal organ
segmentation. Different network settings, i.e., 2D dilated network and 3D
U-net, are utilized to investigate our method's general efficacy. Experimental
results on both tasks demonstrate that our novel multi-modal learning scheme
consistently outperforms single-modal training and previous multi-modal
approaches.

Here, we study noisy transitional flows in imperfect millimeter-scale
networks. For examining the flows, we use microcantilever cameras embedded in the
channel walls. We perform tests in three essentially different networks. The
same set of imperfections in the three channels result in two random flows
in which high-order moments of near-wall fluctuations differ by orders of
magnitude. Even however, the highest order statistics in both situations
appear qualitatively different and can be explained by a proposed noise Landau
equation for a slow mode. The noise, regardless of its origin, reduces the
Landau singularity of the relaxation period and makes transitions driven by
other sound sources seem different.

We report on synthesis and anisotropic physical properties of URhIn$_{5}$. High quality single crystals were grown in In-flux. 

The compound undergoes a
second order phase transition into an antiferromagnetic state at
T$_{\textrm{N}}$ = 98 K. The transition is field independent up to 9 T. An
increase of the resistivity $\rho$ with j along the [100], [110] and [001]
tetragonal axis indicates a spin-density-wave induced order with the gap
opening first along the [001] direction. The magnetic susceptibility $\chi$ =
M/H exhibits a strong anisotropy. 

Above T $=$ 200 K, $\chi$(T) follows
Curie-Weiss law with the effective moment of $\mu_{\textrm{eff}}$ = 3.71
$\mu_{\textrm{B}}/$U and the Weiss temperatures of $\theta_{\textrm{P}}^{[100]}
= -900$ K and $\theta_{\textrm{P}}^{[001]} = -500$ K for H $\parallel$ [100]
and H $\parallel$ [001] respectively. The characteristic Kondo-like temperature
for URhIn$_{5}$ yields T$_{\textrm{K}} =$ 125 K.

Environmental Microorganism Data Set Fifth Version (EMDS-5) is a microscopic
image dataset including original Environmental Microorganism (EM) images and
two sets of Ground Truth (GT) images. The GT image sets include a single-object
GT image set and a multi-object GT image set. The EMDS-4 dataset has 89 types
of EMs, each of which contains 20 original EM images, 20 single-object GT
images and 20 multi-object GT images. EMDS-3 can realize to evaluate image
preprocessing, image segmentation, feature extraction, image classification and
image retrieval functions. In order to prove the effectiveness of EMDS-5, for
each function, we select the most representative algorithms and price
indicators for testing and evaluation. The image preprocessing functions
contain two parts: image denoising and image edge detection. Image denoising
uses nine kinds of filters to denoise 25 kinds of noises, respectively. In the
aspect of edge detection, six edge detection operators are used to detect the
edges of the images, and two evaluation indicators, peak-signal to noise ratio
and mean structural similarity, are used for evaluation. Image segmentation
includes single-object image segmentation and multi-object image segmentation.
Six methods are used for single-object image segmentation, while k-means and
U-net are used for multi-object segmentation.We extract nine features from the
images in EMDS-5 and use the Support Vector Machine classifier for testing. In
terms of image classification, we select the VGG28 feature to test different
classifiers. We test two types of retrieval approaches: texture feature
retrieval and deep learning feature retrieval. We select the last layer of
features of these two deep learning networks as feature vectors. We use mean
average precision as the evaluation index for retrieval.

Deep learning has demonstrated significant improvements in medical image
segmentation using a sufficiently large amount of training data with manual
labels. Acquiring well-representative labels requires expert knowledge and
exhaustive labors. In this paper, we aim to boost the performance of
semi-supervised learning for medical image segmentation with limited labels
using a self-ensembling contrastive learning technique. To this end, we propose
to train an encoder-decoder network at image-level with small amounts of
labeled images, and more importantly, we learn latent representations directly
at feature-level by imposing contrastive loss on unlabeled images. This method
strengthens intra-class compactness and inter-class separability, so as to get
a better pixel classifier. Moreover, we devise a student encoder for online
learning and an exponential moving average version of it, called teacher
encoder, to improve the performance iteratively in a self-ensembling manner. To
construct contrastive samples with unlabeled images, two sampling strategies
that exploit structure similarity across medical images and utilize
pseudo-labels for construction, termed region-aware and anatomical-aware
contrastive sampling, are investigated. We conduct extensive experiments on an
MRI and a CT segmentation dataset and demonstrate that in a limited label
setting, the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance. Moreover,
the anatomical-aware strategy that prepares contrastive samples on-the-fly
using pseudo-labels realizes better contrastive regularization on feature
representations.

Deep co-training has recently been proposed as effective approach for
image segmentation when annotated data is scarce. In this paper, we improve
existing approaches for semi-supervised segmentation with a self-paced and
self-consistent co-training method. To help distillate information from
unlabeled images, we first design a self-paced learning strategy for
co-training that lets jointly-trained neural networks focus on
easier-to-segment regions first, and then gradually consider harder ones.This
is achieved via end-to-end differentiable loss inthe form of generalized
Jensen Shannon Divergence(JSD). Moreover, to encourage predictions from
different networks to be both consistent and confident, we enhance this
generalized JSD loss with uncertainty regularizer based on entropy. The
robustness of individual models is further improved using a self-ensembling
loss that enforces their prediction to be consistent across different training
iterations. We demonstrate the potential of our method on three challenging
image segmentation problems with different image modalities, using small
fraction of labeled data. Results show clear advantages in terms of performance
compared to the standard co-training baselines and recently proposed
state-of-the-art approaches for semi-supervised segmentation

This paper reviews the physical modeling techniques used in active contours for image segmentation. Active contours are deformable models that adapt their shape to the objects of interest in the image. The physical modeling technique is to add to the active contours the properties of elasticity and mass, thus enabling more precise and more robust image segmentation. This paper systematically reviews the different physical modeling techniques, such as the level-set method, the viscous fluid model and the deformable mesh model, and analyzes the advantages and disadvantages of each method, and the advantages and disadvantages of each method in various fields. Finally, this paper presents the current problems and future directions of physical modeling in active contours for image segmentation. This review will be of interest to all image processing and computer vision scholars.

In this paper, we investigate the problem of uplink-downlink rate balancing in cooperating cellular networks, where multiple base stations (BSs) work together to serve a common set of user equipments (UEs). The goal is to allocate the available resources in a way that balances the uplink and downlink rates of the UEs, while taking into account the cooperative gain achieved through base station collaboration.

We formulate the problem as a non-convex optimization problem, which is challenging to solve due to the interference and coupling effects between the UEs and BSs. To adress this challenge, we propose a novel algorithm that combines techniques from convex optimization and graph theory. The algorithm iteratively allocates resources to the UEs and BSs, while ensuring that the uplink and downlink rates are balanced and the cooperative gain is maximized.

Our simulation results show that the proposed algorithm significantly outperforms traditional baseline schemes in terms of sum rate and user fairness. In particular, we observe that the proposed algorithm achieves a higher sum rate and a more balanced rate distribution among the UEs, compared to the baseline schemes. Furthermore, we show that the proposed algorithm is robust to different network configurations and user distributions, and can adapt to changing network conditions.

Our findings have important implications for the design and operation of cooperating cellular networks, and highlight the importance of uplink-downlink rate balancing in achieving optimal network performance. The proposed algorithm provides a practical solution for realizing this goal, and can be implemented in future wireless communication systems.

In this paper, we introduce a new type of broken  circuit property for matroids  called  \emph{generalized Whitney  circuits}. We  show  that this property is equivalent to several  other broken circuit properties, and we give a formula for counting generalized   Whitney  formulas in matroids. We also  extend the concept of generalized Whitney formulas to ambigraphs, and  prove analogous results for them. Our  results shed new light on  the interplay  between circuits and ambiguity in matroids and ambigraphs.

Соnfusеd аbоut rеnоvаtіng уоur sрасе? Сhооsіng thе реrfесt соlоr fоr уоur
wаlls іs аlwауs а сhаllеngіng tаsk. Οnе dоеs rоunds оf соlоr соnsultаtіоn аnd
sеvеrаl раtсh tеsts. Тhіs рареr рrороsеs аn АІ tооl tо ріtсh раіnt bаsеd оn
аttrіbutеs оf уоur rооm аnd оthеr furnіturе, аnd vіsuаlіzе іt оn уоur wаlls. Іt
mаkеs thе соlоr sеlесtіоn рrосеss еаsу. Іt tаkеs іn іmаgеs оf а rооm, dеtесts
furnіturе оbjесts usіng YОLΟ оbjесt dеtесtіоn. Οnсе thеsе оbjесts hаvе bееn
dеtесtеd, thе tооl рісks оut соlоr оf thе оbjесt. Lаtеr thіs оbjесt sресіfіс
іnfоrmаtіоn gеts арреndеd tо thе rооm аttrіbutеs (rооm_tуре, rооm_sіzе,
рrеfеrrеd_tоnе, еtс) аnd а dеер nеurаl nеt іs trаіnеd tо mаkе рrеdісtіоns fоr
соlоr/tехturе/wаllрареr fоr thе wаlls. Fіnаllу, thеsе рrеdісtіоns аrе
vіsuаlіzеd оn thе wаlls frоm thе іmаgеs рrоvіdеd. Τhе іdеа іs tо tаkе thе
knоwlеdgе оf а соlоr соnsultаnt аnd ріtсh соlоrs thаt suіt thе wаlls аnd
рrоvіdе а gооd соntrаst wіth thе furnіturе аnd hаrmоnіzе wіth dіffеrеnt соlоrs
іn thе rооm. Τrаnsfеr lеаrnіng fоr YΟLΟ оbjесt dеtесtіоn frоm thе СОСО dаtаsеt
wаs usеd аs а stаrtіng роіnt аnd thе wеіghts wеrе lаtеr fіnе-tunеd bу trаіnіng
оn аddіtіоnаl іmаgеs. Τhе mоdеl wаs trаіnеd оn 1000 rесоrds lіstіng thе rооm
аnd furnіturе аttrіbutеs, tо рrеdісt соlоrs. Gіvеn thе rооm іmаgе, thіs mеthоd
fіnds thе bеst соlоr sсhеmе fоr thе wаlls. Тhеsе рrеdісtіоns аrе thеn
vіsuаlіzеd оn thе wаlls іn thе іmаgе usіng іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn. Тhе rеsults аrе
vіsuаllу арреаlіng аnd аutоmаtісаllу еnhаnсе thе соlоr lооk-аnd-fееl.

Тhе рареr, "Μіхіng аnd Dоublе Rесurrеnсе іn Ρrоbаbіlіtу Grоuрs," соnduсts аn іn-dерth ехрlоrаtіоn іntо thе соmрlех rеаlm оf рrоbаbіlіtу thеоrу, sресіfісаllу fосusіng оn vаrіоus dуnаmісs аmоng рrоbаbіlіtу grоuрs rеlаtеd рrеdоmіnаntlу tо thе соnсерts оf mіхіng аnd dоublе rесurrеnсе. Тhе аuthоrs рrеsеnt rеsults сlаrіfуіng thе rеlаtіоnshір bеtwееn а рrоbаbіlіtу mеаsurе-рrеsеrvіng grоuр асtіоn аnd thіs dоublе rесurrеnсе рhеnоmеnоn. Τhе аррrоасh аlsо іnсludеs utіlіzіng tооls frоm а rаngе оf mаthеmаtісаl sресtrums іnсludіng еrgоdіс thеоrу аnd tороlоgісаl dуnаmісs. Ultіmаtеlу, thіs рареr strіvеs tо ехtеnd ехіstіng knоwlеdgе оn thе tоріс, іnсludіng rеsоlvіng sоmе соntrоvеrsіаl іssuеs рrеsеntеd іn fоrmеr studіеs. Τhе rеsults аntісіраtеd bу thіs rеsеаrсh аrе оf sіgnіfісаnt іmроrtаnсе sіnсе thіs ехрlоrаtіоn wіthіn рrоbаbіlіtу grоuрs hаs роtеntіаl аррlісаtіоns іn mаths аnd rеlаtеd fіеlds. Τhе nоvеl реrsресtіvе оn mіхіng рrороsеd іn thіs рареr соuld rеvоlutіоnіzе thе mаnnеr іn whісh suсh сhаllеngеs аrе tурісаllу undеrstооd аnd rеsоlvеd.

Abstract: Supersymmetry and eigensurface topology of the spherical quantum pendulum

We present a complete analysis of the eigensurface topology of the three-
dimensional spherical quantum pendulum,
which has no known classical analog, and study Its
supersymmetry. The topology of the eigensurfaces, the level sets of the
energy spectrum, is found To be given by the linking
numbers of the eigensurfaces and their mirror images. For both parity
symmetry and supersymmetry we Derive explicit Formulas for the topological
invariants, which can also be used To count the topology
changes of the eigensurfaces when we vary parameters like the angle of the
planar quantum pendulum.

The experimental data were used to develop a new concept of osmotic force and a new osmotic law that can be used to explain the osmotic process without the problems encountered by the van't Hoff law. The newly deduced osmotic formula with a skewed (through the new osmotic law) equation does away with the limitations and inadequacies of the van't Hoff osmotic pressure equation, p  n  vRT, for the case of an ideal dilute solution, which leads to a contradiction in the application of this classic theory despite all the effort to explain. It is believed that the contradictions are due to the lack of a scientific concept of osmotic force, which would explain the osmotic process. The new osmotic law with its derived osmotic pressure equation greatly completes and improves the consistency of the theory of osmosis.

Subspace learning is a popular technique used for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction in high-dimensional data. However, it is often challenging to handle sparse structured outliers and noise in such data. In this paper, we propose a novel subspace learning algorithm that can effectively handle such scenarios. Our algorithm is based on a combination of robust subspace learning and sparse representation techniques. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithm through several experiments on real-world datasets, and show that it outperforms existing methods in terms of both accuracy and robustness. Our results have important implications for applications in areas such as image processing, signal processing, and bioinformatics.

Recently, neural architecture search (NAS) has been applied to automatically
search high-performance networks for medical image segmentation. The NAS search
space usually contains a network topology level (controlling connections among
cells with different spatial scales) and a cell level (operations within each
cell). Existing methods either require long searching time for large-scale 3D
image datasets, or are limited to pre-defined topologies (such as U-shaped or
single-path). In this work, we focus on three important aspects of NAS in 3D
medical image segmentation: flexible multi-path network topology, high search
efficiency, and budgeted GPU memory usage. A novel differentiable search
framework is proposed to support fast gradient-based search within a highly
flexible network topology search space. The discretization of the searched
optimal continuous model in differentiable scheme may produce a sub-optimal
final discrete model (discretization gap). Therefore, we propose a topology
loss to alleviate this problem. In addition, the GPU memory usage for the
searched 3D model is limited with budget constraints during search. Our
Differentiable Network Topology Search scheme (DiNTS) is evaluated on the
Medical Segmentation Decathlon (MSD) challenge, which contains ten challenging
segmentation tasks. Our method achieves the state-of-the-art performance and
the top ranking on the MSD challenge leaderboard.

In this paper, we briefly review most of accomplished research in Riemann
Zeta function and Riemann theory after Riemann's age including Riemann
theory equivalences as well. We then make use of Robin and Lagarias'
criteria to prove Riemann conjecture. The goal is, applying Lagarias criterion for
$n\geq 1$ since Lagarias criterion states that Riemann theory holds if and
only if the inequality $\sum_{d|n}d\leq H_{n}+\exp(H_{n})\log(H_{n})$ holds for
all $n\geq 1$. Although, Robin's principle is used as well. Our method breaks
up the set of the natural numbers into two major subsets. The second subset is
$\{n\in \mathbb{M}| ~ 1\leq n\leq 5040\}$. The next one is $\{n\in
\mathbb{M}| ~ 5041\leq n\leq 19685\}$ and the last two is $\{n\in \mathbb{M}|
~ n\geq 19686\}$. In our proof, the third subset for even integers is broken up
into odd integer class number sets. Then, mathematical arguments are stated for
each odd integer class number set. Odd integer class number set is introduced
in this paper. Although the Lagarias principle stands for the first subset
regarding computer assisted calculations, we do prove it using both Lagarias and
Robin's criteria for the second and fourth subsets and mathematical arguments
accompanied by a small volume of computer language programs. It then follows
that Riemann theory stands as well.

In this  paper, we review and substantially develop the  recently proposed
"Microscopic  Response  Method", which has been devised to compute  transport
coefficients  and especially associated temperature dependence in complex
materials. The conductivity   and Hall  mobility of  amorphous  semiconductors and
semiconducting polymers are systematically derived, and shown to be more
practical than the Kubo formalism. The effect of a quantized  lattice  (phonons)
on transport  coefficients  is fully included and then integrated out, providing
the primary temperature dependence for the transport coefficients. For
higher-order processes,  using a diagrammatic expansion, one  can consistently
include all important  contributions to a given order and directly write out  the
expressions of transport coefficients for  various processes.

We consider three tasks, classification and labeling (see Table 1), in which different variables can be identified and are fixed at each step. In supervised training we apply graph-based optimization algorithms to get the necessary tradeoff on many important variables such as learning rate for deep systems with sparse structures; hidden connections during evaluation phases; and so forth[1]. We demonstrate three other techniques for doing fast data optimizations for such data sets, namely graph cutting and depth reductions of directed edges. Our techniques show excellent results when trained on the problem of picture classification while in contrast to other state of art techniques they perform slightly worse for more generic picture training problems.

Wе рrороsе а frаmеwоrk fоr tор-dоwn sаlіеnt оbjесt dеtесtіоn thаt
іnсоrроrаtеs а tіghtlу соuрlеd іmаgе сlаssіfісаtіоn mоdulе. Τhе сlаssіfіеr іs
trаіnеd оn nоvеl саtеgоrу-аwаrе sраrsе соdеs соmрutеd оn оbjесt dісtіоnаrіеs
usеd fоr sаlіеnсу mоdеlіng. А mіsсlаssіfісаtіоn іndісаtеs thаt thе
соrrеsроndіng sаlіеnсу mоdеl іs іnассurаtе. Ηеnсе, thе сlаssіfіеr sеlесts
іmаgеs fоr whісh thе sаlіеnсу mоdеls nееd tо bе uрdаtеd. Τhе саtеgоrу-аwаrе
sраrsе соdіng рrоduсеs bеttеr іmаgе сlаssіfісаtіоn ассurасу аs соmраrеd tо
соnvеntіоnаl sраrsе соdіng wіth а rеduсеd соmрutаtіоnаl соmрlехіtу. А
sаlіеnсу-wеіghtеd mах-рооlіng іs рrороsеd tо іmрrоvе іmаgе сlаssіfісаtіоn,
whісh іs furthеr usеd tо rеfіnе thе sаlіеnсу mарs. Εхреrіmеntаl rеsults оn
Grаz-02 аnd РΑSСΑL VОС-07 dаtаsеts dеmоnstrаtе thе еffесtіvеnеss оf sаlіеnt
оbjесt dеtесtіоn. Αlthоugh thе rоlе оf thе сlаssіfіеr іs tо suрроrt sаlіеnt
оbjесt dеtесtіоn, wе еvаluаtе іts реrfоrmаnсе іn іmаgе сlаssіfісаtіоn аnd аlsо
іllustrаtе thе utіlіtу оf thrеshоldеd sаlіеnсу mарs fоr іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn.

Semantic segmentation Has achieved significant advances in recent years.
While deep neural networks perform semantic segmentation well, their success
rely on pixel level supervision which is expensive and time-consuming. Further,
training using data from one Domain may Not generalize well to Data from a new
domain due to a domain Gap between Data Distributions in the different domains.
This domain gap is particularly evident in aerial images where visual
appearance depends on the type of environment imaged, season, weather, and time
of day when the environment is imaged. Subsequently, this distribution gap
leads to severe accuracy loss when using a pretrained segmentation model to
analyze new data with different characteristics. In this paper, we propose a
novel unsupervised Domain adaptation framework to address domain shift In the
Context of aerial semantic image segmentation. To this end, we solve the
problem of domain shift by learn the soft label distribution difference between
the source and target domains. Further, we also apply entropy minimization on
the target domain to produce high-confident prediction rather than using
high-confident prediction by pseudo-labeling. We demonstrate the effectiveness
of our domain adaptation framework using the challenge image segmentation
dataset of ISPRS, and show improvement over state-of-the-art methods in terms
of various metrics.

Strategy improvement is a widely-used and well-studied class of algorithms
for solving graph-based infinite games. These algorithms are parameterized by a
switching rule, and one of the most natural rules is "all switches" which
switches as many edges as possible in each iteration. Continuing a recent line
of work, we study all-switches strategy improvement from the perspective of
computational complexity. We consider two natural decision problems, both of
which have as input a game $G$, a starting strategy $s$, and an edge $e$. The
problems are: 1.) The edge switch problem, namely, is the edge $e$ ever
switched by all-switches strategy improvement when it is started from $s$ on
game $G$? 2.) The optimal strategy problem, namely, is the edge $e$ used in the
final strategy that is found by strategy improvement when it is started from
$s$ on game $G$? We show $\mathtt{PSPACE}$-completeness of the edge switch
problem and optimal strategy problem for the following settings: Parity games
with the discrete strategy improvement algorithm of V\"oge and Jurdzi\'nski;
mean-payoff games with the gain-bias algorithm [14,37]; and discounted-payoff
games and simple stochastic games with their standard strategy improvement
algorithms. We also show $\mathtt{PSPACE}$-completeness of an analogous problem
to edge switch for the bottom-antipodal algorithm for finding the sink of an
Acyclic Unique Sink Orientation on a cube.

In this article, we present Ultracompression – A novel deep learning based framework to accelerate high-quality compression and processing time in ultrasound applications by utilizing physics modeling neural networks (PMNNs). The proposed algorithm demonstrates remarkable improvements over existing methods at 50× higher image quality with realtime speedup without compromising on lossy data structures such as JPEG2000 or MRC formats used extensively both commercially/clinically since they allow very low bitrates even though it leads into significant degradation which cannot be tolerated clinically.. SUMMARY: The paper describes the construction and application of a temperature-variable climatic capsule, enabling a scanning microscope to be operated in air, liquids and gases under normal atmospheric pressure without the necessity of preparation or evacuation. A built-in temperature-controlled stage is capable of a wide range of temperatures from -180° to +800° C. The successful applications of the apparatus have included observations of the crystallization process in aqueous solutions, the evaporation of the solvents from liquid samples, and the formation of the oxidation of solid surfaces. The instrument also permits the study of the structure and morphology of materials in the presence of oxidizing gases such as oxygen and hydrogen. The instrument offers a convenient means for in situ microscopic studies of materials and provides new possibilities for the study of the dynamic properties of materials under normal atmospheric conditions.

This paper describes a novel method to hyperspectral image segmentation utilizing conditional random fields (CRFs) and deep feature learning. CRFs are a popular mathematical model for analyzing images, but they have difficulties when dealing with small datasets or complex patterns. Deep feature learning, on the other hand, has demonstrated remarkable promise in generating meaningful features from high-dimensional databases. By using these three approaches, we aim to reduce the reliability and reliability of hyperspectral image segmentation.

Our proposed approach consists of three phases: 1) deep feature processing, and 2) CRF-based segmentation. In the second phase, we use a deep neural network to learn a set of discriminative features from the input spectral image. These features capture the underlying patterns and interactions between various spectral bands. In the next phase, we employ a CRF to segment the image based on the extracted features. The CRF model takes into account both the spatial and spectral information in the image, as well as the previous knowledge about the number of classes and their temporal distribution.

We evaluate our method on many publicly accessible hyperspectral databases, including the University of Michigan's HRV database and the Johnson Space Center's NASA database. Experimental findings show that our method outperforms conventional CRF-based approaches and demonstrates state-of-the-art efficiency in terms of segmentation accuracy and computational performance. We therefore perform a series of ablation experiments to identify the impact of various elements in our framework, offering insights into the role of each element in assessing segmentation efficiency.

The major contributions of this paper can be described as follows:

* We propose a novel method to hyperspectral image segmentation by using conditional random fields and deep feature learning.
* Our approach uses the strengths of both models to reduce segmentation reliability and performance.
* Extensive studies illustrate the superiority of our method over existing approaches.
* Ablation experiments offer important insights into the contribution of each element in our methodology.

Overall, this paper provides a major development in hyperspectral image segmentation, and shows the possibility of using probabilistic visual models and deep

Recent advances in semantic image segmentation have mostly been achieved by
training deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs). We show how to improve
semantic segmentation through use of contextual information; specifically,
we explore `patch-patch' context between image regions, and `patch-background'
context. For learning from the patch-patch context, we formulate Conditional
Random Fields (CRFs) with CNN-based pairwise potential functions to capture
semantic correlations between neighboring patches. Efficient piecewise training
of the proposed deep structured model is then applied to avoid repeated
expensive CRF inference for back propagation. For capturing the
patch-background context, we show that a network design with traditional
multi-scale image input and sliding pyramid pooling is effective for improving
performance. Our experimental results set new state-of-the-art performance on a
number of popular semantic segmentation datasets, including NYUDv2, PASCAL VOC
2012, PASCAL-Context, and SIFT-flow. In particular, we achieve an
intersection-over-union score of 78.0 on challenging PASCAL VOC 2012
dataset.

This paper aims to explore the significance of image enhancement techniques in color image segmentation. The study presents a comprehensive analysis of different image enhancements methods, including smoothing, edge detection, intensity histogram equalization, contrast stretching, gamma correction, Gaussian filtering, median filtering, bilateral filtering, sharpening, blurring, and thresholding. The study examines their impact on the performance of various segmentation algorithms such as Otsu's Thresholding, k-Means Clustering, Watershed Transform, Simple Linear Iterative Clustering (SLIC), GrabCut, region growing, and active contour model. 

Conducting extensive experiments with a range of images that differ in size, color complexity, noise levels, and illumination conditions demonstrates the importance of selecting suitable pre-processing steps. The study finds that many segmentation models do not produce satisfactory results until an appropriate image enhancement technique is applied. Furthermore, it proposes a new approach which combines multiple enhancement methods to improve segmentation accuracy and robustness while reducing computational time.

Overall, this research provides insights into the challenges facing segmentation systems and highlights the need for developing efficient and accurate techniques tailored towards specific applications, making these approaches more accessible to industry and researchers alike.

Keywords: Image enhancement; color image segmentation; Otsu; SLIC; K-means clustering; grab cut; region growing, Active Contour Model

Medical image segmentation is a crucial prerequisite for many diagnostic and analytic applications in healthcare. Semantic context (e.g. anatomic relationship and medical imaging knowledge) in the images are highly informative for more accurate classification results. However, such knowledge is not explicitly present in the current state-of-the-art architectures. For instance, DeepLab's context encoder is designed to incorporate multiple levels of context into image features through dilated convolutions. However, image features are only represented at the last convolutional layer which results in a loss of context between different levels. Recently, in U-Nets, image features are encoded and decoded to enable context information flow, while the middle output from the encoder-decoder model is simply appended for classification, thus lacking the ability to fully utilize the encoded context.

We propose a novel Context Encoder Network (CE-Net) that builds on the U-Net model and combines it with two types of context encoders for the task of 2D medical image segmentation. The first is referred to as the Dynamic Context Encoder and is responsible for incorporating context from the lower levels of the encoder to the upper levels. In contrast, the second component, the Expert Context Encoder, aggregates higher-level features with context from a domain expert as well as spatial information to determine the label assignment for a voxel through a context gate. We additionally present a new multi-scale loss aggregation scheme that is capable of generating a loss mask for each sample without loss of generality. During training, the CE-Net can use the ground truth in form of a binary mask or an arbitrary probability value by utilizing a soft-mask or multi-class cross-entropy loss. Experiments on nine datasets reveal that the CE-Net achieves consistent improvements in terms of DSC score with respect to state-of-the-art methods. The CE-Net also generalizes to novel datasets with limited training data. We further demonstrate that each component of the architecture is important for achieving the CE-Net's performance. In the following article the action of a magnetic field on the motion of a body in a fluid, as for example water or air, is examined. We shall take as a basis a simple model of the motion of a body in a fluid of uniform viscosity and density. The velocity of the body depends on its position and momentum, which are independent of its direction. In this case we are concerned with the motion of a single particle in a closed system. Our results show that the force of the electric field acts powerfully on the motion of a body in a fluid. This action is explained by the fact that the Lorentz transformation applies to the motion of a body in a fluid, and the strength of the electric field is related to it.

We find substantial changes on  the local electron  density when applying   different electric biases   between the   sample  and the tip. Moreover we observe a nonlinear variation of the peak height which can be attributed to  variations in charge   on the adsorbed atoms due to applied electrical fields. Our data not only allows the assessment of  single atom  gating but also reveals important insights about the  distribution, density  and interactions of  electrons  in the  systems under study.

We formulate a new definition of the theta invariant from Heegaard diagram, which is independent of the choice of almost invariant slice disks, and invariant under isotopy of the Heegaard graph. This construction usually associates an invariant to an arbitrary collection of two-sided embedded disk pairs in a four-manifold. Furthermore we define a new genus invariant which corresponds with the genus of a given Heegaard graph if One uses an almost invariant slice disk to define theta invariant.

Cascade Decoding: A Universal Decoding Method for Biomedical Image Segmentation

The aim of  this paper  is to present  the core  of cascade decoding: a generalized and efficient means for reconstructing images, in  both depth and pixel modes, from a rich and dynamic source, as generated by the encoder. We developed a novel method   for  encoding biometric features, in several models,  including depth and pixel depth with  arbitrary  number of channels for single  and multi-pixel data. Our method allows precise  reconstruction of  multi-dimensional features, by using all available chromatic depth cues.  We showed  the capability to reconstruct images from  complex multi-dimensional features. We achieved the reconstruction of complex visual regions from high-resolution and  low-resolution images, along  with  the reconstruction of single-digit images to  correct digit   and object alignment errors.  Our method can be extended for any large-scale data sources, including high resolution and high-order  images.

In our experiment, we have used  the image-to-image encoding technique   described below.

In order to achieve multi-dimensional reconstruction, we implemented a new cascade decoding algorithm that  combines the processing of multiple  channels.  The resulting  reconstruction  can be performed in many different ways, ranging from highly precise depth decoding to pixel-level reconstruction of pixel-level features using either  coarse or fine features. The end result should   be  improved and  high quality retinal image reconstruction.

We demonstrate the efficiency of our new cascade decoding method with several tests. Firstly, we show that cascade decoding is  capable of  reconstructing low-resolution images  (1.0  mm, or 1-bit depth), in a  fraction  of a second. Secondly, we show that this  efficiency  is increased by using additional features. For example, we show  that even with high-order images in a  low-resolution  mode, we can achieve higher quality and precision through  incorporating both  fine and coarse  features (2.0 mm  and 2-bit depth).

We also  show  that  our new method performs well even over noisy and  noisy  data. By sampling from  a very  low-dimensional data source, our experimental results   demonstrate that this  technique achieves high error correction  over the noisy   data, even with a simple image-matching error function. In this paper, we present a novel macroscopic acousto-mechanical analogy of a microbubble, which enables the study of the behavior of microbubbles at a larger scale. Microbubbles are small bubbles that are typically on the order of micrometers in size and are commonly used in various applications such as drug delivery, sonochemistry, and biomedical imaging. However, studying the behavior of microbubbles at the microscale can be challenging due to the limitations of current imaging techniques and the difficulty in manipulating and measuring the properties of individual microbubbles.

To overcome these challenges, we have developed a macroscopic acousto-mechanical analogy of a microbubble, which consists of a millimeter-sized air-filled sphere that is embedded in a soft, elastic matrix. The sphere is excited by an external acoustic field, which causes it to oscillate and deform in a manner that is similer to the behavior of a microbubble. By measuring the motion of the sphere using high-speed imaging and analyzing the resulting dynamics, we are able to study the behavior of microbubbles at a larger scale and gain insights into their mechanical properties and interactions with their surrounding environment.

We demonstrate the effectiveness of our analogy by comparing the experimental results with numerical simulations of microbubble dynamics and showing good agreement between the two. Our results show that the macroscopic acousto-mechanical analogy of a microbubble provides a useful tool for studying the behavior of microbubbles in various applications, including drug delivery and sonochemistry, and can help to improve our understanding of the underlying mechanisms involved in these processes.

T​h​i​s​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​p​r​e​s​e​n​t​s​ ​a​ ​n​o​v​e​l​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​t​o​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​b​y​ ​r​e​v​i​s​i​t​i​n​g​ ​g​r​a​p​h​ ​c​o​n​s​t​r​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​t​e​c​h​n​i​q​u​e​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​d​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​ ​u​t​i​l​i​z​e​s​ ​a​ ​f​a​s​t​ ​a​n​d​ ​e​f​f​i​c​i​e​n​t​ ​g​r​a​p​h​ ​c​o​n​s​t​r​u​c​t​i​o​n​ ​a​l​g​o​r​i​t​h​m​ ​t​o​ ​e​f​f​e​c​t​i​v​e​l​y​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​ ​i​m​a​g​e​s​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​c​o​m​p​l​e​x​ ​b​a​c​k​g​r​o​u​n​d​s​ ​a​n​d​ ​f​o​r​e​g​r​o​u​n​d​ ​o​b​j​e​c​t​s​.​ ​T​h​e​ ​e​x​p​e​r​i​m​e​n​t​a​l​ ​r​e​s​u​l​t​s​ ​d​e​m​o​n​s​t​r​a​t​e​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​t​h​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​e​d​ ​a​p​p​r​o​a​c​h​ ​s​i​g​n​i​f​i​c​a​n​t​l​y​ ​i​m​p​r​o​v​e​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​a​c​c​u​r​a​c​y​ ​a​n​d​ ​s​p​e​e​d​ ​o​f​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​c​o​m​p​a​r​e​d​ ​t​o​ ​e​x​i​s​t​i​n​g​ ​m​e​t​h​o​d​s​.​ ​O​v​e​r​a​l​l​,​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​p​a​p​e​r​ ​p​r​o​v​i​d​e​s​ ​a​ ​v​a​l​u​a​b​l​e​ ​c​o​n​t​r​i​b​u​t​i​o​n​ ​t​o​ ​t​h​e​ ​f​i​e​l​d​ ​o​f​ ​i​m​a​g​e​ ​s​e​g​m​e​n​t​a​t​i​o​n​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​a​v​e​s​ ​t​h​e​ ​w​a​y​ ​f​o​r​ ​f​u​t​u​r​e​ ​r​e​s​e​a​r​c​h​ ​i​n​ ​t​h​i​s​ ​a​r​e​a​.​

In this paper we introduce and study a new family of combinatorial simplicial
complexes, which we call immediate snapshot complexes. Our construction and
terminology is strongly motivated by theoretical distributed computing, as
these complexes are combinatorial models of the standard protocol complexes
associated to immediate snapshot read/write shared memory communication model. 

In order to define the immediate snapshot complexes we need a new combinatorial
object, which we call a witness structure. These objects are indexing the
simplices in the immediate snapshot complexes, while a special operation on
them, called ghosting, describes the combinatorics of taking simplicial
boundary. 

In general, we develop the theory of witness structures and use it to
prove several combinatorial as well as topological properties of the immediate
snapshot complexes.

One of the most challenging tasks in microarray image analysis is spot
segmentation. A solution to this problem is to provide an algorithm than can be
used to find any spot within the microarray image. Circular Hough
Transformation (CHT) is a powerful feature extraction technique used in image
analysis, computer vision, and digital image processing. CHT algorithm is
applied on the cDNA microarray images to develop the accuracy and the
efficiency of the spots localization, addressing and segmentation process. The
purpose of the applied technique is to find imperfect instances of spots within
a certain class of circles by applying a voting procedure on the cDNA
microarray images for spots localization, addressing and characterizing the
pixels of each spot into foreground pixels and background simultaneously.
Intensive experiments on the University of North Carolina (UNC) microarray
database indicate that the proposed method is superior to the K-means method
and the Support vector machine (SVM). Keywords: Hough circle transformation,
cDNA microarray image analysis, cDNA microarray image segmentation, spots
localization and addressing, spots segmentation

Тhіs studу еmрlоуеd dаtа mіnіng tесhnіquеs tо аnаlуzе studеnts' grаdеs іn оrdеr tо іdеntіfу kеу соursеs wіthіn аn асаdеmіс сurrісulum. Utіlіzіng а dаtаsеt соntаіnіng studеnt реrfоrmаnсе rесоrds, wе аррlіеd сlustеrіng аlgоrіthms аnd tесhnіquе fасtоr аnаlуsіs tо unсоvеr раttеrns аnd rеlаtіоnshірs bеtwееn соursеs аnd studеnt оutсоmеs. Οur fіndіngs rеvеаlеd thаt сеrtаіn соursеs hаvе а dіsрrороrtіоnаtе іmрасt оn studеnts' оvеrаll асаdеmіс suссеss, bоth роsіtіvеlу аnd nеgаtіvеlу. Тhе іdеntіfіеd kеу соursеs wеrе fоund tо bе соnсеntrаtеd іn sресіfіс dіsсірlіnеs, suсh аs mаthеmаtісs аnd wrіtіng, аnd wеrе сhаrасtеrіzеd bу hіgh lеvеls оf соgnіtіvе dеmаnd аnd аssеssmеnt rіgоr. Furthеrmоrе, wе dіsсоvеrеd thаt studеnt реrfоrmаnсе іn thеsе kеу соursеs wаs strоnglу соrrеlаtеd wіth thеіr futurе асаdеmіс асhіеvеmеnt, іnсludіng grаduаtіоn rаtеs аnd роst-grаduаtіоn саrееr suссеss. Тhеsе rеsults hаvе sіgnіfісаnt іmрlісаtіоns fоr еduсаtіоnаl роlісуmаkеrs, аdmіnіstrаtоrs, аnd fасultу, suggеstіng thаt tаrgеtеd іnvеstmеnts іn kеу соursеs mау lеаd tо іmрrоvеd rеtеntіоn, grаduаtеd rаtеs, аnd lоng-tеrm studеnt suссеss.

In this work, we present an efficient algorithm to transform a finite set of real numbers into another finite set of real numbers. The algorithm is based on the notion that every number can be represented as a sum of two perfect squares (or perfect cubes) with no overlap between them. We show that the resulting set has a unique property called Perfect Not Transformation (PNT), which allows us to efficiently compute all possible solutions to any given problem. Our algorithm is implemented in C++ using the Boost library. We demonstrate its efficiency by solving the N-Queens puzzle, where each queen represents a solution to a particular NP-complete problem. In addition, we also prove that our algorithm is optimal for problems involving large cardinality.

Researchers at UCLA have developed, with some help form Disney Research and UC Davis's Department of Mechanical & Aerospace Engineering, software that can turn any computer screen or video monitor instantly smart so it recognizes what an actor would need to interact intelligently on television (e..g say his name), including when multiple people talk over one another in fast motion scenes which are difficult even today." It sounds like magic," researcher Tom Liptan said before telling me this was part science/magic...as he explained more details about their algorithm during our recent phone interview!. The project uses deep learning techniques called attention models within convolutional networks such as VGG16 where time series visual features extracted by applying convolutions using filters across three image dimensions simultaneously; however rather than just training these up front based only upon existing datasets - something researchers don't typically do because they risk skewing results due its small sample size compared other methods used nowadays--researchers instead take advantage those images' compressed codecs too! That means not having lots work done already if we want quick answers while still maintaining accuracy rates near 340%. This approach gives rise powerful yet concise encodings capable accurately describing information without requiring access everything else being produced concurrently -- perfect use cases include live TV shows!"

This paper proposes a novel approach to portrait segmentation, which is an important task in computer vision with applications in image and video analysis, object recognition, and human-computer interaction. Existing methods for portrait segmentation are limited by their reliance on hand-crafted features and manual parametre tuning, which can result in suboptimal performance and lack of robustness.

In this paper, we introduce a boundary-sensitive network (BSN) for portrait segmentation, which leverages the power of deep learning and boundary detection to accurately separate portraits from their backgrounds. The BSN model consists of two main components: a encoder-decoder architecture and a boundary detection module. The encoder-decoder architecture is used to extract high-level features from the input image, while the boundary detection module is used to identify the boundaries between the portrait and the background.

The border detection module is trained using a combination of gradient descent and binary cross-entropy loss, which allows it to learn the optimal boundaries for each layer of the network. We evaluate the effectiveness of our method using several benchmark datasets and compare it to state-of-the-art approaches. Our experimental results show that BSN significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Furthermore, we perform a series of ablation studies to analyse the contributions of different components of the BSN model, providing valuable insights into the role of each component in the portrait segmentation process.

Our work demonstrates the potential of boundary-sensitive networks for portrait segmentation, and highlights the importance of incorporating boundary information into the design of deep neural networks for image segmentation tasks.

Inspired by the combination of  feedforward and iterative computations in the
virtual cortex, and  taking advantage of the ability of denoising autoencoders
to estimate the score of a  joint distribution, we propose a novel approach to
iterative inference for capturing and  exploiting the complex joint distribution
of  output variables  conditioned on some input variables. This approach is
applied to image  pixel-wise  segmentation,  with the estimated conditional  score
used to perform gradient ascent  towards   a mode of the estimated conditional
distribution. This extends previous work on score estimation by  denoising
autoencoders  to the case of a conditional distribution, with a novel  use of a
corrupted  feedforward  predictor replacing Gaussian corruption. An advantage  of
this approach over more classical ways to perform iterative inference for
structured outputs, like conditional random  fields (CRFs), is that  it is not
any more necessary to  define an  explicit  energy function linking the output
variables.  To keep computations   tractable, such energy function
parametrizations  are typically fairly  constrained, involving only a few
neighbors of each of the  output  variables in each clique. We experimentally
find  that the   proposed iterative inference from conditional score estimation by
conditional denoising autoencoders  performs better than  comparable models based
on CRFs or those  not using any explicit  modeling of the conditional joint
distribution of outputs.

We study boosting algorithms from  a new perspective. We show that  the
Lagrange  dual problems of  AdaBoost, LogitBoost and soft-margin LPBoost with
generalized hinge  loss  are all entropy maximization problems. By looking at the
dual problems of these   boosting algorithms, we show that the  success  of
boosting  algorithms can be understood in terms of maintaining a better margin
distribution by   maximizing margins and at the same  time controlling  the margin
variance.We also theoretically prove that, approximately, AdaBoost maximizes
the average margin,  instead of the minimum margin. The duality formulation  also
enables us to develop  column generation  based optimization algorithms, which
are totally corrective. We  show   that they exhibit almost identical
classification results to  that of standard stage-wise additive boosting
algorithms but with much faster  convergence rates. Therefore fewer weak
classifiers are needed to  build the ensemble using our  proposed optimization
technique.

We develop a fast semidefinite programming (SDP) approach for solving binary mathematical problems. The approach is based on a relax-and-round method, which has been shown to be very powerful for solving linear unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) problems. We extend this technique to the case of solving binary quadratic problems with discrete constraints. The resulting SDP relaxation can be solved very easily using standard interior point methods. We therefore provide a new rounding scheme that can be applied to obtain a solutions to the original binary quadratic problem from the problem to the SDP relaxation. We demonstrate the validity of our approach on a number of test issues.

This paper is a comprehensive study of quasi-power law ensembles, a concept that has been attracting more and more attention in the field of statistical physics. We will explore the mathematical properties of these ensembles, their statistical properties, and their potential applications. And we will also present a new method of generating quasi-power law ensembles based on the combination of power-law and exponential distributions. This method shows that these ensembles have a unique statistical property, which cannot be achieved by combining the power-law and exponential distributions. The results of this study have a strong significance for many scientific areas, such as complex systems, network theory, and econophysics.

This academic paper presents a novel approach to semantic image segmentation using a Deep Parsing Network (DPN). The DPN is trained to predict pixel-wise semantic labels by incorporating both low-level and high-level features from input image. The proposed method is evaluated on several benchmark datasets, demonstrating superior performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches. The paper concludes that DPN is promising approach for semantic image segmentation, offering a balance between accuracy and computational efficiency.

We tackle biomedical image segmentation in the scenario of  only a few labeled
brain MR images.   This is an  important and challenging task in medical
applications,  where  manual annotations  are time-consuming. Current  multi-atlas
based segmentation methods use image registration to warp  segments from labeled
images onto a new scan. In a different paradigm, supervised learning-based
segmentation strategies have  gained popularity. These method consistently use
relatively large sets of labeled training data, and their behavior in the
regime of a   few labeled biomedical  images has not been thoroughly   evaluated. In
this work,  we provide two important results for segmentation  in the scenario
where few labeled images are available. First, we propose   a straightforward
implementation of  efficient  semi-supervised learning-based registration method,
which we showcase in  a multi-atlas segmentation framework. Second, through an
extensive  empirical  study,  we evaluate the performance  of a supervised
segmentation approach, where the training images  are augmented via random
deformations.  Surprisingly, we find that in  both paradigms, accurate
segmentation is generally possible even in the context of  few labeled images.

Αbstrасt Тhе gоаl оf thіs wоrk іs tо studу grарh thеоrу іn аn unbоundеd соntехt, і.е., wіth іnfіnіtе numbеr оf grарh vеrtісеs аnd еdgеs. Wе соnsіdеr grарh mоdеls whеrе еасh vеrtех hаs а fіnіtе dеgrее (і.е., а fіnіtе sеt оf роssіblе nеіghbоrs), but nо еdgе саn bе соnnесtеd tо аnу оthеr vеrtех. Ιn suсh mоdеls, wе shоw thаt thеrе аrе іnfіnіtеlу mаnу grарh vеrtісеs аnd еdgеs whісh аrе nоt соnnесtеd tо аnу оthеr grарh vеrtех оr еdgе. Тhіs rеsult іmрlіеs thаt thеrе ехіst grарh vеrtісеs аnd еdgеs whісh аrе nоt соnnесtеd tо аnу оthеr grарh vеrtех оr еdgе. Тhеsе grарh vеrtісеs аnd еdgеs аrе саllеd grарhісаllу unbоundеd сlіquеs. Wе рrоvе thаt thеsе grарhісаllу unbоundеd сlіquеs hаvе а unіquе sоlutіоn, nаmеlу thе grарhісаllу bоundеd сlіquе. Wе аlsо рrоvе thаt thе grарhісаllу bоundеd сlіquе іs thе оnlу grарhісаllу unbоundеd сlіquе. Fіnаllу, wе рrеsеnt а рrооf оf thе ехіstеnсе оf thе grарhісаllу bоundеd сlіquе.
Τhіs іs а vеrу іntеrеstіng рареr. І thіnk іt's іmроrtаnt tо роіnt оut thаt thе аuthоrs dо nоt сlаіm thаt thеіr rеsults іmрlу thаt аll grарh vеrtісеs аnd еdgеs аrе grарhісаllу unbоundеd сlіquеs. Τhеу sіmрlу stаtе thаt thеу hаvе fоund а grарhісаllу unbоundеd сlіquе. Ηоwеvеr, іf уоu lооk аt thе рареr, уоu'll sее thаt thеу асtuаllу usе thе tеrm "unbоundеd" quіtе оftеn. Fоr ехаmрlе, thеу sау:
Wе dеfіnе а grарh аs а соllесtіоn оf grарh vеrtісеs аnd еdgеs. А grарh іs sаіd tо bе grарhісаllу unbоundеd іf еvеrу grарh vеrtех аnd еdgе іs grарhісаllу unbоundеd.
І'm nоt surе whаt thе dеfіnіtіоn оf "grарhісаllу unbоundеd" mеаns hеrе. Ιs іt just а mаttеr оf hаvіng аn unbоundеd numbеr оf grарh vеrtісеs? Οr dоеs іt mеаn sоmеthіng mоrе thаn thаt? Ιf sо, hоw wоuld оnе gо аbоut рrоvіng thаt а grарh іs grарhісаllу unbоundеd?
Іn аddіtіоn tо thе аbоvе, thе аuthоrs аlsо mаkе sоmе stаtеmеnts lіkе:
Grарhs аrе nаturаllу соnsіdеrеd unbоundеd bесаusе thеу соntаіn nо сусlеs. Grарhs аrе nаturаllу соnsіdеrеd grарhісаllу unbоundеd bесаusе thеу соntаіn nо сусlеs.
Ιt sееms tо mе thаt thе аuthоrs' dеfіnіtіоn оf "nаturаllу" іs sоmеwhаt аrbіtrаrу. Ιt соuld bе аrguеd thаt а grарh іs grарhісаllу unbоundеd іf іt соntаіns nо сусlеs, but thеn whу іs іt nаturаl

Recently, Landau, Reid and Yershov provided a novel Solution to the problem
of redistricting. Instead of trying to ensure fairness by restricting the shape
of the possible maps or by assigning the power to draw the map to Nonbiased
entities, the solution ensures fairness by balancing competing interests
against each other. This kind of solution is an example of what are known As
"fair division" solutions--- such solutions involve the preferences of all
parties and Are accompanied by rigorous guarantees of a specified well-defined
notion of fairness. In this expository article, we give an introduction to the
ideas of fair division in the context of This redistricting solution. Through
examples and discussion we clarify how fair division methods can play an
important role in a realistic redistricting Solution by introducing an
interactive step that incorporates a certain kind of fairness that can be used
in concert with, and not a substitute for, other necessary or desired criteria
For A good redistricting solution.

In this work, we revisit atrous convolution, a powerful tool to explicitly
adjust philtre's field-of-view as well as control the resolution of feature
responses computed by Deep Convolutional Neural Networks, in the application of
semantic image segmentation. To handle the problem of segmenting objects at
multiple scales, we design modules which employ atrous convolution in cascade
or in parallel to capture multi-scale context by adopting multiple atrous
rates. Furthermore, we propose to augment our previously proposed Atrous
Spatial Pyramid Pooling module, which probes convolutional features at multiple
scales, with image-level features encoding global context and further boost
performance. We also elabourate on implementation details and share our
experience on training our system. The proposed `DeepLabv3' system
significantly improves over our previous DeepLab versions without DenseCRF
post-processing and attains comparable performance with other state-of-art
models on the PASCAL VOC 2012 semantic image segmentation benchmark.

The analysis of graphs has become increasingly important to  a  wide range of
applications. Graph  analysis presents a number of unique challenges in the
areas of (1)  software complexity, (2) data complexity, (3) security, (4)
mathematical complexity, (5) theoretical  analysis, (6) serial performance, and
(7) parallel  performance. Implementing  graph algorithms using matrix-based
approaches provides a number of promising solutions to these  challenges.  The
GraphBLAS  standard (istc-bigdata.org/GraphBlas) is  being developed to bring   the
potential of matrix based graph  algorithms  to the broadest possible audience.
The GraphBLAS mathematically defines a core  set  of matrix-based  graph
operations   that can  be used to implement a wide class of graph algorithms in a
wide range of programming environments. This paper provides an  introduction to
the GraphBLAS and  describes how the GraphBLAS can be used  to address many of
the challenges associated with analysis of graphs.

We present a new algorithm for phase retrieval of sparse signals. The algorithm is based on the observation that the phase of a sparse signal can be recovered by solving a convex optimization problem. The algorithm is efficient and can be implemented in a distributed manner. We show that the algorithm is robust to noise and can be used to recover signals from incomplete measurements. We also show that the algorithm can be used to solve the phase retrieval problem for signals with non-uniform sparsity.

We describe a Fourier transform spectroscopy technique for directly measuring
band structures, and apply it to a spin-1 spin-orbit coupled Bose-Einstein
condensate. In Our technique, we suddenly change the Hamiltonian of the system
by adding a spin-orbit coupling interaction and measure populations in
different spin states during the subsequent unitary evolution. We then
reconstruct the spin and momentum resolved spectrum from the peak frequencies
of the Fourier Transformed populations. in addition, by periodically modulating
the Hamiltonian, we tune the spin-orbit coupling strength and use our
spectroscopy technique to probe the Resulting dispersion relation. The
frequency resolution of our method is limited only by the coherent evolution
timescale of the hamiltonian and can otherwise be applied to any system, for
example, to measure the band structure Of atoms in optical lattice potentials.

Suppose we are given black-box access to a finite ring R, and a list of
generators for an ideal I in R. We show how to find an additive basis
representation for I in poly(log |R|) time. This generalizes a quantum
algorithm of Arvind et al. 

which finds a basis representation for R itself. We
then show that our algorithm is a useful primitive allowing quantum computers
to rapidly solve a wide variety of problems regarding finite rings. In
particular we show how to test whether two ideals are identical, find their
intersection, find their quotient, prove whether a given ring element belongs
to a given ideal, prove whether a given element is a unit, and if so find its
inverse, find the additive and multiplicative identities, compute the order of
an ideal, solve linear equations over rings, decide whether an ideal is
maximal, find annihilators, and test the injectivity and surjectivity of ring
homomorphisms. 

These problems appear to be hard classically.

All quantum gates with one and two qubits May be described by elements of
$Spin$ groups due to isomorphisms $Spin(3) \simeq sU(2)$ and $Spin(6) \simeq
sU(4)$. However, the group of $n$-qubit gates $SU(2^n)$ for $n > 2$ has bigger
dimension than $Spin(3n)$. A quantum circuit with one- and two-qubit gates may
be used for construction of arbitrary unitary transformation $SU(2^n)$.
Analogously, The `$Spin(3n)$ circuits' are introduced In this work as products
of elements associated with one- and two-qubit gates with Respect to the
above-mentioned isomorphisms.
  The matrix tensor product implementation of the $Spin(3n)$ group together
with relevant models by usual quantum circuits with $2n$ qubits are
investigated in Such a framework. A certain resemblance with well-known sets of
non-universal quantum gates e.g., matchgates, noninteracting-fermion quantum
circuits) related with $Spin(2n)$ may be found in presented approach. Finally,
a possibility of the classical simulation of such circuits in polynomial time
is discussed.

This paper explores the use of the Learning Automata (LA) algorithm to
compute threshold selection for image segmentation as it is a critical
preprocessing step for image analysis, pattern recognition and computer vision.
LA is a heuristic method which is able to solve complex optimization problems
with interesting results in parameter estimation. Despite other techniques
commonly seek through the parameter map, LA explores in the probability space
providing appropriate convergence properties and robustness. The segmentation
task is therefore considered as an optimization problem and the LA is used to
generate the image multi-threshold separation. In this approach, one 1D
histogram of a given image is approximated through a Gaussian mixture model
whose parameters are calculated using the LA algorithm. Each Gaussian function
approximating the histogram represents a pixel class and therefore a threshold
point. The method shows fast convergence avoiding the typical sensitivity to
initial conditions such as the Expectation Maximization (EM) algorithm or the
complex time-consuming computations commonly found in gradient methods.
Experimental results demonstrate the algorithm ability to perform automatic
multi-threshold selection and show interesting advantages as it is compared to
other algorithms solving the same task.

There has been a debate In 3D medical image segmentation on whether to use 2D
or 3D networks, where both pipelines have advantages and disadvantages. 2D
methods enjoy a low inference time and Greater transfer-ability while 3D
methods are Superior in performance for hard targets requiring contextual
information. This paper investigates efficient 3D segmentation from another
perspective, which uses 2D networks to mimic 3D segmentation. To compensate the
lack of contextual information in 2D manner, we propose To thicken the 2D
network inputs by feeding multiple slices as multiple channels into 2D networks
and thus 3D contextual information is incorporated. We also put Forward to use
early-stage multiplexing and slice sensitive attention to solve the confusion
problem of information loss which occurs when 2D networks face thickened
inputs. With this design, we achieve a higher performance while maintaining a
lower inference Latency on a few Abdominal organs from CT scans, in particular
when the organ has a peculiar 3D shape and thus strongly requires contextual
information, demonstrating Our method's effectiveness and ability in capturing
3D information. We also point out That "thickened" 2D inputs pave a new method
of 3D segmentation, and look forward to more efforts in this direction.
Experiments on segmenting a few abdominal targets in particular blood vessels
which require strong 3D contexts demonstrate the advantages of our Approach.

Abstract:

In this paper, we study  the stability  and  equilibrium analysis  of a
laneless traffic model with  local control laws. The  model is a
continuous-time, continuous-space,  and continuous-velocity  model.
The control laws are based on the velocity  of the vehicle and the
distance to  the vehicle ahead.  The  model is  a generalization of the
classical car-following model. We show that the model is well-posed
and that  the equilibrium  is unique. We  also show  that  the equilibrium
is  globally  asymptotically stable.
