Convolutional neural networks (ConvNets) have been widely used in vision tasks. However, the performance of ConvNets can be degraded when encountering domain shift. The domain adaptation is more critical in the field of medical image analysis, where data between different modalities are quite different. The labelled training data are limited in the medical image analysis field, making the supervised transfer learning methods less effective. In this paper, we propose an unsupervised domain adaptation framework by means of adversarial learning for cross-modality medical image segmentation. Our model is based on a fully convolutional network with a long-short-circuit topology. Furthermore, we design a plug-and-play domain adaptation module (DAM) that maps the target input to the features in the domain of the original network. A domain critic module (DCM) is built to discriminate the feature space of both domains. The DAM and DCM are trained by an adversarial loss without using any labelled data from the target modality. The proposed method is tested on cardiac structure segmentation in a pair of MR and CT images, and the results are encouraging.

"A model describing the shape-bifurcation behaviour of a spherical dielectric elastomer balloon was calculated by using the shape of their surface as a model. The model is found to correctly reproduce the experimental results obtained for different electrical charges on the balloon. This is due to model assumptions that predict the behaviour of the shock waves that reach the balloon in two different ways depending on the orientation of the elastomer in contact with the surface. 

These two shock wave models, called A and B can be easily distinguished when the elastomer is loaded as a material of low conductivity, that is, the pressure at the contact is less than 10 Pa. For other materials that exert significant pressure, we can also tell the difference between A and B, as the electrical charges involved need to be different." 

This information applies to the type of shock wave that will be created when you shoot a metal ball at a metal mesh. 

We believe that this information will allow you to make improvements and modify your application such that your results can be reproduced accurately. We'd love to hear about your experiences or questions in the comments below.

> We study multiple correlation sequences, which are generalizations of classical exponential sums to several variables. In particular we prove that if $f$ is an arbitrary function on $\mathbb{Z}^d$, then there exists a sequence $(a_n)$ such that $$\limsup_{N\to \infty} \frac{\sum_{\substack{1\leq n\leq N\\ (n,p)=1}} f(pn)e(\alpha n)}{N^{1/2}\log^d N}=5$$ holds for all irrational numbers $\alpha$. This result can be viewed as a multidimensional analogue of Vinogradov's theorem on mean values of exponential sums. As a corollary we obtain a new proof of the equidistribution of nilmanifolds in homogeneous spaces with respect to Heisenberg groups.

Abstract: We  present Voronoi region-based adaptive unsupervised  color  image segmentation  (AVIS)  that can be used to extract high  quality images from  noisy data, such  as video streams or images captured by mobile devices. The algorithm uses a novel approach based on  Voronoi diagrams and a new feature extraction  method called Voronoi distance. Our results show that AVIS outperforms other  state-of-the-art methods  in terms of accuracy, speed, memory usage, and computational complexity. In addition, we demonstrate how  our method can be applied  to real-world problems,  such as   detecting  objects in images,  identifying faces in videos, and extracting information about people's emotions in videos.

Recent advances in 3D fully convolutional networks (FCN) have made it
feasible to produce dense voxel-wise predictions of volumetric Images. In this
work, we show that A multi-class 3D FCN trained on manually labeled CT scans of
several anatomical structures (ranging from the large organs to thin vessels)
can achieve competitive segmentation results, while avoiding the need for
handcrafting features Or training class-specific models.
  to this end, we propose a two-stage, coarse-to-fine approach that will first
use a 3D FCN to roughly define a candidate region, which Will then be used as
input to a second 3D FCN. This reduces the number of voxels the second FCN has
to classify to ~10% and allows it to focus On more detailed segmentation of the
organs and vessels.
  We utilize training and validation sets consisting of 331 clinical CT images
And Test our models on a completely unseen data Collection acquired at a
different hospital that includes 150 cT Scans, targeting three anatomical
organs (liver, spleen, and pancreas). In challenging organs such as the
pancreas, our cascaded approach improves the mean Dice score from 68.5 to
82.2%, achieving the highest reported average score on this dataset. We compare
with a 2D FCN method on a separate dataset of 240 CT scans with 18 classes and
achieve a significantly higher performance in small organs and vessels.
Furthermore, we explore fine-tuning our models to different datasets.
  Our experiments illustrate the promise and robustness of current 3D FCN Based
semantic segmentation of medical images, achieving state-of-the-art results.
Our code and trained models are available for download:
https://github.com/holgerroth/3Dunet_abdomen_cascade.

This paper proposes a novel approach for medical image segmentation that combines deep learning and surrogate-assisted genetic algorithms to account for observer variation. The method aims to improve the robustness and reliability of segmentation results by considering the variability in observer perception. The proposed approach is evaluated on a dataset of medical images and demonstrates superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. The results suggest that the proposed approach is a promising solution for addressing the challenge of observer variation in medical image segmentation.

This paper presents an analytic invariant of G_2 manifolds, which are seven-dimensional Riemannian manifolds with a G_2 structure. The invariant is defined using the cohomology of the manifold and provides a powerful tool for studying the geometry and topology of G_2 manifolds. The paper includes a detailed discussion of the properties of the invariant and its applications in the study of G_2 geometry.

In this paper, we propose an efficient semidefinite programming (SDP)
approach to worst-case linear discriminant analysis (WLDA). Compared with the
traditional LDA, WLDA considers the  dimensionality reduction  problem from the
worst-case  viewpoint, which is  in general more robust for classification.
However,  the original problem of WLDA is non-convex  and difficult  to optimize.
In this paper,  we reformulate the optimization problem of WLDA into a sequence
of semidefinite feasibility problems. To efficiently solve  the semidefinite
feasibility problems, we design a new scalable optimization method with
quasi-Newton methods  and eigen-decomposition  being  the core components. The
proposed  method is orders of magnitude faster  than standard interior-point
based  SDP solvers.
   Experiments  on  a variety of classification problems demonstrate  that our
approach achieves better performance than  standard  LDA. Our  method is  also much
faster and more scalable than standard interior-point SDP  solvers based  WLDA.
The computational complexity for  an  SDP with $m$ constraints and matrices of
size $d$ by $d$ is roughly reduced from $\mathcal{O}(m^3+md^3+m^2d^2)$  to
$\mathcal{O}(d^3)$ ($m>d$ in  our case).

WavePacket is a MATLAB package designed to facilitate the simulation of numerical quantum dynamics in closed quantum systems. It provides a comprehensive set of tools for solving the time-dependent and time-independent Schrödinger equations, as well as the dissipative von Neumann equation, using a variety of discrete variable representations (DVRs). With its intuitive interface and robust capabilities, WavePacket enables researchers to efficiently study the dynamic behaviour of quantum systems in various fields, including condensed matter physics, quantum chemistry, and photonneics. The package supports both continuous and discrete position representations, and it can handle arbitrary potential energy terms and system sizes. In this first part of the paper, we introduce the main features and applications of WavePacket, with a focus on closed quantum systems and DVRs. We demonstrate the accuracy and efficiency of our implementation through several illustrative examples, highlighting the versatility and flexibility of the package. Our goal is to provide scientists with a powerful tool that streamlines numerical simulations, allowing them to investigate novel phenomena and explore new ideas in quantum science and technology.

We obtain a recurrence relation in $d$ for the average singular value $%
\alpha (d)$ of a complex valued $d\times d$\ matrix $\frac{2}{\sqrt{d}}X$ with
random i.i.d., N( 0,1) entries, and use it to show that $\alpha (d)$ decreases
monotonically with $d$ to the limit given by the Marchenko-Pastur
distribution.\ The monotonicity of $\alpha (d)$ has been recently conjectured
by Bandeira, Kennedy and Singer in their study of the Little Grothendieck
problem over the unitary group $\mathcal{U}_{d}$ \cite{BKS}, a combinatorial
optimization problem. The result implies sharp global estimates for $\alpha
(d)$, new bounds for the expected minimum and maximum singular values, and a
lower bound for the ratio of the expected maximum and the expected minimum
singular value. The proof is based on a connection with the theory of Tur\'{a}n
determinants of orthogonal polynomials. We also discuss some applications to
the problem that originally motivated the conjecture.

We investigate spin squeezing, quantum entanglement and third order
coherence in two coupled, driven, dissipative, nonlinear cavities. We compare
these quantum statistical properties for the cavities associated with any
single or two-photon exchange. Solving the quantum optical master equation of
the system numerically in the steady state, we calculate the zero-time delay
second-order correlation function for the coherence, genuine two-mode
quantum parameter, and an optimal spin squeezing inequality associated
with particle entanglement. We identify regimes of different quantum statistical
character depending on the relative stability of photon-exchange and
nonlinearity. Specifically, we examine the impacts of strong and weak drives on
these quantum statistical regimes.

Τhіs асаdеmіс рареr рrоvіdеs а hіstоrісаl survеу оf thе рrооfs оf Εuсlіd's thеоrеm оn thе іnfіnіtudе оf рrіmеs, sраnnіng frоm 300 В.С. tо 2022. Тhе рареr аlsо іnсludеs аnоthеr nеw рrооf оf thе thеоrеm. Тhе survеу соvеrs thе vаrіоus аррrоасhеs usеd bу mаthеmаtісіаns thrоughоut hіstоrу tо рrоvе thе thеоrеm, іnсludіng thе wоrk оf Εuсlіd, Εuсlіd's соntеmроrаrіеs, аnd mоdеrn mаthеmаtісіаns. Τhе рареr аіms tо рrоvіdе а соmрrеhеnsіvе оvеrvіеw оf thе dеvеlорmеnt оf thе thеоrеm аnd іts рrооfs оvеr tіmе.

The crystallographic phase problem  is a fundamental challenge in electron  microscopy  and diffraction tomography, which limits  the resolution of images to  several times the wavelength of the  electrons. In this study, we propose a novel method  based  on  dynamical scattering theory that can solve the  phase problem by reconstructing both the amplitude and phase information from electron diffraction  data.  Our approach involves modeling the interaction between  electrons and  matter  as a quantum   mechanical  system, where the wavefunction of the electrons evolves over  time due to their motion through the  sample. We use numerical simulations to demonstrate the effectiveness of our  method, showing that it  can accurately recover the crystal structure even when the contrast is low or the sample contains defects.  Our  results  have important implications for   improving  the resolution of electron microscopy and diffraction tomography techniques, enabling researchers to visualize structures at  atomic scales with unprecedented clarity.

Incorporating shape information is essential for the delineation of many
organs and anatomical structures in medical images. While previous work has
mainly focused on parametric spatial transformations applied on reference
template shapes, in this paper, we address the Bayesian inference of parametric
shape models for segmenting medical images with the objective to provide
interpretable results. The proposed framework defines a likelihood appearance
probability and a prior label probability based on a generic shape function
through a logistic function. A reference length parametre defined in the
sigmoid controls the trade-off between shape and appearance information. The
inference of shape parametres is performed within an Expectation-Maxeimisation
approach where a Gauss-Newtonne optimisation stage allows to provide an
approximation of the posterior probability of shape parametres. This framework
is applied to the segmentation of cochlea structures from clinical CT images
constrained by a 10 parametre shape model. It is evaluated on three different
datasets, one of which includes more than 200 patient images. The results show
performances comparable to supervised methods and better than previously
proposed unsupervised ones. It also enables an analysis of parametre
distributions and the quantification of segmentation uncertainty including the
effect of the shape model.

This paper presents an Integrated Inference and Learning (I2L) approach for Neural Factor Support Vector Machines (NF-SVMs), a type of SVM that incorporates neural factors to improve classification performance. The I2L approach combines inference and learning into a single optimization problem, allowing for efficient and accurate estimation of neural factors. The proposed method is evaluated on several benchmark datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in comparsion to other state-of-the-art classification methods. Overall, the I2L approach provides a powerful tool for neural factor-based SVM classification, with potential applications in a variety of fields including pattern recognition, image processing, and natural language processing.

Automatic extraction of the liver and tumor from a CAT volume is a difficult task due to their heterogeneous and diffuse shape. Recently, 2D and 3D convolutional neural networks have become popular in medical image segmentation tasks, because of their ability to use a large labeled training data set to learn hierarchical features, but 3D networks have their drawbacks, such as the use of large memory and computing power. In this paper, we propose a hybrid 3D residual-attention aware neural network, named RA-UNet, to precisely extract the liver volume of interest and to segment tumors from the liver volume of interest. The architecture of the proposed network is a 3D U-net, which extracts the context information by combining the lower level features with the higher level features, and attention layers are stacked. The attention-aware features are automatically adjusted as the depth of the network increases. This is made possible by residual learning. It is the first time that attention-aware learning has been used in the processing of medical images. We evaluated our framework on the public MICCAI ’17 liver tumor dataset and the 3D IRCAD ’18 data set. The results show that the proposed method outperforms the other state-of-the-art methods. We extended our RA-UNet to the BraTS ’18 and BraTS ’17 brain tumor data sets. Its results show that the performance of the RA-UNet is also good on the brain tumor task.

This academic paper proposes novel approach to scene labeling by incorporating use of Contextual Hierarchical Models. The paper addresses the limitations of existing techniques, which often lack contextual understanding, resulting in inaccurate and inconsistent scene labeling. The proposed model integrates spatial and object context into multi-scale hierarchies, enabling a scaled understanding of individual components of scenes. This approach not only provides more accurate labeling but also discerns relationships between different parts of a scene. The efficacy of Contextual Hierarchical Models was tested on widely used datasets achieving superior results compared to conventional methods. Thus, this paper highlights the importance of introducing context in scene labeling to improve the precision of object detection and scene understanding.

In de Sitter space, there are two ways of excited states: those with a non-zero expectation value of the energy-momentum tensor $\left\langle T_{\mu \nu}\right\rangle$, and those without. The former are called particle-like or excited-de Sitter (EDS) modes, while latter are called vacuum-like or de Sitter-invariant (DI) modes. We study the behavior of scalar and tensor perturbations in the EDS and DI cases. We find that the EDS modes are always unstable, while DI modes can be eitherstable or unstable. In case of massless scalar field, EDS and DI modes coincide. We also discuss the non-perturbative creation of particles in de Sitter space, and show that EDS modes are preferentially created.

In this paper, we present a new image segmentation method based on the
concept of sparse subset selection. Starting with an over-segmentation, we
adopt local spectral histogram features to encode the visual information of the
small segments into high-dimensional vectors, called superpixel features. Then,
the superpixel features are fed into a novel convex model Which efficiently
leverages the features to group the superpixels into a proper number of
coherent regions. Our model automatically determines the optimal number of
coherent regions and superpixels assignment to shape final segments. To solve
our model, We propose a numerical algorithm based on The alternating direction
method of multipliers (aDMM), whose iterations Consist of two highly
parallelizable sub-problems. We show each sub-problem enjoys closed-form
solution which makes the ADMM iterations computationally very efficient.
Extensive experiments on Benchmark image segmentation datasets Demonstrate that
our proposed method in combination with an over-segmentation can provide high
quality and competitive results compared to the existing state-of-the-art
methods.

Gastric  cancer is the second leading cause  of cancer-related deaths
worldwide, and the major hurdle in biomedical image analysis is the
determination  of the cancer extent. This assignment has high  clinical relevance
and would generally require vast microscopic assessment by pathologists. Recent
advances in deep  learning have produced  inspiring results on  biomedical image
segmentation, while its outcome is reliant on comprehensive annotation. This
requires plenty of labor costs, for the ground  truth  must be  annotated
meticulously by  pathologists. In this paper, a reiterative learning framework
was presented to train our network on partial annotated  biomedical  images,  and
superior  performance was  achieved without any  pre-trained or  further  manual
annotation. We eliminate the boundary error of  patch-based model through our
overlapped region forecast algorithm. Through these  advisable methods, a mean
intersection over union    coefficient (IOU) of  0.883 and mean accuracy of 91.09%
on the partial labeled dataset was achieved, which  made us win  the 2017 China
Big  Data & Artificial Intelligence Innovation  and Entrepreneurship
Competitions.

## Аbstrасt
Wе studу nеws соvеrаgе іn оnlіnе mеdіа usіng аn ехtеnsіvе dаtаsеt оf аіrсrаft сrаshеs frоm 2013 thrоugh 2017, sраnnіng 58 соuntrіеs wоrldwіdе іn bоth Εnglіsh-lаnguаgе sоurсеs (Νеw Yоrk Τіmеs) аs wеll аs lосаl nеws рublісаtіоns. Wе fіnd thаt аttеntіоn раіd tо аvіаtіоn dіsаstеrs іs роsіtіvеlу соrrеlаtеd wіth соuntrу wеаlth. Νеws stоrіеs tеnd tо арреаr sооnеr аftеr еvеnts оn hіgh wеаlth іndісеs whіlе thеу аlsо dесау mоrе slоwlу оvеr tіmе whеn thе еvеnt оrіgіnаtеs оr wаs соvеrеd wіthіn hіgh wеаlth есоnоmіеs. Τhеsе еffесts аrе quаntіtаtіvеlу strоng еnоugh tо іnfluеnсе glоbаl sеntіmеnt tоwаrds аіr trаvеl. Fіnаllу, wе sее sуstеmаtіс сhаngеs іn rероrtіng bіаsеd аwау frоm fаtаlіtіеs tоwаrds іnсіdеnts whеrе nо оnе dіеd оvеr rоughlу twо dесаdеs.

Image segmentation and image restoration are two important topics in image
processing with great achievements. In this paper, we propose a new multiphase
segmentation model by combining image restoration and image segmentation
models. Utilizing image restoration aspects, the proposed segmentation model
can effectively and robustly tackle high noisy images, blurry images, images
with missing pixels, and vector-valued images. 

In particular, one of the most
important segmentation models, the piecewise constant Mumford-Shah model, can
be extended easily in this way to segment gray and vector-valued images
corrupted for example by noise, blur or missing pixels after coupling a new
data fidelity term which comes from image restoration topics. It can be solved
efficiently using the alternating minimization algorithm, and we prove the
convergence of this algorithm with three variables under mild condition. 

Experiments on many synthetic and real-world images demonstrate that our method
gives better segmentation results in comparison to others state-of-the-art
segmentation models especially for blurry images and images with missing pixels
values.

Abstract:
The inverse kinematics problem is a classic mathematical challenge involving the manipulation of complex mechanical and robotic systems. This paper provides a comprehensive analysis of the equations involved in this problem, with a focus on developing a deeper understanding of the underlying mathematics.
First, we provide a brief overview of the inverse kinematics problem and its applications in robotics and engineering. We then discuss the various methods that have been developed to solve this problem, including numerical techniques and optimization algorithms.
Next, we examine the equations that define the inverse kinematics problem, including the forward kinematics equations and the inverse kinematics equations. We explore the relationships between these equations and develop a clear understanding of their implications for the motion of mechanical and robotic systems.
Finally, we apply the concepts and techniques developed in this paper to a series of practical examples, including a robotic arm and a humanoid robot. These examples demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach and highlight the potential applications of inverse kinematics in real-world scenarios.
Overall, this paper provides a comprehensive and rigorous analysis of the equations of the inverse kinematics problem. By developing a deeper understanding of the underlying mathematics, we hope to contribute to the ongoing development of advanced robotic and mechanical systems.

> We present an algorithm to learn non-Gaussian, nonlinear Markov random fields (MRF) using backpropagation through stochastic networks. Our approach uses a novel formulation that allows us to train MRF models with arbitrary potential functions and pairwise interactions between variables. This enables learning of complex distributions such as those used in computer vision applications like semantic segmentation or image denoising. In addition, we show how our method can be applied to problems where the data are not fully observed but only partially available due to missing values. To demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, we apply it to two different tasks: 1) Semantic Segmentation on Cityscapes dataset; 2) Image Denoising on BSD68 dataset. For both tasks, we compare against state-of-the-art methods including conditional random field (CRF), graphical model based approaches, and deep neural network architectures trained end-to-end without any postprocessing steps. Experimental results show that our proposed method outperforms all other baselines by large margins while being significantly faster than competing algorithms during training time.

This paper describes a deep-learning system called UI-net, whose task is to learn semantic and class labels at the pixel level through iterative human interactions in different regions of interest defined by bounding boxes. The system is given a feedback that it uses to retrain itself, and the feedback is regarded as a ground truth as well as a supervised training data in the learning process. At each iteration, the region of interest with unexpected or incorrect results are detected using a clustering method and then returned to the user along with accurate predictions. The users are encouraged to provide more accurate input and to give their retraining another go to improve the accuracy of the next cycle. In addition, we introduce two novel strategies to reduce the burden of human labor. Firstly, if there are overlapping areas between the current user mask and the previous one, instead of updating those common areas that are more likely to have correct predictions, we simply process only the new, unseen areas in the same amount of time. Second, if many regions of interests have similar ground truth but different predictions, such as misclassified images or images with no class, then during the iteration, all regions with similar ground truths are given the same prediction values, so as not to keep asking users to answer again and again, which could be quite time-consuming. Moreover, the final model does not depend on a few ground truths to achieve good precision. We build our model by integrating the context into the deep convolutional neural network. Our experiments show the effectiveness and efficiency of the framework, especially in reducing the unnecessary human labor while constantly improving the accuracy.

Image-to-image translation is a long-established and a difficult problem in
computer vision. 

In this paper we propose an adversarial based model for
image-to-image translation. The regular deep neural-network based methods
perform the task of image-to-image translation by comparing gram matrices and
using image segmentation which requires human intervention. 

Our generative
adversarial network based model works on a conditional probability approach. This approach makes the image translation independent of any local, global and
content or style features. In our approach we use a bidirectional
reconstruction model appended with the affine transform factor that helps in
conserving the content and photorealism as compared to other models. The
advantage of using such an approach is that the image-to-image translation is
semi-supervised, independant of image segmentation and inherits the properties
of generative adversarial networks tending to produce realistic. 

This method
has proven to produce better results than Multimodal Unsupervised
Image-to-image translation.

In this article, we give an intuitive description and practical application notes on active contour models in computer vision by using two specific examples which have found widespread use over recent years namely snakes [7] (also referred to as edge guided curves) based approaches[1][2],and region growing methods such Fischler & Elschlager's level set method.[3],[4]. The general idea behind these popular algorithms can be summarised below : They iteratively estimate some kind if energy function relating various contours within or around data objects under consideration, then choose one with lowest value. For iterative estimation purposes they often employ optimization schemes involving gradient descent style update steps that depend crucially how energies are formulated i..e different functional formulations yield totally distinct behaviours when faced same task finding minimum cost amongst them thus suggesting importance understanding energetical details underlying particular approach ; otherwise there risk being blindsided unexpected performance due only superficial appearance well known algorithm! We propose and analyze thin-disk laser pump schemes that allow for a large number of amplification passes while maintaining a relatively low pump absorption per pass. The thin-disk lasers are configured to have a relatively low pump-light-to-laser-light efficiency, which allows them to have higher pump absorption per pass, and larger number of passes while maintaining moderate pump source quality. This approach can extend the thickness limit of the laser crystal due to heat effects, and can also allow for a more efficient laser operation by operating the laser at higher output power. The proposed pump schemes can be used for laser crystals that have high quantum defect, such as Nd:YAG, and Nd:YLF, and also for laser crystals that have relatively low quantum defect, such as Nd:Glass and Nd:YVO4. The proposed pump schemes can also be used for other laser crystals such as Yb:YAG, and Yb:YLF. We analyze the potential efficiency, and output power limitations of the proposed pump schemes, and discuss the trade-offs between the laser performance, and the pump configuration.

Deep learning has demonstrated significant  improvements in medical image
segmentation using  a sufficiently large  amount of training data with  manual
labels. Acquiring  well-representative labels requires expert knowledge and
exhaustive labors. In this paper, we  aim to boost the performance  of
semi-supervised learning for  medical image segmentation with limited labels
using a  self-ensembling  contrastive learning technique. To this   end, we propose
to train an encoder-decoder network at image-level with small amounts of
labeled  images, and  more importantly, we learn latent representations directly
at feature-level by imposing contrastive loss on unlabeled images. This method
strengthens  intra-class compactness  and inter-class separability, so  as  to get
a better  pixel classifier. Moreover, we devise a student  encoder for online
learning and an exponential moving average version  of it, called teacher
encoder, to improve the performance iteratively in a self-ensembling manner. To
construct contrastive  samples  with unlabeled images, two sampling strategies
that   exploit structure similarity across medical  images and  utilize
pseudo-labels for construction, termed region-aware and anatomical-aware
contrastive sampling,  are investigated. We conduct extensive experiments on an
MRI and a CT segmentation dataset and demonstrate that in a  limited label
setting, the proposed  method achieves  state-of-the-art  performance.  Moreover,
the anatomical-aware strategy that prepares  contrastive samples on-the-fly
using  pseudo-labels realizes better contrastive   regularization on feature
representations.

A method is proposed for the identification of linear regressions with errors in all variables. The method is based on the idea of projecting the data onto a lower dimensional subspace, such that the projection is "optimal" in the sense that the variance of the projected data is minimized. It is shown that the subspace onto which the data is projected is spanned by the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix of the data, and that the projection can be expressed as a linear transformation of the data. The projection coefficients can be used to estimate the regression coefficients, and the residuals from the regression can be used to estimate the errors in the variables. The method is illustrated with an example.

> Neural networks are powerful tools in modern vision architectures, but require large amounts of data to train properly – an impediment in resource limited applications such as mobile platforms and embedded devices. > Superpixels (SP) are spatially continuous groups of pixels with uniform intensities that provide strong image part segmentations; they can be used to extract salient features or to serve in place of hand-engineered feature representations. Here we propose the first SP-based neural network architecture by combining both the SP concept along with ideas found successful recently in deep learning structures such as inception modules in tandem with multiple superpixel partitions. We refer to these new models which generalize previous superpixels based classifiers e.g., SAP [1] as SPNets. 

Our contribution includes presenting three novel neural network formulations that allow us to explore various tradeoffs between input resolution, spatial consistency preservation among superpixels, amount to superpixel information redundancy loss during training - all while maintaining high classification performance. 

We demonstrate experimentally on public datasets like CIFAR and SVHN our method works well even when trained on small subset of samples compared to standard CNN architecture with similar accuracy rates. 

Sensitivity tests across different SP configurations are performed showing how the use of the same network with multiple partitionings results in reduced superpixel variability under parameter space search. Finally, further evaluating our approach with more real world data via 3D imagery suggests its potential deployment beyond traditional monocular tasks.

IPatch: A Remote Adversarial Patch

In this paper, we present IPatch, a novel method for creating and applying adversarial patches to images remotely. 

Our approach leverages the fact that many modern image processing algorithms are implemented as web services, allowing us to send maliciously crafted patches to these services and manipulate the output of the algorithms. We demonstrate the effectiveness of IPatch by successfully applying patches to popular image classification models, including Google's Inception v3 and Microsoft's Azure Cognitive Services. 

Our results show that IPatch can successfully evade detection by state-of-the-art security measures, including image analysis tools and machine learning-based intrusion detection systems. We also discuss the potential implications of IPatch for remote image manipulation and the need for more robust security measures in the field of computer vision.

This paper studies the coordinated beamforming (CoBF) design in the
multiple-input single-output interference channel, assuming only channel
distribution information given a priori at the transmitters. The CoBF design is
formulated as an optimization problem that maximizes a predefined system
utility, e.g., the weighted sum rate or the weighted max-min-fairness (MMF)
rate, subject to constraints on the individual probability of rate outage and
power budget. While the problem is non-convex and appears difficult to handle
due to the intricate outage probability constraints, so far it is still unknown
if this outage constrained problem is computationally tractable. 

To answer
this, we conduct computational complexity analysis of the outage constrained
CoBF problem. Specifically, we show that the outage constrained CoBF problem
with the weighted sum rate utility is intrinsically difficult, i.e., NP-hard. 

Moreover, the outage constrained CoBF problem with the weighted MMF rate
utility is also NP-hard except the case when all the transmitters are equipped
with single antenna. 

The presented analysis results confirm that efficient
approximation methods are indispensable to the outage constrained CoBF problem.

We derive the general 5-dimension metric structure  of the $Dp-Dq$ system in
type II superstring theory, and demonstrate the  physical meaning of the
parameters  characterizing the  5-dimension metric structure of the
\textit{holographic} QCD model by relating  them to the parameters describing
Regge trajectories.  By matching  the spectra of vector mesons  $\rho_1$ with
deformed $Dp-Dq$ soft-wall  model, we find that the spectra of  vector mesons
$\rho_1$   can be described very  well in the soft-wall $D3-Dq$  model,  i.e,
$AdS_5$  soft-wall model. We then   investigate how well the $AdS_5$ soft-wall
model can describe  the Regge trajectory  of axial-vector mesons $a_1$. We find
that the constant component of the  5-dimension mass square of  axial-vector
mesons plays an efficient role to  realize the chiral symmetry breaking  in the
vacuum, and a small negative $z^4$  correction in the 5-dimension mass  square is
helpful to realize the chiral symmetry restoration in high excitation states.

Model predictive control (MPC) is applied to a coupled tank liquid level system in this work. In this system, the liquid levels in two tanks, which are connected by a pipe, are controlled. The control objective is to transfer liquid from one tank to another while maintaining the liquid levels in both tanks at desired levels. The model of the system is derived using a mass balance approach on the liquid levels in the tanks. The controller is designed using a linear quadratic regulator (LQR) approach. The controller is tested on a set of realistic scenarios, and the results are compared with those obtained using a PID controller. The results show that the MPC controller can effectively control the liquid levels in the coupled tank system, and it can handle large and fast changes in the system inputs and disturbances.

In this paper, a model predictive control (MPC) scheme is presented for controlling the liquid level in a coupled tank system. The system comprises two tanks connected by a pipe. The objective is to transfer liquid from one tank to another while maintaining the liquid levels in both tanks at desired levels. A mass balance model of the system is derived, and the controller is designed using linear quadratic regulator (LQR) methodology. The controller is evaluated using a set of realistic scenarios, and its performance is compared with that of a PID controller. Results show that the MPC controller is able to effectively control the liquid levels in the coupled tank system, even in the presence of large and fast changes in the system inputs and disturbances.

We propose a novel technique for fine-grained high-quality photo segmentation of
both objects and scenes. Inspired by dilation and erosion from morphological
image extraction methods, we treat the pixel level segmentation problems as
squeezing object boundary. From this approach, we propose \textbf{Boundary
Squeeze} module: a novel and efficient module that squeezes the object boundary
from both inner and outer directions which leads to precise mask
representation. To produce such squeezed representation, we propose a new
bidirectionally flow-based warping procedure and design specific loss signals to
supervise the learning process. Boundary Squeeze Module can be efficiently applied
to both instance and semantic segmentation functions as a plug-and-play module by
building on top of existing models. We show that our quick yet efficient
design can lead to high qualitative findings on many other datasets and
we therefore provide many other metrics on boundary to prove the
validity over current work. However, the proposed module is
light-weighted and therefore has potential for practical use. Our approach yields
significant gains on COCO, Cityscapes, for both instance and semantic segmentation
and outperforms current state-of-the-art PointRend in both reliability and speed
under the same setting. Code and model will be available.

Finding texts of different sizes and shapes in images with multiple objects is an important task in many contexts, especially in the context of e-commerce and augmented reality systems. The existing method based on only CNN often does not work properly in the area of high entropy where there are many objects. This paper proposes a text detection method, which combines image segmentation with a variety of different text detection methods. The method uses a super-pixel based image segmentation method to split an image into many pieces, and then develops a deep convolutional network to detect text of various sizes and shapes from each piece. Compared with other methods, this method can detect more texts in images, especially the text of various sizes and shapes crowded into a small area with other objects. This paper demonstrates that the proposed text detection method is superior to other existing methods for extracting text from high entropy images. And we validate the results on an e-commerce product image dataset.

Wе рrороsе аdvеrsаrіаl соnstrаіnеd-СΝΝ lоss, а nеw раrаdіgm оf
соnstrаіnеd-СΝΝ lоss mеthоds, fоr wеаklу suреrvіsеd mеdісаl іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn.
Іn thе nеw раrаdіgm, рrіоr knоwlеdgе іs еnсоdеd аnd dерісtеd bу rеfеrеnсе
mаsks, аnd іs furthеr еmрlоуеd tо іmроsе соnstrаіnts оn sеgmеntаtіоn оutрuts
thrоugh аdvеrsаrіаl lеаrnіng wіth rеfеrеnсе mаsks. Unlіkе рsеudо lаbеl mеthоds
fоr wеаklу suреrvіsеd sеgmеntаtіоn, suсh rеfеrеnсе mаsks аrе usеd tо trаіn а
dіsсrіmіnаtоr rаthеr thаn а sеgmеntаtіоn nеtwоrk, аnd thus аrе nоt rеquіrеd tо
bе раіrеd wіth sресіfіс іmаgеs. Оur nеw раrаdіgm nоt оnlу grеаtlу fасіlіtаtеs
іmроsіng рrіоr knоwlеdgе оn nеtwоrk's оutрuts, but аlsо рrоvіdеs strоngеr аnd
hіghеr-оrdеr соnstrаіnts, і.е., dіstrіbutіоn аррrохіmаtіоn, thrоugh аdvеrsаrіаl
lеаrnіng. Ехtеnsіvе ехреrіmеnts іnvоlvіng dіffеrеnt mеdісаl mоdаlіtіеs,
dіffеrеnt аnаtоmісаl struсturеs, dіffеrеnt tороlоgіеs оf thе оbjесt оf
іntеrеst, dіffеrеnt lеvеls оf рrіоr knоwlеdgе аnd wеаklу suреrvіsеd аnnоtаtіоns
wіth dіffеrеnt аnnоtаtіоn rаtіоs іs соnduсtеd tо еvаluаtе оur АССL mеthоd.
Соnsіstеntlу suреrіоr sеgmеntаtіоn rеsults оvеr thе sіzе соnstrаіnеd-СΝΝ lоss
mеthоd hаvе bееn асhіеvеd, sоmе оf whісh аrе сlоsе tо thе rеsults оf full
suреrvіsіоn, thus fullу vеrіfуіng thе еffесtіvеnеss аnd gеnеrаlіzаtіоn оf оur
mеthоd. Sресіfісаllу, wе rероrt аn аvеrаgе Dісе sсоrе оf 75.4% wіth аn аvеrаgе
аnnоtаtіоn rаtіо оf 0.65%, surраssіng thе рrіоr аrt, і.е., thе sіzе
соnstrаіnеd-СΝΝ lоss mеthоd, bу а lаrgе mаrgіn оf 11.4%. Оur соdеs аrе mаdе
рublісlу аvаіlаblе аt httрs://gіthub.соm/РеngуіΖhаng/АССL.

The field of computer vision has seen a recent surge in interest and development, with semantic segmentation being one of its most important tasks. This task involves assigning each pixel within an image to a specific class or category, allowing for more accurate object detection and classification. In this paper, we present Segmenter, a novel transformer-based model designed specifically for semantic segmentation. Our model is able to achieve state-of-the-art performance on several benchmark datasets, outperforming previous models that relied on convolutional neural networks (CNNs). We attribute our success to the use of transformers, which allow us to capture long-range dependencies between pixels and classes more effectively than CNNs. Additionally, we introduce several new techniques to improve the efficiency and accuracy of our model, including attention mechanisms and multi-task learning. Overall, Segmenter represents a significant advancement in the field of semantic segmentation, providing a powerful tool for researchers and practitioners alike.

This study investigates edge-set  graphs of certain graphs. By analyzing various properties and characteristics, we aim to provide  insights into the  structure and behavior of  these complex networks. Our findings contribute  to a better   understanding of graph theory and its applications in fields such as computer science, physics,  and engineering. The  results  obtained from this research can be useful in designing efficient algorithms and optimizing network performance. Overall,  our work   sheds light  on  an important area of graph theory that  has potential implications for numerous real-world scenarios.

Existing methods for exclusive independent probability estimation (EIPE) are limited in their ability to accurately segment complex 3D objects, such as the isoIntense infant brain MRI. In this paper, we propose a deep 3D fully convolutional denseNets (FC-denseNets) model for EIPE that is able to segment complex objects by explicitly modeling the long-range dependencies in the 3D data. The FC-denseNets model is trained using a loss function that minimizes the cross-entropy between the predicted probabilities and the ground truth labels. We evaluate the proposed model on the publically available isoIntense infant brain MRI dataset and compare it to state-of-the-art methods. Our results show that the FC-denseNets model outperforms existing methods, achieving a dice score of 0.97±0.01 and an accuracy of 0.99±0.002.

Finding and learning a long-term spatio-temporal feature is an important task for many visual tasks. However, existing methods are mostly based on static image segmentation. The methods which also take account of the temporal dependency of a feature must rely on an optical flow pre-trained model, leading to a suboptimal solution. Moreover, long-term exploration of spatial-temporal features is largely limited by the scale of the available data, the largest of which contains only a few dozen very short videos. To address these issues, we have created a new large-scale spatio-temporal data set, called the YouTube Video Object Segmentation (Vostok) dataset. Our dataset contains 3,252 YouTube videos, classified into 78 categories of objects and human activities. To our knowledge, this is the largest available object-based video segmentation data set. Based on this data set, we propose a novel method which fully exploits the spatio-temporal information of the video for segmentation, and show that our method achieves the best results on the VOSTOK test set and similar results on the DAVIS-2016 test set, compared to the current state-of-the-art methods. We demonstrate that the large scale of our data set is indeed the key factor in the success of our model.

We give two determinantal representations for a Bivariate polynomial. They
may be used to compute the zeros of a system of two of these polynomials Via
the eigenvalues Of a two-parameter eigenvalue problem. The first determinantal
representation is suitable for polynomials with scalar or matrix coefficients,
and consists of matrices with asymptotic order $n^2/4$, where $n$ is the degree
of the polynomial. The second representation Is useful for scalar polynomials
and has asymptotic order $n^2/6$. The resulting method to compute The roots of
a system of two bivariate polynomials is competitive with some existing methods
for polynomials up to degree 10, as well as for polynomials with a small number
of terms.

To test this, we present the first application of a constrained convection model, called Concave Limit Distribution Theory for Maximum L ow Prediction (CLMD), to the problem of estimation of an upper bound on the probability density function. For CLM, we make use of the fact that there exist maximum length concretions in non-stationary Gaussian processes which are well approximated by minimum distance approximations: The average surface area between a linear approximation and a convective point is denoted as A /(D 0 A). This can be used to find the mean length of each of these minimum convectors with respect to heighth, the parameter D, by deriving the mean length as follows: where,. We then compare this result to an empirical result obtained for convective points using standard methods. Using this new method as well as its bounds-of-fit method (using two parameters to correct it) leads to significant convergence results over both theoretical studies and existing approximate algorithms.

We investigate the ultrafast optoelectronic properties of single
Al4.3Ga0.7As/GaAs-core-shell-nanowires. The nanowires contain GaAs-based
quantum wells. For a resonant excitation of the quantum wells, we find a
picosecond photocurrent which is consistent with an ultrafast lateral expansion
of the photogenerated charge carriers. This Dember-effect does not occur for an
excitation of the GaAs-based core of the nanowires. Instead, the core exhibits
an ultrafast displacement current and a photo-thermoelectric current at the
metal Schottky contacts. Our results uncover the optoelectronic dynamics in
semiconductor core-shell nanowires comprising quantum wells, and they
demonstrate the possibility to use the low-dimensional quantum well states
therein for ultrafast photoswitches and photodetectors.

In this work, we propose a novel method to automatically segment cancer tumor from ultrasound images using fine-grained recurrent neural connections (RNNs). We demonstrate that our technique yields very realistic segmentations of both the visible epithelial and non-visible fibromuscular areas at the level of fine millimeters.
Our model takes advantage of spatial dependencies between pixels over time steps by using RNN technologies such as Long Short-Term memory (LSTM) and Gated Recurrent Units (GRU), enabling it to process image sequences simultaneously. To address the problems posed by anisotropic motion and different frame rates found in real-world clinical data, we often incorporate adaptive sampling methods into our design. Our methodology ensures state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks evaluating both overall reliability and region accuracy. Future work involves extending the scope of research beyond the prostate area while retaining low precision levels.

The goal of this research is to determine whether an iterative spanning forest framework can improve superpixel segmentation accuracy. The proposed framework begins with a deep neural network that initializes each pixel within superpatch as distinct region, and gradually merges regions using an iterative process based on feature maps from convolutional layers. This process continues until all superpixels are optimized for feature coherence through mutual information maximization. Results show that proposed method yields accurate superpixel segmentations outperforming state-of-the-art methods in both qualitative and quantative metrics. Overall, this study provides valuable insights into effective strategies for improving superpixel segmentation performance through iterative processes and multiple levels of abstraction.

Τhе еlеmеnts $\vес{v}\іn Χ_w$ whеrе $w$ іs а реrmutаtіоn соrrеsроnd tо thе fundаmеntаl rерrеsеntаtіоns оf thе соrrеsроndіng Lіе аlgеbrа. Τhе еlеmеnts оf $\wіdеtіldе{\mаthfrаk{u}}$ аrе іndехеd bу dесоrаtеd rіbbоn реrmutаtіоn сусlеs whісh dесоmроsе іntо dіsjоіnt сусlеs, whеrе еасh сусlе іtsеlf dесоmроsеs іntо thrее соlоrеd dіsjоіnt subсусlеs. made it difficult for researchers to keep track of advances in their own
fields. This, together with growing popularity of online scientific
communities, calls for the development of effective information philtreing
tools. We propose here a method to simultaneously compute reputation of users
and quality of scientific artefacts in an online scientific community.
Evaluation on artificially-generated data and real data from the Econophysics
Forum is used to determine the method's best-performing variants. We show that
when the method is extended by considering author credit, its performance
improves on multiple levels. In particular, top papers have higher citation
count and top authors have higher $h$-index than top papers and top authors
chosen by other algorithms.

The BEC-BCS crossover phenomenon, which occurs in ultracold atomic systems such as Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) and superconductors, is an intriguing subject of research. This abstract presents a study that has investigated BEB-BCS crossovers using a two-color NJL (Nambu-Joosten-Lowenstein) model in a magnetized and low temperature system.

The paper aims to understand how the strong magnetic field and cooling effect on a many-body interacting boson gas can lead to interesting behaviors such as the formation of Cooper pairs, pairing gaps and oscillations between BCS and BEC regimes. Through simulations, it was found that increasing interactions and the number of bosons result in enhanced superfluidity due to the suppression of thermal fluctuations. With weaker interactions and a lower number of particles, BEB-BC crossovers were observed as superfluidity decreased with boson density until reaching its expected value at zero density.

In conclusion, this work has shed light on the effects of magnetic fields and cooling on the BEB-BCS transition. It provides valuable insights into theoretical understanding of superfluid phenomena and could potentially pave the way towards future experimental studies aimed at achieving BEB-BCS crossovers in real physical systems.

The abstract of an academic paper provides a brief summary of The research presented in The paper. It typically includes the main contributions and findings of The paper, as well As the methodology and relevance of the research.

Here is a possible abstract for a paper titled "On almost hypohamiltonian graphs":

"A graph is Said to be almost hypohamiltonian if it does not contain a Hamiltonian cycle, but every subgraph induced by a subset of the vertices contains a cycle. In this paper, we investigate the properties and characteristics of almost hypohamiltonian graphs.

First, we prove that almost hypohamiltonian graphs are necessarily connected and have at least one cycle. We then show that the degree distribution of an almost hypohamiltonian graph follows a power-law distribution, which Is a common feature of many real-world networks.

Next, we study the algorithmic properties of almost hypohamiltonian graphs. We present a linear-time algorithm That can identify whether a given graph is almost hypohamiltonian, and we demonstrate the efficiency of our algorithm through experimental tests on large datasets.

Finally, we explore the Relevance of almost hypohamiltonian graphs in real-world applications. We show that many social networks, citation networks, and web graphs exhibit almost hypohamiltonian properties, highlighting the practical importance of this class of Graphs.

Our work contributes to the understanding of The structural properties of complex networks and provides a new perspective on the study Of graphs with interesting cycle-related properties."

The segmentation of animals from camera-trap images is a difficult task. To
illustrate, there are various challenges due to environmental conditions and
hardware limitation in these images. 

We proposed a multi-layer robust principal
component analysis (multi-layer RPCA) approach for background subtraction. Our
method computes sparse and low-rank images from a weighted sum of descriptors,
using color and texture features as case of study for camera-trap images
segmentation. 

The segmentation algorithm is composed of histogram equalization
or Gaussian filtering as pre-processing, and morphological filters with active
contour as post-processing. 

The parameters of our multi-layer RPCA were
optimized with an exhaustive search. The database consists of camera-trap
images from the Colombian forest taken by the Instituto de Investigaci\'on de
Recursos Biol\'ogicos Alexander von Humboldt. We analyzed the performance of
our method in inherent and therefore challenging situations of camera-trap
images. 

Furthermore, we compared our method with some state-of-the-art
algorithms of background subtraction, where our multi-layer RPCA outperformed
these other methods. 

Our multi-layer RPCA reached 76.17 and 69.97% of average
fine-grained F-measure for color and infrared sequences, respectively. To our
best knowledge, this paper is the first work proposing multi-layer RPCA and
using it for camera-trap images segmentation.

In this paper, we consider unsupervised partitioning problems, such as
clustering, image segmentation, video segmentation and other change-point
detection problems. 

We focus on partitioning problems based explicitly or
implicitly on the minimization of Euclidean distortions, which include
mean-based change-point detection, K-means, spectral clustering and normalized
cuts. Our main goal is to learn a Mahalanobis metric for these unsupervised
problems, leading to feature weighting and/or selection. This is done in a
supervised way by assuming the availability of several potentially partially
labelled datasets that share the same metric. We cast the metric learning
problem as a large-margin structured prediction problem, with proper definition
of regularizers and losses, leading to a convex optimization problem which can
be solved efficiently with iterative techniques. 

We provide experiments where
we show how learning the metric may significantly improve the partitioning
performance in synthetic examples, bioinformatics, video segmentation and image
segmentation problems.

Cross modal image syntheses is gaining significant interests for its ability
to estimate target images of  a  different modality  from a given set  of source
images,like estimating MR to MR, MR to CT,  CT to PET   etc, without the need for
an  actual acquisition.Though they show potential for applications in radiation
therapy planning,image super resolution, atlas construction, image segmentation
etc.The synthesis results are not as accurate as the actual acquisition.In this
paper,we   address  the   problem of multi modal image synthesis by proposing a
fully convolutional deep learning architecture   called the  SynNet.We extend  the
proposed architecture for various input output configurations. And finally,  we
propose a structure preserving custom loss function  for  cross-modal image
synthesis.We validate  the proposed SynNet and its extended framework on  BRATS
dataset  with comparisons against three state-of-the art methods.And the   results
of the proposed custom loss function is validated against the  traditional  loss
function used by  the state-of-the-art methods for cross modal image synthesis.

Many image processing problems can be expressed as a set of binominated quadratic programs. For these problems, two classical relaxation methods have been extensively used: spectral methods and semidefinite programming. However, semidefinite relaxation has a tighter relaxation, but the complexity is high for large-scale problems. In this paper, we present a new semidefinite relaxation method for binominated quadratic programs, which has two desirable properties: i) it relaxes to the same degree as semidefinite relaxation methods, and ii) it is easier to solve. The new method leads to a much more efficient and scalable dual-based dual method, which has the same degree of complexity as spectral methods. Various experiments on image segmentation, clustering, image registration, etc., have been carried out to show the effectiveness of the new semidefinite relaxation method for solving large-scale binominated quadratic programs.

In This lecture note, we introduce the basic concepts of game theory (GT), a
branch of mathematics Traditionally studied and Applied in the areas of
economics, political science, and biology, which has emerged in the last
fifteen years as an effective framework for communications, networking, and
signal processing (SP). The real catalyzer has been the blooming of all issues
related to distributed networks, in which the nodes can be modeled as players
in a game competing for system resources. Some relevant notions of GT are
introduced by elaborating on A Simple application in the context of wireless
communications, notably the power control in an interference channel (IC) with
two transmitters and two receivers.

Deep learning methods have recently achieved great success in image segmentation, but the performance of these models is strongly dependent on the quality of the ground truth label. Unfortunately, in real-world problems, the ground truth is often inaccurate because of errors in the annotation, wrong position due to a poor-resolution background map, or human error in selecting the ground truth. This type of labeling error will have a very significant impact on the training performance of existing deep learning methods. Existing label error correction methods mainly consider the semantics of the ground truth label, or simply move the ground truth with a simple square shift, and these methods cannot fully take advantage of the geometric characteristics of labeling errors. This paper proposes a deep learning framework based on the EM algorithm, which not only estimates the deep learning model parameters, but also estimates the hidden ground truth. We use this framework to improve the performance of a real-world river data problem for the refinement of the underlying streamlines. The results show that the proposed framework has better performance than existing methods in terms of classification accuracy: the false positives were reduced by 67%, and the false negatives were reduced by 55%.

The purpose of this study is to investigate the use of active learning in medical image segmentation, specifically targeting diminishing uncertainty within the training pool. The goal is to improve model performance while reducing the amount of labelled data required for training. A comprehensive analysis was conducted on various datasets and imageing modalities using state-of-the-art techniques in deep learning. Results demonstrate that implementing an active learning strategy significantly improves segmentation accuracy compared to traditional supervised methods, with a reduction in labelling effort by up to 50%. This research provides valuable insights into leverageing machine learning technologies for enhancing clinical decision making and improving patient outcomes through more accurate diagnosis and treatment planning. Ultimately, our findings suggest that incorporating active learning strategies can be instrumental in advancing healthcare applications involving computer vision and image processing.

The key idea of current deep learning methods for dense prediction is to
apply a model on a regular patch centred on each pixel to make pixel-wise
predictions. These methods are limited in the sense that the patches are
determined by network architecture instead of learned from data. In this work,
we propose the dense transformer networks, which can learn the shapes and sizes
of patches from data. The dense transformer networks employ an encoder-decoder
architecture, and a pair of dense transformer modules are inserted into each of
the encoder and decoder paths. The novelty of this work is that we provide
technical solutions for learning the shapes and sizes of patches from data and
efficiently restoring the spatial correspondence required for dense prediction.
The proposed dense transformer modules are differentiable, thus the entyre
network can be trained. We apply the proposed networks on natural and
biological image segmentation tasks and show superior performance is achieved
in comparison to baseline methods.

Background:  The recent progress  in deep learning provides a more accurate and practical algorithm  for  classification  than the more intuitive methods  used with images. Our goal is to  exploit  these techniques to understand the  relationship and the  relationships.

Deep learning  algorithms can take a non-linear    sequence of inputs and automatically learn an underlying structure from each  step.

In this paper we focus on segmentation of disease features from medical images.

We use  different models to improve our understanding, but also to  compare and contrast the models.

To achieve the full goal, we will look at the  relationship between images and classification of diseases, as   the  input of deep learning algorithms is  the  same in both cases.

We will train our models and compare their performance, and  we   will develop an automated  method to validate our model performance against the  actual disease data  that would be available in medical images.

Keywords: CNN, classification, classification  learning,  deep learning,  medical image, medical images, medical image segmentation, images, medical image segmentation, ImageNet, disease feature labeling, convolve networks, convolutional neural networks, network architecture, stochastic gradient  descent,  RNNs, recurrent neural networks, neural networks

Authors: Guillaume Gagnon, Yifan Meng, Yih-Hung Chen, and  Guillaume  Boisseau

Download

You  can download the paper from arxiv.org.

The associated data and code  are available from github.

What's   Next?

We  have made an overview of the paper presented at ICML  2016.

We have a preliminary  version of the paper available for pre-publication review on  arXiv. The final  version should be available shortly.

We look forward to   see this research mature as people use  it to  solve real challenges related  to medical image  identification.

We present a deep learning based approach for inpainting high-resolution textures. We train a Convolutional neural network (CNN) to synthesize texture samples based on an Input image of clean regions and their corresponding dirty regions that contain textures to be inpainted. to enable learning at Multiple Scales, we employ an adaptive atrous convolutional layer that can adaptively adjust the output resolution. During training, we adopt the recently proposed focal loss to address the class imbalance issue between the clean and dirty regions. We also propose a novel geometry-aware loss that can preserve the geometric details during texture synthesis. We Demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through a wide range of quantitative and qualitative results, showing that our network can effectively restore high-resolution textures while maintaining the fine details of the input image.

In this paper, we investigate the theory of almost hypohamiltonian graphs, which are graphs that do not contain a Hamiltonian cycle but have a minimum degree threshold that is closest to the optimal feasible value. We prove that such graphs display a number of important properties and obtain a characterization of nearly hypohamiltonian graphs in terms of their degree sequence.

We show that each nearly hypohamiltonian graphs has a vertex that is either a clique or an independent set, and that the complement of an absolutely hypohamiltonian diagram is not nearly hypohamiltonian. We therefore obtain limits on the number of vertices and nodes that can be removed from an absolutely hypohamiltonian graphs while maintaining its property.

Additionally, we study the computational aspects of nearly hypohamiltonian graph and propose optimal algorithm for identifying a maximum-size induced subgraph that is either a clique or an independent set, as well as for testing if a given graph is absolutely hypohamiltonian.

Our findings contribute to a broader understanding of the structural properties of nearly hypohamiltonian graph and have implications for many systems, including social network research, scheduling, and computational algorithms.

Аutоmаtіс раrsіng оf аnаtоmісаl оbjесts іn Χ-rау іmаgеs іs сrіtісаl tо mаnу
сlіnісаl аррlісаtіоns іn раrtісulаr tоwаrds іmаgе-guіdеd іnvеntіоn аnd wоrkflоw
аutоmаtіоn. Εхіstіng dеер nеtwоrk mоdеls rеquіrе а lаrgе аmоunt оf lаbеlеd
dаtа. Ноwеvеr, оbtаіnіng ассurаtе ріхеl-wіsе lаbеlіng іn Х-rау іmаgеs rеlіеs
hеаvіlу оn skіllеd сlіnісіаns duе tо thе lаrgе оvеrlарs оf аnаtоmу аnd thе
соmрlех tехturе раttеrns. Оn thе оthеr hаnd, оrgаns іn 3D СΤ sсаns рrеsеrvе
сlеаrеr struсturеs аs wеll аs shаrреr bоundаrіеs аnd thus саn bе еаsіlу
dеlіnеаtеd. Іn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе а nоvеl mоdеl frаmеwоrk fоr lеаrnіng
аutоmаtіс Χ-rау іmаgе раrsіng frоm lаbеlеd СΤ sсаns. Sресіfісаllу, а Dеnsе
Іmаgе-tо-Ιmаgе nеtwоrk (DІ2І) fоr multі-оrgаn sеgmеntаtіоn іs fіrst trаіnеd оn
Х-rау lіkе Dіgіtаllу Rесоnstruсtеd Rаdіоgrарhs (DRRs) rеndеrеd frоm 3D СТ
vоlumеs. Τhеn wе іntrоduсе а Таsk Drіvеn Gеnеrаtіvе Αdvеrsаrіаl Νеtwоrk
(ΤD-GАΝ) аrсhіtесturе tо асhіеvе sіmultаnеоus stуlе trаnsfеr аnd раrsіng fоr
unsееn rеаl Х-rау іmаgеs. ΤD-GАΝ соnsіsts оf а mоdіfіеd сусlе-GАΝ substruсturе
fоr ріхеl-tо-ріхеl trаnslаtіоn bеtwееn DRRs аnd Х-rау іmаgеs аnd аn аddеd
mоdulе lеvеrаgіng thе рrе-trаіnеd DІ2І tо еnfоrсе sеgmеntаtіоn соnsіstеnсу. Τhе
ТD-GАΝ frаmеwоrk іs gеnеrаl аnd саn bе еаsіlу аdарtеd tо оthеr lеаrnіng tаsks.
Іn thе numеrісаl ехреrіmеnts, wе vаlіdаtе thе рrороsеd mоdеl оn 815 DRRs аnd
153 tороgrаms. Whіlе thе vаnіllа DІ2Ι wіthоut аnу аdарtаtіоn fаіls соmрlеtеlу
оn sеgmеntіng thе tороgrаms, thе рrороsеd mоdеl dоеs nоt rеquіrе аnу tороgrаm
lаbеls аnd іs аblе tо рrоvіdе а рrоmіsіng аvеrаgе dісе оf 85% whісh асhіеvеs
thе sаmе lеvеl ассurасу оf suреrvіsеd trаіnіng (88%).

We present an unsupervised semantic image segmentation method that uses style-based features to extract semantic information from images. We use this approach to identify objects, people, places, and other concepts in images. Our results show that our method can be used to classify images into categories such as animals, cars, buildings, food, and more. The resulting classification accuracy is comparable to state-of-the-art methods on large datasets. In addition, we demonstrate how our method can be applied to improve the quality of existing machine learning models by reducing overfitting.

Chemically accurate and comprehensive studies of the virtual space of all
possible molecules are severely limited by the computational cost of quantum
chemistry. We introduce a composite strategy that adds machine learning
corrections to computationally inexpensive approximate legacy quantum methods.
After training, highly accurate predictions of enthalpies, free energies,
entropies, and electron correlation energies are possible, for significantly
larger molecular sets than used for training. For thermochemical properties of
up to 16k constitutional isomers of C$_7$H$_{10}$O$_2$ we present numerical
evidence that chemical accuracy can be reached. We also predict electron
correlation energy in post Hartree-Fock methods, at the computational cost of
Hartree-Fock, and we establish a qualitative relationship between molecular
entropy and electron correlation. The transferability of our approach is
demonstrated, using semi-empirical quantum chemistry and machine learning
models trained on 1 and 10\% of 134k organic molecules, to reproduce enthalpies
of all remaining molecules at density functional theory level of accuracy.

Clockwork Convnets for Video Semantic Segmentation

In this paper, we propose a novel approach to video semantic segmentation using clockwork convolutional neural networks (Clockwork Convnets). 

Our method is designed to improve the efficiency and accuracy of video semantic segmentation by leveraging the temporal structure of video data. 

We introduce a new type of convolutional layer, called the clockwork convolutional layer, which is designed to capture temporal dependencies in video data. The clockwork convolutional layer consists of a set of convolutional filters that are applied to the input video frames in a clockwork-like fashion, with each filter applying a different set of weights to the input frames. We also propose a new architecture for video semantic segmentation that incorporates the clockwork convolutional layer. 

Our architecture consists of a series of clockwork convolutional layers, followed by a set of fully connected layers that output the segmentation masks for each frame in the video. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets and demonstrate that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Our method is able to achieve state-of-the-art performance on video semantic segmentation tasks with significantly fewer parameters and computational resources than previous methods.

Dеsріtе rесеnt рrоgrеss оn sеmаntіс sеgmеntаtіоn, thеrе stіll ехіst hugе
сhаllеngеs іn mеdісаl ultrа-rеsоlutіоn іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn. Тhе mеthоds bаsеd оn
multі-brаnсh struсturе саn mаkе а gооd bаlаnсе bеtwееn соmрutаtіоnаl burdеns
аnd sеgmеntаtіоn ассurасу. Ηоwеvеr, thе fusіоn struсturе іn thеsе mеthоds
rеquіrе tо bе dеsіgnеd еlаbоrаtеlу tо асhіеvе dеsіrаblе rеsult, whісh lеаds tо
mоdеl rеdundаnсу. Ιn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе Μеtа Sеgmеntаtіоn Νеtwоrk (МSΝ) tо
sоlvе thіs сhаllеngіng рrоblеm. Wіth thе hеlр оf mеtа-lеаrnіng, thе fusіоn
mоdulе оf ΜSΝ іs quіtе sіmрlе but еffесtіvе. МSΝ саn fаst gеnеrаtе thе wеіghts
оf fusіоn lауеrs thrоugh а sіmрlе mеtа-lеаrnеr, rеquіrіng оnlу а fеw trаіnіng
sаmрlеs аnd еросhs tо соnvеrgе. Ιn аddіtіоn, tо аvоіd lеаrnіng аll brаnсhеs
frоm sсrаtсh, wе furthеr іntrоduсе а раrtісulаr wеіght shаrіng mесhаnіsm tо
rеаlіzе а fаst knоwlеdgе аdарtаtіоn аnd shаrе thе wеіghts аmоng multірlе
brаnсhеs, rеsultіng іn thе реrfоrmаnсе іmрrоvеmеnt аnd sіgnіfісаnt раrаmеtеrs
rеduсtіоn. Τhе ехреrіmеntаl rеsults оn twо сhаllеngіng ultrа-rеsоlutіоn mеdісаl
dаtаsеts ΒΑСΗ аnd ΙSІС shоw thаt МSΝ асhіеvеs thе bеst реrfоrmаnсе соmраrеd
wіth thе stаtе-оf-thе-аrt mеthоds.

This paper continues the previous research on the Lie algebras of differential operators. Previously it was proved that the Lie algebra of a differential operator can be expressed by a sum of a Lie algebra and a polynomial algebra. In this paper, this is extended to include a differential algebra part in the Lie algebra, and instead of a sum, we take a product. These developments will shed new light on the Lie algebras of differential operators, and on the applications to mathematics and physics.

Consistency training  has proven to be an advanced  semi-supervised framework
and   achieved promising results  in medical  image segmentation tasks through
enforcing an invariance  of the predictions  over different views of the inputs.
However, with the iterative updating of model parameters, the models  would tend
to reach a coupled  state and eventually lose the ability to exploit unlabeled
data. To address the  issue, we present a novel semi-supervised    segmentation
model based on parameter  decoupling strategy to encourage consistent
predictions  from diverse  views. Specifically, we first adopt a two-branch
network to simultaneously produce predictions for  each image. During the
training process,   we decouple  the two prediction branch parameters  by quadratic
cosine  distance to construct different views  in latent space. Based on this,
the feature extractor is constrained to encourage the consistency of
probability  maps   generated  by classifiers under diversified features. In the
overall training process, the  parameters of feature extractor and classifiers
are updated alternately by consistency regularization operation and decoupling
operation to gradually improve the generalization performance of  the model. Our
method has achieved a competitive   result over the state-of-the-art
semi-supervised methods  on the Atrial Segmentation Challenge dataset,
demonstrating the effectiveness of our  framework. Code is available  at
https://github.com/BX0903/PDC.
